Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Algeria predominantly use paper-based records. Recent analyses (2026) describe public hospitals, including those in rural/southern regions, as largely reliant on handwritten patient folders and notes, with no EHRs at facility level.LinkedIn analysis by health expert. Digital initiatives like DEM.DZ EMR and digital hospitals exist but are pilots (e.g., Annaba wilaya) or hospital-focused, not rolled out to rural clinics.ASJP digital transformation paper. A 2025 rural village study shows low utilization of local primary care due to lack of comprehensive services, with no mention of digital tools.PMC rural access study. DHIS2 listed in old forums but unconfirmed for Algeria; likely aggregate HMIS, not facility-level digital recording. National digital plans aim for zero-paper but rural facilities lag, indicating entirely paper-based at clinics with possible later digitization at higher levels.
Source URLs: [LinkedIn: Algeria Public Hospitals Paper Records](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/sengwiss_why-algeria-cant-afford-to-keep-its-public-activity-7415122216939548672-slja), [ASJP: Digital Transformation Health Algeria](https://asjp.cerist.dz/en/downArticle/311/10/1/277781), [PMC: Rural Algeria Healthcare Access](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12641105/)

Comments: No direct evidence found on digital data recording at rural primary school level for attendance, enrollment, or grades. National stats from Ministry of Education collected via ONS show aggregates but no methodology indicating school-level digital entry.ONS Education Stats UNESCO report mentions digital platforms for enrollment/results access but pilot digital schools and ICT initiatives focus on access/teaching, not core admin records.UNESCO Algeria Report Rural digital divide noted.Zenodo Rural Digital Divide World Bank notes ICT policy but vast rural areas lagging, implying paper-based collection standard with central aggregation. Evidence points to entirely paper-based at rural facilities, digitized later if at all. Score 1 per rules for broad paper use.
Source URLs: [ONS Algeria](https://www.ons.dz/IMG/pdf/Education2021_2022.pdf), [UNESCO Algeria Report](https://media.unesco.org/sites/default/files/webform/ed3002/ALGERIE_NC_report.pdf), [World Bank ICT Education Algeria](https://openknowledge.worldbank.org/bitstreams/4ad4eeee-f1f1-572c-b985-7a00f0e7ebf7/download), [Zenodo Rural Study](https://zenodo.org/records/18734679)

Comments: Rural police stations in Algeria, primarily Gendarmerie Nationale brigades, record complaints, case files, and incident reports on paper via handwritten procès-verbaux (official records). Official procedure requires victims to present orally at stations where facts are consigned into a PV, then transmitted to prosecutors; digital pre-complaints online require physical signature of PV at the station within 30 days. No evidence of digital recording at rural facilities; later digitization efforts focus on urban justice, traffic fines, or online submissions, not initial rural case logging. Consistent with francophone African norms of paper-based PVs at police/gendarmerie outposts. US State Dept reports detail procedures without mentioning digital tools at station level.
Source URLs: [Ministry of Justice - La plainte](https://www.mjustice.gov.dz/fr/la-plainte/), [Gendarmerie 2017 meeting PV](http://www.ena.dz/pdf/Gendarmerie%20N%20261217.pdf), [US State Dept 2023 HR Report](https://www.state.gov/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/528267_ALGERIA-2023-HUMAN-RIGHTS-REPORT.pdf)

Comments: Algeria's civil registration (état civil) is managed at commune level, including rural areas, with births declared at the commune of birth. Official Ministry of Interior describes procedures requiring physical presence and documents like livret de famille, suggesting paper-based initial recording by local officers, consistent with traditional registry practices.Ministry of Interior. However, national centralization is advanced: civil registries interconnected since ~2014 allowing extracts from any commune; online 12S secure birth certificate requests via demande12s.interieur.gov.dz; digital birth extracts (نسخة رقمية لشهادة الميلاد) via etatcivil.interieur.gov.dz; biometric integration for passports/ID. US Embassy notes non-acceptance of handwritten certificates, implying printed/digital standard. No direct rural-specific evidence, but central digitization indicates paper at rural points digitized centrally. ECA CRVS reports exclude Algeria but note Africa's rural services often inaccessible/poor quality. Predominantly paper locally with national digital systems/pilots.
Source URLs: [Ministry of Interior Etat Civil](https://interieur.gov.dz/category/etat-civil/), [Algerian Embassy 12S](https://embwashington.mfa.gov.dz/consular-services/12s-birth-certificate), [ECOI Canada Report](https://www.ecoi.net/en/document/1163828.html), [US Embassy Algeria](https://dz.usembassy.gov/checklist-for-reporting-birth-in-algeria-to-u-s-citizen-parents/)"

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Angola predominantly use paper-based registers for patient data recording (e.g., consultations, vaccinations, treatments). Data is aggregated monthly and transcribed into the national DHIS2 system at municipal or provincial levels, not entered digitally at point-of-care.Serbian Journal of the Medical Chamber No evidence found of routine EMR or digital tools at rural clinics; pilots like TB EMR are urban/Luanda-based.Frontiers in Public Health Digital efforts include Excel training for reports in select areas (e.g., 52 Huambo units) and national DHIS2 since 2015 for aggregation, with NHIS strategic plan 2024-2030 underway.WHO Angola Annual Report 2024 Key barriers: inadequate rural infrastructure (electricity, internet), staff shortages/training gaps, uneven personnel distribution, post-civil war legacies. National ambition for digital HIS outpaces rural PoC reality, where paper remains default with digital limited to upward reporting/pilots (score 2).
Source URLs: [Serbian Journal of the Medical Chamber](https://smj.rs/en/volume-6-no-2/the-role-of-health-information-systems-in-transforming-and-enhancing-the-efficiency-of-angolas-health-system-challenges-and-opportunities); [Frontiers in Public Health](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9009439/); [WHO Angola Annual Report 2024](https://www.afro.who.int/sites/default/files/2025-03/WHO%20Angola%20Annual%20Report%202024.pdf)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Angola predominantly use paper-based recording for attendance, enrollment, grades, and admin data, aggregated later into central EMIS/SIGE systems at provincial/district levels via paper forms or manual entry. Evidence: 2007 World Bank report describes initial school mapping exercise as first step to EMIS, implying manual collection (World Bank ICT Report). Recent GPE analyses highlight 'data and evidence' as high priority due to lack of timely data, indicating incomplete digitisation at facility level (GPE Angola Compact). No evidence of direct digital entry in rural schools; infrastructure poor—no electricity policies for schools, low ICT access (Education Profiles). SIGE systems exist but provincial/pilot scale, not rural-wide digital recording. Pilots and donor projects (e.g., Escola Meu Kamba) exist but limited.
Source URLs: [World Bank ICT in Education Angola](https://openknowledge.worldbank.org/bitstreams/dc9e8776-b7b1-5c30-a70d-4f999387d815/download), [GPE Partnership Compact Angola](https://untalent.org/jobs/partnership-compact-for-the-implementation-of-the-global-partnership-for-education-gpe-initiative-in-angola-from-16-09-30-12-45-days-nationals-and-internationals-can-apply-portuguese), [Education Profiles Angola Technology](https://education-profiles.org/sub-saharan-africa/angola/~technology)

Comments: No evidence found of digital case management or record-keeping in rural Angola police stations despite extensive searches. SIC offers online complaints, but core functions like incident reports and case files appear paper-based per standard African practices (e.g., signing physical documents warned in UK FCDO Prisoner Pack). US State Dept 2024 HR report details arrests, interrogations at stations, pretrial detention without digital mentions, indicating manual processes (US State Dept 2024 HR Angola). Justice digitization limited to ombudsman courts, not police (UNDP Angola). Rural areas likely paper-only given infrastructure challenges.
Source URLs: [US State Dept 2024 HR Angola](https://www.state.gov/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/62451_ANGOLA-2024-HUMAN-RIGHTS-REPORT.pdf), [UK FCDO Angola Prisoner Pack](https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/media/61e8c4e68fa8f5058d5a772c/Angola_-_Prisoner_Pack_Luanda_2022.pdf), [UNDP Digital Justice Angola](https://www.undp.org/angola/blog/digital-justice-foundation-inclusive-development)

Comments: Rural registry offices in Angola predominantly use paper-based processes for birth registration, with forms filled manually at point of service and later digitized at higher levels. A 2023 UNECA survey self-reports Angola among 10 countries where all civil registration offices countrywide have digitized databases and electronic entry of vital events, with electronic transmission from local to central levels.UNECA CRVS Digitalization Report However, an African Union CRVS status report scores Angola's digitization as <35% (very weak), indicating limited electronic capture at local offices.AU CRVS Status Report UNICEF is procuring a CRVS assessment (2025), signaling ongoing gaps. Recent initiatives like 'Born with a birth certificate' target rural areas with ID issuance at hospitals (pilots in Zaire province), but routine rural registration remains paper-default. Barriers: poor rural infrastructure, electricity/internet gaps, low capacity; national digital ambitions (e.g., integrated platforms) outpace rural rollout, with coverage ~30% for IDs. Score 2 reflects self-claimed national digital but weak evidence for majority rural point-of-service use vs. aggregation-level digitization.
Source URLs: [UNECA CRVS Digitalization Report](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/Digitalization%20of%20Civil%20Registration%20and%20Vital%20Statistics%20in%20Africa.pdf); [AU CRVS Status Report](https://au.int/sites/default/files/newsevents/workingdocuments/33070-wd-en_-_report_on_the_status_of_civil_registration_and_vital_statistics_in_africa_25sept.pdf); [UNICEF Angola CRVS Tender](https://360mozambique.com/tenders/angola-evaluation-of-the-civil-registration-and-vital-statistics-system-crvs/)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Benin predominantly use paper-based recording for patient data. Evidence from multiple sources indicates reliance on paper registers: Abt Global's PSHPA project distributed 4,000 health data registers to private facilities across all regions, implying manual entry before aggregation into DHIS2; a 2024 study in rural Bopa district references annual statistical reports and monthly data from health centers without digital mention; Enabel equipped 244 facilities (unspecified if rural) with IT for data collection, but not point-of-care patient records. National DHIS2 is used for aggregate reporting from district level, with high concordance to facility registers in some areas, suggesting paper at clinics transcribed upward. Pilots exist (e.g., some direct DHIS2 entry in one clinic, District.Team for management), but no evidence of majority rural point-of-service digital use. Barriers: poor infrastructure (power, internet), low ICT skills, funding gaps. National eHealth strategy (2017) aims for digital HIS, but rural reality lags, with paper default and digital limited to aggregation/pilots. Score 1: entirely paper-based at rural clinics.
Source URLs: [Abt Global PSHPA](https://www.abtglobal.com/insights/impact-briefs/pshpa-trains-private-health-facilities-to-submit-rmnch-data-for-dhis2); [Pan African Medical Journal Bopa study](https://www.panafrican-med-journal.com/content/article/49/46/full/); [Enabel eHealth PDF](https://www.enabel.be/app/uploads/2025/07/PP06_E-Health_Integrating_Digital_Health_Systems-1.pdf)"

Comments: Rural primary schools in Benin predominantly use paper-based systems for recording attendance (call registers), administrative data, and monitoring, as evidenced by NGO projects like AGIR in Atacora/Atlantic using paper absence tracking sheets filled weekly from registers Action Education. National EMIS exists, with INFRE producing statistical yearbooks from EMIS data focused on primary education UNESCO IICBA, but no evidence of digital entry at rural school level—likely paper collection aggregated upward. Electricity access in primary schools is only 35% (2021, up from 20% in 2016), below SSA average, with rural national access at 12% IMF. Most primary schools lack computers, especially public/rural ones, leading to low ICT literacy Southern Voice. Isolated pilots (e.g., mobile money fees in one rural secondary) do not indicate widespread PoS digitalisation. Key barriers: infrastructure deficits, poverty, low skills. National policies promote free primary education and ICT integration ambitions, but rural PoS remains paper-based, with digital at higher aggregation levels only.
Source URLs: [UNESCO IICBA Benin Brief](https://www.iicba.unesco.org/en/benin); [Southern Voice](https://southernvoice.org/benin-digital-technologies-and-educational-inequality/); [Action Education](https://action-education.org/en/au-benin-innovative-and-adapted-school-monitoring-tools-for-school-pupils/); [IMF Report](https://www.elibrary.imf.org/view/journals/002/2024/339/article-A004-en.xml)"

Comments: No evidence found of digital case recording at rural police stations in Benin. As a francophone African country, rural Republican Police (merger of former police/gendarmerie in 2018) produce standard handwritten "procès-verbaux" (PVs) for complaints/incidents by judicial police officers, as per justice ministry site charging 300 FCFA per page for paper copies. No rural-specific digital case management, pilots, or IT systems identified despite searches on national police site, INTERPOL WAPIS (regional, not station-level), justice digitalisation (ID/civil, not police), and donor trainings (ICITAP capacity building, no systems). US State Dept/UK guidance describe procedures implying paper (statements, reports to prosecutor). Directorate of Information Systems exists centrally but no rural deployment evidence. Consistent with paper-based norm for rural facilities; data likely aggregated later at higher levels.
Source URLs: [Ministère de la Justice](https://justice.gouv.bj/service/21/copie-enquete-preliminaire/), [Republican Police](https://www.dgpr.bj/), [Force Publique Benin Police](https://www.force-publique.net/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/2023-Benin-gb.pdf)"

Comments: Rural registry offices in Benin are transitioning from paper-based to hybrid systems. Traditional practice involves paper declaration vouchers completed at health centres or via village chiefs, entered into paper registers at municipal civil status offices. However, significant digitalisation is underway: the e-SIRCEV electronic civil registration system was deployed to 121 out of 164 registries by 2021. In October 2024, Benin received 2,050 mobile registration terminals from the World Bank/WURI project to enable digital birth registration directly into the National Register of Natural Persons (RNPP) and National Register of Civil Status Events (RNEC) at health facilities across all districts. In August 2025, ANIP launched a one-stop digital birth registration pilot in 10 health facilities including regional hospitals in Natitingou and Djougou, with data going directly into the RNPP and automatic PIN generation. By 2025, ANIP issued 10.5 million civil status and identity documents, with 75% of all identification procedures handled through digital channels (37% web portal, 30% mobile app, 8% USSD). However, ANIP itself acknowledges that rural areas remain underserved and are a priority for 2026. The initial point-of-service capture at rural registry offices still largely involves paper forms before digital entry, though the infrastructure for digital-first registration is rapidly expanding. UNECA rates Benin's CRVS digitisation below 35%.
Source URLs: [BiometricUpdate WURI Terminals](https://www.biometricupdate.com/202410/benin-receives-2050-biometric-terminals-from-world-bank-to-boost-civil-registration); [WeAreTech ANIP 2025](https://www.wearetech.africa/en/fils-uk/news/tech/benin-advances-e-government-with-mass-digital-id-adoption); [EMURGO e-SIRCEV](https://www.emurgo.africa/blog/posts/digital-id-initiatives-by-country-in-africa)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Botswana predominantly use paper-based registers for patient data recording at point of service, such as case notification forms for malaria and general clinical notes. Digital systems like DHIS2 are deployed nationally for aggregate reporting and specific programs (e.g., malaria surveillance, HIV), but data is typically collected on paper at facilities and entered later at district level, not routinely at point of care in rural clinics. Limited EMR pilots exist in some clinics (e.g., IPMS rollout to 16 clinics pre-2010), but no evidence of majority adoption in rural settings; VisualDx CDSS faced infrastructure hurdles in public facilities. Key barriers include poor rural internet connectivity, unreliable power, limited devices/training, staff shortages, and system fragmentation (18+ parallel systems). National ambitions (eHealth Strategy 2020-24, DPI push) focus on integration and connectivity (223 clinics connected by 2025), but rural reality lags with paper default and digital for upward reporting only.
Source URLs:
[DHIS2 Malaria Study](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8965668/); [HIS Development Botswana](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4576446/); [WHO e-Health Botswana](https://cdn.who.int/media/docs/default-source/digital-health-documents/global-observatory-on-digital-health/unpan028383.pdf?sfvrsn=7375c633_3)"
</xai:function_call()>

<parameter name="user_description">Finalized findings and submitted result for Botswana rural clinics

Comments: Rural primary schools in Botswana predominantly use paper-based recording for administrative data, attendance, and learning outcomes, as evidenced by historical reliance on paper registers that are prone to loss or damage Craft Cart case study. Annual school censuses for EMIS are conducted via questionnaires sent to schools, implying paper forms filled at the facility and aggregated centrally, not real-time digital entry at point of service Statistics Botswana Primary Stats Brief 2018, Secondary Stats Brief 2022. Digital pilots exist, such as tablet-based offline attendance tracking with SMS notifications in one remote rural primary school (200km from town), improving accuracy from 65% to 99%, but this is isolated Craft Cart. Recent tenders for EMIS/LMS implementation (2025) and announcements for digital platforms/digital classroom trackers indicate ongoing central efforts, but no evidence of majority adoption at rural point-of-service. Key barriers: poor connectivity (intermittent signal, no fixed internet in remote areas), lack of infrastructure/devices, and teacher training gaps. National EMIS ambition contrasts with rural paper reality, where data is transcribed upward rather than captured digitally on-site. (198 words)
Source URLs: [Statistics Botswana Primary Stats Brief 2018](https://www.statsbots.org.bw/sites/default/files/Primary%20School%20Stats%20Brief%202018.pdf); [Statistics Botswana Secondary Stats Brief 2022](https://www.statsbots.org.bw/sites/default/files/Secondary%20Schools%20Stats%20Brief%202022.pdf); [Craft Cart rural school case study](https://www.craftcart.app/insights/case-study-rural-school-in-botswana-achieves-99-attendance-tracking)"

Comments: Rural police stations in Botswana predominantly use paper-based recording for case files, complaints, and incident reports, as evidenced by standard procedures requiring physical presence at any police station for fingerprints on paper forms and incident reports prepared by officers. Occurrence Books (OB) are standard paper tools for logging crimes at stations, common in anglophone Africa. No evidence found of digital recording at rural level; aspirations for digital reporting noted in 2025 presidential speech to reduce paperwork (60% of time), but currently paper dominates. Central systems like criminal records exist but rely on paper inputs from stations. Older reports (2011) note hard copy records in prisons; courts have some e-records but police procedures remain manual, especially rural.
Source URLs: [Gov.bw Police Incident Report](https://www.gov.bw/policing/police-incident-report), [US State Dept 2011 HR Report](https://2009-2017.state.gov/documents/organization/186379.pdf), [Daily News on police tech integration](https://dailynews.gov.bw/news-detail/86181)

Comments: Rural registry offices in Botswana, part of 12 districts and 24 sub-districts, are linked to a national online real-time computerized Births and Deaths Registration System (BDRS) since 2004, enabling electronic entry at point of service.StatsBots CRVS Report.pdf) Electronic on-site registration exists at 13+ health facilities, with mobile pilots in remote rural areas like Okavango.World Bank Botswana CRVS Birth registration coverage is high nationally (~90-92%), but lower in rural/remote areas due to access issues.StatsBots Report.pdf) Digital systems standard, but some paper remains for printing certificates or backups. Key barriers: poor network/electrification in remote villages, terrain, resources for mobile units, parental awareness. National ambition for universal digital CRVS exceeds rural reality due to infrastructure gaps, yet majority of rural facilities use digital point-of-service via linked system (score 3).UNECA CRVS Digitalization
Source URLs: [StatsBots CRVS Report](https://www.statsbots.org.bw/sites/default/files/documents/REPORT%20CIVIL%20AND%20NATIONAL%20REGISTRATION%20(BSDS).pdf); [World Bank Botswana CRVS Success](https://openknowledge.worldbank.org/bitstreams/bf9c94cb-b0ba-55ef-9e4f-b560e8edeff3/download); [UNECA Africa CRVS Digitalization](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/Digitalization%20of%20Civil%20Registration%20and%20Vital%20Statistics%20in%20Africa.pdf)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics (CSPS) in Burkina Faso predominantly use paper-based recording for patient data and service volumes. Health workers summarize indicators on paper forms monthly, submitted to districts for entry into national DHIS2/Endos system; digital entry occurs at district level, not point-of-care.BMJ Global Health study. Digital pilots exist, e.g., REC-Maternity/IeDA tablet app for maternal/child consultations, deployed in >1,600 CSPS across select districts (556 by 2022), recording millions of visits but running parallel to paper without integration, causing dual burden and discrepancies.BMJ Global Health study; MoH site. No evidence of majority rural CSPS using digital routinely; EMRs limited to pilots/incomplete, absent at primary level.BMC study. Barriers: poor integration, infrastructure (power/connectivity), training, fragmentation (35+ DHIs, 66% pilots). National ambition (e.g., e-sante strategy) outpaces rural reality of paper default.
Source URLs: [BMJ Global Health (2026)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12820863/), [BMC Med Inform Decis Mak (2024)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11186100/), [Burkina Faso MoH (2020)](https://www.sante.gov.bf/detail?tx_news_pi1%5Baction%5D=detail&tx_news_pi1%5Bcontroller%5D=News&tx_news_pi1%5Bnews%5D=152&cHash=590986c0c346b9a74a4b410e671fdd56)

Comments: Burkina Faso operates a national Education Management Information System (SIGE/EMIS) for education statistics, rated acceptable (3/4) by ECOWAS standards, based on annual school census data ADEA report. School census questionnaires feed into EMIS UNESCO IIEP. No evidence found of digital tools for direct data entry in rural primary schools for attendance, enrollment, or grades. Analogous rural health facilities use paper forms later digitized into DHIS2, with parallel digital pilots not replacing paper BMJ study. Limited pilots like FIUE-BF student database exist but not widespread in rural areas Gov site. Rural schools predominantly paper-based, data aggregated/digitized at district level: score 2.
Source URLs: [ADEA EMIS Review](https://knowledgehub.adeanet.org/en/elibrary/adea2019d007), [UNESCO IIEP](https://www.iiep.unesco.org/en/articles/protecting-education-attack-eie-data-burkina-faso), [BMJ Global Health](https://gh.bmj.com/content/10/1/e019312), [Burkina Education Ministry FIUE-BF](https://www.education.gov.bf/informations/actualites/articles?tx_news_pi1%5Baction%5D=detail&tx_news_pi1%5Bcontroller%5D=News&tx_news_pi1%5Bnews%5D=734&cHash=d3f899b1e2e6c51eac35e679a8610041)

Comments: Rural police stations (police/gendarmerie brigades) in Burkina Faso record case files, complaints, and incident reports using handwritten procès-verbaux (PVs), as mandated by the Criminal Procedure Code (Loi n°040-2019/AN). Officers draft originals and certified copies, signed per page, and transmit physically to prosecutors—no digital specification at station level. Official police site describes in-person filing at stations open 7/7, triggering procedures without digital mention. Digitisation limited to urban courts (e.g., e-criminal records in Ouagadougou/Bobo/Tenkodogo via UNDP) and recent online filing platforms (justice-penale.gov.bf, 2025)—not initial rural recording. Francophone norms and lack of rural digital evidence confirm paper-based. No pilots or aggregate entry found for rural police. Criminal Procedure Code, Police.gov.bf, UNDP RoLHR.
Source URLs: [Criminal Procedure Code](https://academiedepolice.bf/index.php/telechargement/category/38-codes?download=166:la-loi-portant-code-de-procedure-penale), [Police National Site](https://police.gov.bf/index.php/a-votre-service/plainte-et-denonciation), [UNDP Rule of Law Report](https://rolhr.undp.org/annualreport/2023/africa/burkina-faso.html)"

Comments: Rural registry offices in Burkina Faso predominantly use paper-based systems for birth registration, requiring manual transcription into duplicate registers, with copies sent to courts annually. Parents must travel long distances (up to 600km, 3-4 days) to these offices, contributing to low timely registration rates (74% rural vs 93% urban). The iCivil digital system, using smartphone apps and bubble-code wristbands, enables digital notification and registration primarily at ~130/2584 points (~5%), mostly health facilities (104) and some community sites (16), including rural areas, sending data via SMS to a national digital register. Parents can then retrieve certificates at any office. However, legal framework still recognizes only paper registers; digital revision pending. No evidence majority of rural registries (~80% population rural) use digital at point-of-service; iCivil is donor-supported expansion/pilot, not standard. Barriers: poor infrastructure, illiteracy, insecurity, low coverage. National ambition for full digital CRVS exists, but rural reality lags with paper default and digital in isolated projects. CRVS Systems; African Union; UNFPA
Source URLs: [CRVS Systems technical brief](https://crvssystems.ca/sites/default/files/assets/images/CRVS_Webinar_BurtkinaFaso_e_WEB.pdf); [African Union iCivil modernization](https://au.int/sites/default/files/newsevents/workingdocuments/37474-wd-crvs-v-modernization_of_the_civil_registration_and_vital_statistics_system.pdf); [UNFPA iCivil](https://www.unfpa.org/updates/ensuring-human-rights-burkina-faso-one-baby-time)

Comments: Burundi has implemented DHIS2 nationally up to CDS (district) level for health data aggregation and analysis, enabling decision-making and improved outcomes DHIS2 Implementation History. Prior to digital projects in hospitals (2015+), systems were largely paper-based, including patient records and registers Hospital Information System Study. EMRs like OpenClinic GA piloted in 11 hospitals (mostly district level), but no evidence of widespread individual digital recording in rural primary clinics (health centers level 1-3). Rural clinics likely use paper registers/patient records, with aggregates compiled and entered into DHIS2 at district level, consistent with common African HMIS where facility-level is paper and higher levels digitize. No SARA/WHO assessment found confirming digital tools in rural facilities; digital pilots focus on reminders/mobile for specific programs, not core records.
Source URLs: [DHIS2 Burundi Implementation](https://dac2023.dryfta.com/posters/abstract/public/41/dhis2-implementation-history-to-improve-health-interventions-and-outcomes-in-burundi), [Pre-digital HIS Paper-based](https://www.hrpub.org/download/20210430/UJPH8-17622812.pdf), [US PEPFAR COP on DHIS2](https://www.state.gov/wp-content/uploads/2020/07/COP-2020-Burundi-SDS-FINAL.pdf)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Burundi predominantly use paper-based registers for recording administrative data, attendance, and learning outcomes. Data is collected manually at the school level and aggregated into a national Education Management Information System (EMIS), which includes annual statistical yearbooks published by the Ministry of Education (latest 2020-2021) UNESCO IICBA. EMIS has been operational since 2008 but faces data management challenges, with no evidence of routine digital entry at rural point-of-service. Digital systems are limited to isolated donor-funded pilots, such as Ideas Box offline digital libraries in 20 remote schools (2023, EU-funded) Libraries Without Borders and proposed ICT labs in 15 provinces (2022 NGO project). Key barriers include lack of electricity (only 15% rural access, 10% schools powered), teacher training gaps, poverty, and infrastructure deficits. National policies promote ICT integration since 2014, but rural reality lags, with data likely transcribed centrally rather than captured digitally on-site.
Source URLs: [UNESCO IICBA Country Brief](https://www.iicba.unesco.org/en/burundi); [Libraries Without Borders Report](https://www.librarieswithoutborders.org/2024/03/28/the-digital-tools-at-the-heart-of-burundian-education/); [EPDC Burundi Policy Note](https://www.epdc.org/sites/default/files/documents/EPDC_NEP_2018_Burundi.pdf)

Comments: Burundi's rural police stations record case files, complaints, and incident reports using handwritten paper procès-verbaux (PVs), as mandated by the Criminal Procedure Code (Articles 3-5, 61). PVs require physical signatures, page initials, oaths, and manual transmission to prosecutors; no digital methods mentioned. HRW 2010 report confirms rural OPJs rely on paper dossiers, lacking even basic paper supplies (donated by NGOs), vehicles, or comms; files physically transferred, often incomplete. Recent US State Dept HR reports (2023-2024) describe manual processes (e.g., magistrates failing to convene hearings due to backlogs, lost files), implying paper persistence. No evidence of digital case management systems at stations; general admin digitisation (e.g., voter registers, data protection law 2026) does not extend to rural police recording. Rural facilities (limited staffing, resources) predominantly/entirely paper-based, per francophone Africa norms (handwritten PVs standard).
Source URLs: [Code de Procédure Pénale du Burundi](http://defensewiki.ibj.org/index.php/Code_de_Proc%C3%A9dure_P%C3%A9nale_du_Burundi_(Burundi_Criminal_Procedure_Code)), [HRW Mob Justice Report](https://www.hrw.org/report/2010/03/26/mob-justice-burundi/official-complicity-and-impunity), [US State Dept 2024 HR Report Burundi](https://www.state.gov/reports/2024-country-reports-on-human-rights-practices/burundi)"

Comments: Rural registry offices in Burundi, known as communal état civil bureaus, record births using entirely paper-based processes: parents declare births within 15 days, presenting hospital notifications; officials assign volume numbers, stamp documents, and type extracts manually. No electronic data entry or databases at point-of-service.ECOI.net report based on Burundi Embassy A 2023 UNECA survey (Oct 2022-Apr 2023) confirms Burundi has no national electronic CRVS database, with business processes (notification to certification) remaining paper-based nationwide, including rural areas; records transferred via paper copies.UNECA-AfDB-COMESA CRVS Digitalization report No evidence of e-CRVS pilots, digital tools, or deployments in rural registries found in recent searches (up to 2026). Barriers include lacking IT infrastructure, electricity/internet in rural zones, understaffing, and funding shortages noted in regional CRVS assessments. While national digital economy diagnostics highlight ambitions,World Bank Burundi Digital Economy rural CRVS reality lags, with completeness rates low (implied by surveys) and no shift from manual recording.
Source URLs: [UNECA CRVS Digitalization Report](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/Digitalization%20of%20Civil%20Registration%20and%20Vital%20Statistics%20in%20Africa.pdf); [ECOI.net Burundi Birth Registration](https://www.ecoi.net/en/document/2027844.html); [Citizenship Rights Africa CRVS Snapshot](https://citizenshiprightsafrica.org/snapshot-of-crvs-systems-in-burundi/)

Comments: Cabo Verde has implemented DHIS2 for health management information system, including trackers for 18 programs (WHO AFRO-funded) and used for COVID surveillance/vaccines, indicating aggregate digital reporting from facilities including rural island clinics DHIS2 Community Forum, DIAL Exchange. WHO CCS 2024-2028 prioritizes strengthening digital health, modernizing HIS/DHIS2, but notes challenges in data timeliness/quality and lack of integrated digital strategy; no evidence of EMR or digital patient records (registers, prescriptions) at primary/rural clinics WHO CCS. Rural clinics likely use paper records with aggregate entry at higher levels, fitting score 2 (predominantly paper, some digital aggregate/pilots). No paper-specific confirmation found, but absence of EMR evidence in advanced system supports this over higher scores.
Source URLs: [DHIS2 Community Forum](https://community.dhis2.org/t/realistic-dhis2-implementation-timeline/64021), [DIAL Exchange](https://exchange.dial.global/projects/cape-verde-dhis2-for-covid19-surveillance-cabo-verde), [WHO CCS 2024-2028](https://www.afro.who.int/sites/default/files/2025-09/CCSCaboVerde-eng.pdf)"

Comments: Rural primary schools in Cabo Verde use the SIGE (Sistema Integrado de Gestão Escolar), a digital platform rolled out nationally by 2019 to all 395 basic education schools, including rural ones across the territory, for recording attendance, grades, administrative data, and enabling online parental access. This indicates digital systems are standard at point of service for admin and attendance. National EMIS/SIGE supports evidence-based decisions with real-time data. Digital initiatives like computer distribution to all basic schools (2021) and WebLabs enhance capacity. However, rural-urban gaps persist in internet access, device ownership, and connectivity (Afrobarometer 2020; World Bank 2020), with some reliance on printed materials in low-coverage areas during COVID, suggesting some paper processes remain. Barriers include infrastructure disparities and digital literacy. National policy via SIGE achieves majority digital use at rural facilities, but full rollout challenges yield score 3.
Source URLs: [Ministry of Education SIGE](https://minedu.gov.cv/recursos_educativossigemenu); [UNESCO IICBA Brief](https://www.iicba.unesco.org/en/cabo-verde); [Education Profiles Technology](https://education-profiles.org/sub-saharan-africa/cabo-verde/~technology); [World Bank Digital CV](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/933721603977574627/pdf/Cabo-Verde-Digital-Cabo-Verde-Project.pdf); [Afrobarometer](https://www.afrobarometer.org/articles/many-cabo-verdeans-have-access-e-learning-devices-rural-urban-and-poverty-gaps-persist/)

Comments: No specific evidence found of digital recording of case files, complaints, or incident reports at rural police stations/posts. National Police has regional commands and posts on rural islands (e.g., Santo Antão, Fogo). Recent SIJ judicial system (2026) integrates police with courts for process tracking but focuses on court-level digitalisation, not initial station recording. US State Dept HR reports and UNODC training mention no station-level digital tools. General gov digital push exists, but rural police likely use paper for initial data collection, consistent with many African countries absent contrary evidence. Score 1 as broad paper use indicated.
Source URLs: [US State Dept 2024 HR Report](https://www.state.gov/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/624521_CABO-VERDE-2024-HUMAN-RIGHTS-REPORT.pdf), [SIJ Terra Nova](https://terranova.cv/pt_PT/cabo-verde-lanca-sistema-digital-para-modernizar-funcionamento-da-justica/), [Wikipedia Police Structure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Cape_Verde)

Comments: Cabo Verde has one of Africa's most robust CRVS systems, with nearly 100% death certification by doctors and significant integration of information systems for vital statistics. WHO assessment highlights progress in CRVS, with recommendations for real-time digitization and IT reinforcement, indicating predominantly digital operations with some paper elements. National SNIAC system coordinates civil identification and registration, providing electronic authentication, digital signatures, and integration with health, education, etc. Government sites mention online requests for certificates via portals like Porton di Nos Ilha and consular platforms (100% digital processes). High birth registration completeness (~91% per World Bank). No explicit rural-specific evidence found, but centralized digital infrastructure (SNIAC aims for national coverage including diaspora) suggests rural registry points use digital systems, though initial recording may involve paper in remote areas. Score 3 as predominantly digital nationally, with ongoing digitization.
Source URLs: [WHO CRVS Assessment](https://www.afro.who.int/countries/cabo-verde/news/cabo-verde-takes-historic-step-mortality-management-support-who-and-portuguese-cooperation), [World Bank Digital Transformation](https://www.worldbank.org/en/news/feature/2024/10/23/afw-digital-transformation-in-cabo-verde-the-new-horizon), [Direção Geral Registos](https://justica.gov.cv/dgrni), [SNIAC System](https://justica.gov.cv/sniac)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Cameroon predominantly use paper-based registers and manual forms for patient data recording at point-of-service. Individual client data management functions adequately in only 26% of facilities (urban study), with clinical staff handling data alongside duties using physical tools; electronic patient files are proposed but not implemented routinely. Aggregated data for DHIS2 reporting is compiled manually and entered at district level, not digitally at clinics. Pilots exist for EMRs like OpenMRS/GNU Health in specific regions (e.g., East/Centre for HIV/TB) and DAMA electronic registers in 154 PEPFAR sites, but these are isolated, program-specific, and not representative of most rural clinics. National DHIS2 is scaled but for aggregate reporting, not point-of-care patient records. Key barriers include poor electricity/internet in rural areas (avg. 6.9km to power; low 3G/4G), low computer access (32% facilities), lack of ICT skills among staff, high poverty, and governance gaps (85% unaware of digital health policies). National Digital Health Strategy 2020-2024 aims for EMR in 70% facilities by 2024, but rural reality lags far behind urban/national ambitions, with paper default and digital limited to upward reporting/pilots.
Source URLs: [Routine health information system in Yaoundé HFs](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709293/); [Digital health landscape Cameroon](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12605872/); [Cameroon Digital Health Strategy 2020-2024](https://extranet.who.int/cpcd/sites/default/files/public_file_repository/cmr_cameroon_digital-health-strategy_2020-2024.pdf)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Cameroon predominantly use paper-based registers for recording administrative data, attendance, and learning outcomes. Only 3% of public primary schools have computers, with 87% of ICT teaching theoretical due to lack of resources.ERIC study on ICT integration Recent reports confirm many rural schools rely on paper records instead of digital databases.Ministry warning post National EMIS (STATEDUC platform) exists for aggregation at national level via INS, but implementation is delayed and focused on central/regional levels, not point-of-service in rural schools.World Bank ISR Digital pilots like UNICEF Connect My School are isolated (e.g., one e-container in Southwest).IHS-UNICEF partnership Key barriers: electricity/internet shortages, no equipment (66.6% admin structures lack computers), insufficient training, funding reliance on donors.ERIC study National policies (GESP, ETSS, ERSP) aim for integrated EMIS and digitalisation, but rural reality lags due to infrastructure gaps and crises disrupting data collection in areas like Far North.World Bank Equity project
Source URLs: [ERIC ICT integration study](https://files.eric.ed.gov/fulltext/EJ1099586.pdf); [World Bank Education Reform ISR](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099011724134528081/pdf/P16092615b7f0704918a461f76a0018d893.pdf); [World Bank Equity and Quality project](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/720691587749369240/pdf/Cameroon-Equity-and-Quality-for-Improved-Learning-Project.pdf)

Comments: Rural police stations in Cameroon, primarily Gendarmerie Nationale brigades/posts (444 brigades, 160 posts), record case files, complaints, and incident reports using handwritten procès-verbaux (PVs) and paper files, as standard in francophone Africa and per Criminal Procedure Code. Criminal records derived from these are manually stored in hardcopy at courts/Ministry of Justice; police do not maintain digital criminal records. No evidence of digital case management systems, rural pilots, or IT for incident reporting found on official sites, despite general digital pushes (e.g., civil registration). Paper-based at facility level; any later court entry does not qualify as digital at rural station per rules. Gendarmerie, Records Paper, CPC
Source URLs: [Gendarmerie Nationale Presentation](https://mindef.gov.cm/gendarmerie-nationale/en/presentation/), [Official Management of Civil and Criminal Records in Cameroon](https://ijnrd.org/papers/IJNRD2504292.pdf), [Cameroon Criminal Procedure Code via UNODC](https://www.unodc.org/cld/uploads/res/document/criminal-procedre-code_html/Cameroon_Criminal_Procedure_Code_2005.pdf)"

Comments: Rural registry offices (2,350 secondary civil status centers in villages) predominantly use paper-based recording for births and other vital events, as the national system was not computerized as of 2018-2019 reports.UNECA CRVS Snapshot New 2024 law (Law No. 2024/016) introduces digital certificates, UPIN, national database, and pilot phases for computerization, with first digital births issued in select urban councils in 2026 (e.g., Limbe I).Journal of Digital Technologies and Law; UNDP Strategic plan (2025-2029) aims for full digital by 2029, but current rollout limited to pilots/donor projects in handful of municipalities, not majority rural facilities.Vital Strategies Key barriers: poor infrastructure/internet in rural areas, low digital literacy, distance/cost to centers, backlog under-registration.UNECA CRVS Snapshot; Journal of Digital Technologies and Law National ambition for digital CRVS contrasts with rural paper reality; no evidence of predominant point-of-service digital use in rural registries.
