Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Central African Republic rely entirely on paper-based registers and forms for patient data recording at the point of care. The HMIS roadmap (2019) revised paper tools rolled out to 12 districts, with DHIS2 piloted only in Bangui (urban capital); no routine use in rural health centers (387 centers, 434 posts serving rural areas). World Bank SENI-PLUS (2022) notes plans for DHIS2 rollout but persistent barriers: poor internet connectivity, low staff capacity for data collection, weak data quality culture, insecurity from ongoing conflict.World Bank SENI-PLUS No national eHealth strategy exists (2023).Mayo Clinic Proceedings Recent donor-driven pilots like DHIS2-based immunization micro-planning exist but are isolated, not for patient records at POC.HISP Rwanda Key barriers: conflict (70% countryside outside govt control), infrastructure deficits, low capacity. National ambition for DHIS2 contrasts sharply with rural paper reality; data transcribed upward from paper, not captured digitally at facilities. No evidence majority rural clinics use digital systems routinely.
Source URLs: [World Bank SENI-PLUS Project](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/810311653593644795/pdf/Central-African-Republic-Health-Service-Delivery-and-System-Strengthening-Project-SENI-PLUS.pdf); [Mayo Clinic Digital Health Africa](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10512197/); [Conflict and Health CAR HIS](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8444360/)

Comments: No direct evidence on rural primary school data recording methods found despite extensive searches on EMIS, school data collection, UNESCO/World Bank/GPE documents. CAR participates in regional EMIS efforts and has a national system, but given ongoing conflict, low ICT infrastructure, and plans to strengthen data systems (PSE 2020-2029), rural facilities predominantly or entirely use paper registers for attendance, enrollment, grades, and admin data. 2007 World Bank survey highlights ICT challenges in education; recent reports note limited learning data availability.
Source URLs: [UNESCO CAR Education Brief](https://www.iicba.unesco.org/en/central-african-republic-car), [GPE KIX EMIS Meeting](https://www.gpekix.org/news/education-management-information-systems-emis-heart-kix-objectives), [World Bank ICT Survey CAR](https://openknowledge.worldbank.org/entities/publication/9eb68001-a942-54c5-857b-2726c3ff89b0)

Comments: No evidence found of digital case management or record-keeping systems in rural Central African Republic (CAR) police stations or gendarmerie brigades. US State Department 2022 Human Rights Report notes poor recordkeeping of arrests/detentions, slow case processing due to understaffed/undertrained judicial police, and destruction of records in 2013 conflict, with rural stations (e.g., Bossembele, Boda) holding detainees in basic conditions. Complainants reportedly pay fees to file complaints. Recent UN MINUSCA donations (2025) of computers/printers/solar to rural Baoro station aim to facilitate drafting procès-verbaux/investigation reports, implying prior manual processes and suggesting transition from paper but not yet implemented widely. UK FCDO arrest pack and UNODC reports silent on digital tools; searches yielded no national police IT system for rural use. Ongoing conflict, infrastructure deficits, and aid focus on basics indicate entirely paper-based (or inadequate) systems in rural facilities.
Source URLs: [US State Dept 2022 HR Report](https://www.state.gov/wp-content/uploads/2023/02/415610_CENTRAL-AFRICAN-REPUBLIC-2022-HUMAN-RIGHTS-REPORT.pdf), [UK FCDO CAR Arrest Info](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/central-african-republic-legal-and-prison-systems/information-pack-for-british-nationals-arrested-or-detained-in-central-african-republic), [UN MINUSCA Baoro Equipment](https://peacekeeping.un.org/fr/de-nouveaux-equipements-pour-le-commissariat-et-la-gendarmerie-de-baoro)"

Comments: Rural registry offices in Central African Republic (secondary centres in villages) record births using paper forms and ledgers, with paper copies transferred throughout the system to central storage, per 2023 UNECA assessment of 43 African countries. No electronic CRVS database exists (one of 7 countries). FamilySearch confirms secondary centres in villages handle births, with no digital mention. World Bank ID4D (2017) details paper-based processes (loose-leaf/ledgers), no ICT (score 1/4); post-2013 crisis destroyed most rural offices, but surviving processes remain manual. Rural birth registration rates low (e.g., 36-63% 2000-2010). No evidence of digital rollout in recent (2020-2026) reports; one digital ministry (economy, 2026) unrelated. CDC older report notes subnational centres d'état civil, low completeness. Entirely paper-based in rural facilities.
Source URLs: [UNECA CRVS Digitalization Africa (2023)](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/Digitalization%20of%20Civil%20Registration%20and%20Vital%20Statistics%20in%20Africa.pdf), [World Bank ID4D CAR Report (2017)](https://openknowledge.worldbank.org/server/api/core/bitstreams/fde5ba5d-4bb9-5671-94ca-67ee6a832dd3/content), [FamilySearch CAR Civil Registration](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Central_African_Republic_Civil_Registration)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Chad predominantly use paper registers for patient data recording at the point of service. Aggregate data from these paper records is collated monthly and forwarded to district levels, where it is entered digitally into DHIS2, with 96% of districts now reporting directly into the system as of 2022.DHIS2 Chad transition. A 2021 study in Massaguet district (including rural health centres) confirmed routine use of paper-based external consultation registers and HMIS forms, with data digitized only at district level using Excel; no EMR at clinics, and paper stock-outs common, leading to inaccuracies.BMC study Massaguet. No evidence found of routine digital entry, EMR, or DHIS2 at point of care in rural clinics; plans exist for tablets in northern regions for offline DHIS2 aggregate entry, but not implemented widely.DHIS2 Chad transition. Key barriers: poor internet/power in rural north, staff workload, form stock-outs, low digital literacy. National ambition via DHIS2 rollout contrasts with rural paper reality; pilots/digital limited to aggregation, not point-of-service.
Source URLs: [DHIS2 Chad HMIS](https://dhis2.org/chad-hmis-transition/); [BMC Med Inform Massaguet HMIS](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8609810/); [BMC Health Serv rural Chad nurses](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5759836/)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Chad record administrative data, attendance (annual snapshots), and proxies for learning outcomes (repetition/pass rates) using handwritten paper questionnaires and registers. School directors and teachers manually fill 4-10 page EMIS forms covering enrollment by class/gender/age/status, infrastructure, teachers, materials, finances. These paper forms are physically transported upward through a hierarchical structure: school → zone inspectors → district → provincial → national DAPRO, where data is entered digitally via StatEduc2 software.UNESCO ECW Chad EMIS Case Study%20and%20Data%20for%20Increased%20Resilience%20to%20Crisis_%20country%20case%20study_%20Chad.pdf) No evidence of digital tools at point-of-service; process remains fully manual at rural facilities, with last full survey in 2016 and 2019 requiring special national efforts due to logistics. Digital systems like national EMIS database exist for aggregation, but rural recording is paper-based transcription later. Limited pilots: UNICEF EduTrac SMS/mobile in 2 provinces (not scaled); no widespread adoption.UNESCO ECW Chad EMIS Case Study%20and%20Data%20for%20Increased%20Resilience%20to%20Crisis_%20country%20case%20study_%20Chad.pdf);World Bank Chad Learning Poverty Brief Key barriers: vast rural distances/poor transport, lack of subnational capacity/training/verification, no feedback to schools causing fatigue, French-only forms in Arabic areas, no birth certificates for accurate age/enrollment. Gap: National EMIS strategy (2014)/sector plans aspire to better data, but rural reality lags due to infrastructure/human resources; digital ambitions (e.g., ecole.cnc.td platform) focus on content, not POS recording.UNESCO IICBA Chad Brief
Source URLs: [UNESCO ECW Chad EMIS Case Study](https://www.educationcannotwait.org/sites/default/files/2022-03/Strengthening%20Education%20Management%20Information%20Systems%20(EMIS)%20and%20Data%20for%20Increased%20Resilience%20to%20Crisis_%20country%20case%20study_%20Chad.pdf); [World Bank Chad Learning Poverty Brief](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099062624135034155/pdf/P179209-46e8d7eb-c95b-40e3-8f7b-6103046b35d1.pdf); [UNESCO IICBA Chad Education Brief](https://www.iicba.unesco.org/en/chad)

Comments: Evidence suggests rural police stations in Chad are still predominantly paper-based, with digitisation happening mainly at central level. INTERPOL’s EU-funded Chad Police Information System (SIPT) (a WAPIS-like system) was created to move away from paper records and provide a centralized computerized database for police data (persons, offences, vehicles, documents), managed via a Data Collection/Registration Center (DACORE/CREDOP) that records and validates police data; INTERPOL notes longer-term plans to deploy access to the system across agencies and regions/border posts depending on infrastructure, implying that broad station-level digital entry is not yet universal. Recent US State Department human rights reporting describes police and detention procedures but does not indicate routine digital case/complaint recording, consistent with continued reliance on traditional paper procès-verbaux at station level, especially outside N’Djamena. Overall: predominantly paper at rural stations, with some digitalisation through SIPT at national/central nodes and potential roll-out pilots.
Source URLs: [INTERPOL WAPIS/SIPT brochure (2020)](https://www.interpol.int/content/download/16378/file/20COM0209-WAPIS_Programme_21x21_08-2020_EN_03_24pagesLR_Optimized.pdf), [US State Dept Chad Human Rights Report 2023 (PDF)](https://www.state.gov/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/528267_CHAD-2023-HUMAN-RIGHTS-REPORT.pdf), [INTERPOL SIPT inauguration post (LinkedIn)](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/interpol_inauguration-of-the-chad-police-data-collection-activity-7372186166529318913-B65p)

Comments: Rural registry offices in Chad predominantly use paper-based systems for birth registration. Recent data shows only 26% national birth registration rate among under-5s, with 84% of children rural and significantly lower rates in rural vs urban areas (largest gaps in Chad per UAPS 2024 analysis), indicating poor coverage and likely manual recording at remote points. A UNICEF-supported Tasdjil digital app (launched 2022, offline-capable, <10 min process) has enabled 109k+ registrations, often at health facilities, representing a donor pilot to boost access. No evidence of majority rural facilities using digital point-of-service; paper remains default. Barriers: remoteness (Chad 80%+ rural), poverty, low awareness, infrastructure deficits. National efforts (e.g., centers, campaigns) exist but gap persists between policy and rural reality—digital limited to pilots, not scaled/standardized at rural registries. Score conservative due to thin specific evidence on recording methods.
Source URLs: [UAPS 2024 birth registration analysis](https://uaps2024.popconf.org/uploads/191469); [UNICEF USA on Tasdjil app](https://www.unicefusa.org/stories/unicef-and-child-protection-birth-registration-first-line-defense-childrens-rights); [FamilySearch Chad CR overview](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Chad_Civil_Registration)

Comments: Rural clinics in Comoros transitioned from paper-based stock and service reporting to DHIS2, used by 99% of public facilities including primaries. 2024 World Bank-funded eLMIS digitized stock management at 78% of 74 facilities via Android RTS tool, replacing handwritten records and enabling real-time data (previously weeks for aggregation) DHIS2. WB COMPASS (2019) notes HMIS DHIS2 upgrade ongoing, but PBF verifies against paper registries/cards; only 14% facilities have computers World Bank. No EMR/patient records found; likely paper at clinic level, digitized aggregates later. Score 2: Predominantly paper, some digital via DHIS2 pilots/projects.
Source URLs: [DHIS2 Supply Chain Report](https://dhis2.org/supply-chain-management-dhis2-open-msupply-comoros/), [World Bank COMPASS Project](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/988861560788593578/pdf/Comoros-Comprehensive-Approach-to-Health-System-Strengthening-Project.pdf), [PMC Health Overview](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8378088/)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Comoros predominantly use paper-based registers for administrative data, attendance, and learning outcomes. The national EMIS (SIGE) was designed to collect data from all schools but has been defunct since around 2018 due to funding shortages and complexity, relying on likely manual paper inputs aggregated centrally World Bank 2021 Assessment. Digital infrastructure is minimal: only 30.8% primary schools had computers (2017), 40.9% electricity (2017)/48.4% (2021), and 8% internet (2017)/21.4% (2021), with no rural-urban split but rural areas facing greater disparities in access and infrastructure UNESCO SDG4 Profile. Recent national digital platforms like e-Msomo (launched ~2025, for student records/grades/staff mgmt) and E-Shiyo (online textbooks) exist, supported by UNICEF/ANADEN, but no evidence of routine use at rural point-of-service; low connectivity (35.7% national internet) and hardware limits rollout to pilots or urban. Key barriers: poor infrastructure, electricity/internet deficits, budget dominated by salaries (96-99%), cyclones disrupting data. National ambitions (e.g., digital strategy 2028) outpace rural reality, where paper remains default.
Source URLs: [World Bank Assessment](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099625012152115623/pdf/P17443203a278e060b1b607154998bf331.pdf); [UNESCO SDG4 Profile](https://download.uis.unesco.org/SDG4/SDG4-Profile-Comoros.pdf); [e-Msomo](https://www.e-msomo.com)

Comments: Comoros rural police stations use entirely paper-based recording for case files, complaints, and incident reports. The 2014 Criminal Procedure Code mandates officers draft procès-verbaux (PVs) on-site for all investigations, statements, seizures, and garde à vue; PVs are signed per page, read aloud, and originals physically sent to prosecutors—no digital/electronic methods mentioned. No evidence of digital case management systems, pilots, or digitisation at station level found in national justice site, US State Dept HR reports (2023-2024), UNODC, or OC Index. Justice reforms focus on courts/IT equipment, not police. Francophone procedures confirm handwritten PVs standard in rural posts. Score 1 as paper-mandated, no rural digital evidence.
Source URLs: [Comoros Code de Procédure Pénale](https://justice.gouv.km/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/loi-du-22-decembre-2014-portant-code-de-procedure-penale.pdf), [US State Dept 2023 HR Report Comoros](https://www.state.gov/reports/2023-country-reports-on-human-rights-practices/comoros), [US State Dept 2024 HR Report Comoros](https://www.state.gov/reports/2024-country-reports-on-human-rights-practices/comoros)

Comments: Rural registry offices in Comoros (prefectures and communes, many in rural areas) currently record births using paper-based état civil registers and forms, as indicated by historical records and lack of evidence for electronic entry at point of service.FamilySearch Wiki A 2016 ECA assessment placed Comoros in the lowest category (<35%) for digitization, infrastructure (35-49%), and processes, with regional trends showing most local offices paper-dependent and under-resourced.ECA Report No routine digital systems at rural facilities; completeness low due to rural inaccessibility. Recent developments include a 2023 law mandating computerization and 2026 validation of a nationwide digitization plan (Amecc, funded by France/UNICEF), responding to a 2022 assessment highlighting a "largely manual" system prone to errors and fraud. This remains a national ambition/pilot stage, not deployed at rural points of service.We Are Tech Africa Key barriers: poor infrastructure, limited HR/training, low digital maturity (esp. outer islands), underfunding. Gap: Policy pushes digital ID/CRVS interoperability, but rural reality is entirely paper-based with ad hoc efforts.
Source URLs: [ECA CRVS Report](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/report_on_the_status_of_civil_registration_and_vital_statistics_in_africa.pdf); [We Are Tech Africa](https://www.wearetech.africa/en/fils-uk/news/tech/comoros-advances-digital-identity-reform-with-nationwide-registry-overhaul); [FamilySearch Wiki](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Comoros_Civil_Registration)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics (centres de santé rurale, CSRs) in Côte d'Ivoire predominantly record patient data using paper-based registers and forms at the point of service. Data is manually aggregated into summary reports and transmitted monthly/quarterly to district or regional levels, where it is entered into the national DHIS2 health management information system (HMIS).<grok:richcontent id="d0a9a8" type="render_inline_citation"><argument name="citation_id">0</argument></grok:richcontent><grok:richcontent id="5e5f2d" type="render_inline_citation"><argument name="citation_id">1</argument></grok:richcontent> No evidence indicates routine use of electronic health records (EHR), electronic medical records (EMR), or digital point-of-care entry in most rural clinics; paper remains the default method despite national digital infrastructure like DHIS2 rollout (nationwide since ~2019).<grok:richcontent id="9d5a92" type="render_inline_citation"><argument name="citation_id">2</argument></grok:richcontent> Digital systems are limited to isolated pilots or disease-specific programs, such as SIGDEP for HIV patient management (233 sites, electronic but not general care) and recent donor-supported trackers (e.g., cervical cancer screening).<grok:richcontent id="044c38" type="render_inline_citation"><argument name="citation_id">0</argument></grok:richcontent> The 2021+ PHISICC project redesigns paper tools for better usability in rural Côte d'Ivoire facilities, confirming paper's ongoing dominance and overload on health workers.<grok:richcontent id="a8e5d6" type="render_inline_citation"><argument name="citation_id">3</argument></grok:richcontent> Key barriers include unreliable electricity/internet in rural areas, limited devices/training, and prioritization of aggregate national reporting over point-of-service digitalisation. Recent plans (2025) aim to connect 1,000 clinics to high-speed internet for telemedicine/digitized records, signaling ambition but current gap. National policy focuses on DHIS2/eLMIS for HMIS/logistics, but rural PoS reality lags: paper collection followed by higher-level transcription, fitting score 2 (predominantly paper with some digital pilots/aggregate tools).
Source URLs: [MEASURE Evaluation](https://www.measureevaluation.org/resources/publications/fs-19-380/at_download/document); [Global Health Action](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4185136/); [PHISICC protocol](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8355573/)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Côte d'Ivoire predominantly record attendance, enrollment, grades, and admin data on paper. Data flows from schools to regional DRENs then ministry DTSI, often via physical questionnaires causing delays and errors. Digital EMIS/SIGE centralizes aggregates but relies on higher-level entry, not school-level digital recording. SIGE piloted in select regions (Bouaké, San Pedro - mixed urban/rural) and secondary schools; primary extension ongoing but rural coverage limited by infrastructure (62% primary schools lack electricity, 2% have multimedia per SNDECI 2024-28). No evidence of digital at rural facility level; score 2 for pilots/aggregate digital.
Source URLs: [MENA SIGE Launch](https://www.education.gouv.ci/index.php/Activite/details/372), [Development Gateway Data Flow](https://developmentgateway.org/blog/improving-the-national-ministry-of-educations-information-management-system/), [SNDECI Strategy](https://www.education.gouv.ci/assets/pdf/Document/Orientation/document_220.pdf)

Comments: No specific evidence found of digital recording at rural police stations or gendarmerie brigades in Côte d'Ivoire. Standard francophone practice of handwritten procès-verbaux (police reports) persists, as indicated by models and procedures. Central initiatives exist: CTIP/BTIP for police data processing in Abidjan (Ministry of Interior), WAPIS regional system (INTERPOL), and justice digitalisation (e-justice.ci). US State Dept reports describe arrests/detentions at stations but no digital mention (State.gov 2023). Rural facilities likely paper-based, with possible later central entry (score 1 per rules).
Source URLs: [Ministry of Interior](https://interieur.gouv.ci/actualite/19), [INTERPOL WAPIS](https://www.interpol.int/content/download/12480/file/WAPIS_BROCHURE_EN_002_LR.pdf), [US State Dept HR Report](https://www.state.gov/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/528267_COTE-DIVOIRE-2023-HUMAN-RIGHTS-REPORT.pdf)

Comments: Rural registry offices in Côte d'Ivoire predominantly use paper-based recording for births and vital events, with manual registration at local civil status centers (Bureau de l'État Civil). Birth registration coverage is lower in rural areas (60% in 2016) compared to urban (90%), indicating accessibility issues.GFF CRVS Profile A national digital system (Cityweb software by ONECI) is being rolled out, currently in 105 of 860 centers across 5 regions as of early 2024, with plans for 80% by end-2024, enabling digital entry, real-time updates, and no manual transmission.Semlex Cityweb Côte d'Ivoire uses fully electronic transmission from local to central levels and outsources data hosting, but lacks dedicated IT staff and details on rural point-of-service digital use.UNECA CRVS Digitalization This represents isolated digital pilots/projects amid paper default; no evidence majority rural facilities routinely digital at point of care. Key barriers: rural infrastructure, training, electricity. National ambition (RNPP, e-services) outpaces rural reality, where paper persists for most facilities.
Source URLs: [GFF CRVS Profile](https://gffklportal.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/01/5.5.-Cote-dIvore-CRVS-country-profile.pdf); [UNECA Digitalization of CRVS in Africa](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/Digitalization%20of%20Civil%20Registration%20and%20Vital%20Statistics%20in%20Africa.pdf); [Semlex Cityweb Modernization](https://www.semlex.com/en/2024/02/12/la-modernisation-de-letat-civil-en-cote-divoire/)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) predominantly use paper-based systems for recording patient data. Community health workers collect data on paper forms, which facilities collate into paper reports on program outcomes, stock, and surveillance. These are sent to health zones for entry into the national DHIS2 (SNIS) platform.DHIS2 DRC Data Use Decentralized digital entry at facilities is rare, limited to some large sites with reliable power/internet; most entry is centralized at zones due to infrastructure deficits.DHIS2 DRC Data Use TB data from 2061 centers follows this: paper forms validated at centers, forwarded to zones for DHIS2.DHIS2 DRC Data Use Isolated pilots (e.g., donor laptops in 63 facilities) succeeded briefly but failed post-funding due to maintenance issues.DHIS2 DRC Data Use No widespread EMR; some OpenMRS pilots exist but not scaled to majority rural clinics.OpenMRS EMR4All Barriers: poor electricity/internet, funding, skills, outdated paper tools. National DHIS2 (since 2013) excels in aggregate reporting (80%+ completeness), but rural point-of-care remains paper, causing transcription errors. Policy ambition for digital UHC contrasts with rural paper reality.
Source URLs: [DHIS2 DRC Data Use](https://dhis2.org/drc-data-use/); [Stop TB DRC TB Report](https://tbassessment.stoptb.org/assets/docs/Digital%20TB%20Surveillance%20System%20Assessment%20All%20Country%20Reports/DR%20Congo%20Digital%20TB%20Surveillance%20System%20Assessment%20Report.pdf); [HISP DHIS2 Implementation DRC](https://www.childhealthtaskforce.org/sites/default/files/2018-09/data_wkshp_drc_dhis2_implem.pdf)

Comments: Rural primary schools in DRC predominantly use paper-based registers for recording attendance, enrollment, and administrative data. School directors complete printed questionnaires for the national statistical yearbook, sent and returned physically by ground transport, leading to low response rates, delays, and reliance on estimates due to losses and poor infrastructure, especially in rural areas. No evidence of routine digital data entry at the point of service; EMIS efforts focus on national/provincial aggregation and decentralized planning (e.g., UNICEF-supported decentralized EMIS, Cordaid's GPS-mapped database combining existing sources), not school-level digital tools. SECOPE maintains a centralized (insecure) digital payroll database, but schools handle paper files. Barriers include poor ICT infrastructure, electricity/connectivity deficits in rural areas (e.g., only 7% rural schools with electricity per recent WB analysis), logistical challenges, and lack of devices/training. National SSEF policy aspires to EMIS dashboards, but rural reality remains manual recording with upward paper transcription, unchanged as of 2017-2024 reports.
Source URLs: [IRC Education Governance Report](https://www.rescue.org/sites/default/files/document/1310/drceducationgovernancefinaljanuary2017.pdf); [UNESCO IICBA DRC Brief](https://www.iicba.unesco.org/en/congo-democratic-republic-drc); [Cordaid School Database Report](https://reliefweb.int/report/democratic-republic-congo/better-data-better-schools-better-education); [WB Digital Readiness Secondary Schools](https://thedocs.worldbank.org/en/doc/d3291c08f4b645fdc31be4908947ef6b-0140022025/related/5EDU-WP-5-An-Assessment-of-the-Digital-Readiness-of-Secondary-Schools-in-the-DRC.pdf)"

Comments: Rural police stations in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (PNC) record criminal cases and complaints primarily through paper-based procès-verbaux (official statements/reports for findings, arrests, hearings, etc.). These documents are generally typed but hearings are handwritten in most cases, with strict formal requirements for validity (e.g., signed, dated). No evidence of digital case management systems at rural level; procedures rely on written complaints and manual OPJ processes. Recent HRIS is for personnel management only, not cases. UN tools like goCASE used by MONUSCO, not PNC. Reforms emphasize training/accountability, not digital recording. Rural infrastructure likely precludes digital adoption.
Source URLs: [ECOI.net/IRB Canada Report on PNC Documents](https://www.ecoi.net/en/document/2092757.html), [Coginta on PNC HRIS](https://coginta.org/en/DRC-modernization-of-HR-management-of-the-Congolese-national-police/), [UNODC goCASE](https://gocase.unodc.org/gocase/uploads/documents/goCASE_System_Description_2025.pdf)

Comments: Rural registry offices in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (877 out of 979 local offices) record births using entirely paper-based carbon copy registers with four copies per event: one to applicant, one to local court, one to central office in Kinshasa, one retained locally. No computers or mobile technology used in the registration process. Accessibility challenges in rural areas exacerbate low registration rates, but recording remains manual. Recent pilots (e.g., Masina urban) and plans for historical digitization exist, but no evidence of digital recording in rural facilities as of 2019 reports; national digital plans (2026+) do not yet impact rural registries.
Source URLs: [CRVS Snapshot DRC](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/CRVS_CongoDRSnapshot_e.pdf), [FamilySearch DRC Civil Reg](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_Civil_Registration)

Comments: Djibouti's rural primary health centres (38 facilities) operate in a pyramidal system. No evidence found of EMR or digital patient records/registers/prescriptions at clinic level. Djibouti uses DHIS2 for HMIS, with recent IGAD digital equipment donations to districts/HIS. World Bank Health Strengthening Project reports 84% facilities with DHIS2 data entry; some enter directly, others use paper and submit immediately; manual disaggregated data collection noted. Indicates predominant paper-based recording at rural clinics with aggregate digital entry (some direct), fitting score 2. Lacks rural-specific data but general facility mix and absence of EMR evidence supports this over 1 or 3. World Bank ISR, IGAD, WHO EMRO private health
Source URLs: [World Bank ISR](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099102225172035856/pdf/P178033-79193a4a-bd86-4d36-b863-cdbc43bde6c7.pdf), [IGAD digital health](https://igad.int/igad-hands-over-digital-health-equipment-to-djiboutis-ministry-of-health/), [WHO private health sector Djibouti](https://applications.emro.who.int/docs/9789292742102-eng.pdf)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Djibouti use the OPAD digital application for data collection on administrative metrics like enrollment and outcomes, as per World Bank analysis. However, data is manually re-entered centrally due to system incompatibilities, causing 7-month delays in reporting. This suggests digital tools exist at point-of-service but are not fully integrated or reliable for routine use. No specific evidence confirms daily attendance or learning outcomes are recorded digitally at rural facilities; traditional paper registers likely persist alongside apps for aggregate reporting. EMIS supports national statistical yearbooks via the Ministry's Planning Department, but rural schools (avg. 120 students, smaller than urban 527) face infrastructure barriers like poor connectivity, electricity, and ICT skills, as inferred from general reports. World Bank project (2019) aims to modernize EMIS with real-time portals and student tracking, prioritizing urban rollout before 60-70% rural coverage, indicating current predominant paper with emerging digital pilots. Key barriers: technical incompatibilities, limited training, rural access issues. National EMIS ambition contrasts with rural reality of partial digitization focused on aggregation, not point-of-care recording. Score 2 reflects isolated digital use for reporting, not standard at most rural sites.
Source URLs: [UNESCO IICBA Djibouti Brief](https://www.iicba.unesco.org/en/djibouti); [World Bank Expanding Opportunities Project](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/605651570154537746/pdf/Djibouti-Expanding-Opportunities-for-Learning-Project.pdf); [Oxford Business Group Report](https://oxfordbusinessgroup.com/reports/djibouti/2023-report/health-education/greater-reach-progress-is-being-made-to-make-education-more-accessible-nationwide-in-both-rural-and-urban-areas-overview/)"

Comments: Rural police stations in Djibouti are managed by the National Gendarmerie, responsible for security outside Djibouti City. No evidence found of digital case management, incident reports, or complaints recording at rural facilities. Djiboutian law mandates handwritten procès-verbaux (police reports) and registers for garde à vue in police and gendarmerie stations, indicating paper-based initial recording. US State Dept 2020 HR report notes records digitalization only in certain urban National Police precincts (phased implementation), aimed at tracking arrestees; no mention for gendarmerie or rural areas. Police response slow, resources limited per OSAC reports. Casier judiciaire (criminal records) issued manually by Ministry of Justice. Recent digital initiatives (e.g., 2025 Digital Code, mobile ID) focus on public services/ID, not police case files. In francophone Africa, rural stations typically use paper procès-verbaux. Thus, predominantly paper at rural level, with possible urban digitization pilots.
Source URLs: [US State Dept 2020 HR Report](https://www.state.gov/wp-content/uploads/2021/10/DJIBOUTI-2020-HUMAN-RIGHTS-REPORT.pdf), [Loi n° 30/78 garde à vue](https://www.journalofficiel.dj/texte-juridique/loi-n30-78-portant-sur-la-garde-a-vue/), [OSAC Djibouti Security Report](https://www.osac.gov/Content/Report/85c702b3-caa5-4490-8fdb-1d1697d44b6f)"

Comments: Djibouti rural registry offices (interior regions: Tadjourah, Obock, Dikhil, Ali-Sabieh, Arta) record vital events in paper registers with no computers or internet (~2013 WHO assessment). Monthly stats sent physically to central. Low rural coverage (20%), poor access. Urban areas have databases for hospital births. 2019 RNPP biometric ID system assigns NNI to those in civil registries, implying paper data entry at higher levels later. No evidence of rural digital recording or pilots; plans for computers (2013) unconfirmed in recent sources. Thus, rural facilities entirely paper-based.
Source URLs: [WHO EMRO CRVS Assessment Djibouti](https://www.emro.who.int/images/stories/crvs/documents/djibouti.pdf?ua=1), [Djibouti RNPP System](https://numerique.gouv.dj/Articles/detailArticleAvancee/15), [UNICEF CRVS Djibouti](https://data.unicef.org/crvs/djibouti/)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Egypt predominantly use paper-based patient records, with handwritten registers for clinical data like history, diagnosis, and treatment. A 2014 WHO-EMRO study in Alexandria PHC units showed parallel paper and early e-records, but paper had higher completeness; e-records varied widely by unit and were incomplete (EMRO study). Recent reports (2024-2026) indicate only 314 hospitals have EMRs, with public/rural lagging due to infrastructure limits (Arxiv paper). DHIS2 launched nationally in 2026 with pilots in select primary facilities for emergencies/refugees (nutrition screening in 16 sites), but not routine patient care at most rural clinics (DHIS2.org). National Digital Health Strategy 2025-2029 aims for ecosystem-wide digitalisation, but rural point-of-service remains paper-default. Barriers: poor connectivity, training gaps, resource scarcity in rural areas. Gap: Ambitious policies contrast with rural reality of paper dominance and pilot-only digital use.
Source URLs: [EMRO WHO study on PHC e-records](https://www.emro.who.int/emhj-vol-20-2014/volume-20-issue-2/quality-of-documentation-of-electronic-medical-information-systems-at-primary-health-care-units-in-alexandria-egypt.html); [Arxiv on EHR barriers Egypt](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.05603v1); [DHIS2 Egypt launch](https://dhis2.org/egypt-launches-dhis2-in-health-information-system/)

Comments: Egypt has an EMIS system integrated with national dashboards for decision-making, with recent (2025) EMIS-linked daily attendance monitoring achieving >80% attendance nationwide, including rural areas World Education Blog. 36,878/42,931 schools meet smart school criteria, but primary schools average 95 students/device, indicating limited digital tools in rural primaries UNESCO IICBA. World Bank projects mention EMIS data use but no school-level collection details. No evidence of direct digital recording (e.g., tablets/apps) at rural primary schools for attendance/enrollment/grades; likely paper-based with later aggregation (pilots/digital in some). Rural infrastructure gaps affect data reliability. Predominantly paper with some digital/pilots.
Source URLs: [World Bank Egypt Education Reform](https://thedocs.worldbank.org/en/doc/280941527621087910-0280022018/render/EgyptEducationReform.pdf), [UNESCO World Education Blog](https://world-education-blog.org/2025/12/22/monitoring-equity-through-verified-data-how-egypts-2025-education-reforms-are-reframing-progress-and-revealing-what-still-must-be-done/), [MOE Egypt](https://moe.gov.eg/en), [UIS UNESCO EMIS Guide](https://emis.uis.unesco.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/5/2020/09/EMIS-Operational-Guide-EN-WEB.pdf)

Comments: Egypt has national digital transformation initiatives in law enforcement, including the Digital Justice Platform by Egabi that starts with complaint registration and police record issuance at stations, and a unified law enforcement system integrating criminal cases.Egabi Digital Justice Platform, SIS Egypt. However, EFHR reports (2021) indicate police stations, especially outside Cairo/Alexandria (i.e., rural), often lack technology, with ~50% documents handwritten; arrest/investigation records sometimes paper-based depending on location.ECOI/IRB EFHR Report. No rural-specific digitalisation evidence found; paper predominant at rural stations with digital aggregation higher up, fitting score 2.
Source URLs: [Egabi Digital Justice Platform](https://egabi.com/solutions/digital-justice-platform), [SIS Egypt Digital Transformation](https://sis.gov.eg/en/media-center/news/pm-follows-up-application-of-digital-transformation-system-in-law-enforcement/), [ECOI/IRB EFHR Report](https://www.ecoi.net/en/document/2064759.html)

Comments: Rural registry offices and civil registration points in Egypt predominantly use paper-based forms for recording births and other vital events. Notifications are collected manually from health offices (some digitized via EBDRS since 2017 for ~4,571 offices, including rural via GPRS/3G) and sent as paper to local Civil Registry Offices, then compiled at governorate level where electronic data entry occurs before central processing WHO EMRO Questionnaire. Civil registry offices lack computers, scanners, internet, or other digital equipment—only paper is used WHO EMRO Questionnaire. Digital systems like EBDRS handle health notifications digitally at point-of-care and notify civil registry electronically, but registration at registry remains paper/manual African Union EBDRS. Recent advances (2024-2026) focus on urban kiosks/machines for certificate issuance and biometric apps for online services, with no evidence of rural rollout Ahram Online. Key barriers: poor ICT infrastructure in villages, no mobile registration units, inadequate budgets. National ambition (Egypt Vision 2030) pushes digital gov, but rural point-of-service lags, relying on aggregation/transcription—score 2 reflects some digital upstream but predominant paper at rural registries.
Source URLs: [WHO EMRO CRVS Questionnaire](https://www.emro.who.int/images/stories/crvs/documents/egypt.pdf); [African Union EBDRS Egypt](https://au.int/sites/default/files/newsevents/workingdocuments/37474-wd-crvs-v-the_electronic_birth_and_death_registration_system_in_egypt.pdf); [CDC Arab Countries CRVS](https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/data/isp/033_organization_and_status_of_civil_regist_and_vital_stat_in_arab_countries.pdf)

Comments: Equatorial Guinea lacks a national eHealth strategy. WHO SCORE assessment (2013-2018 data) shows low capacity for health service data, with no electronic patient-level data capture in primary care facilities or standardized electronic entry at district level; routine facility reporting scores mostly 0. Bioko Island (not rural mainland) uses DHIS2 for malaria surveillance and digitized patient registries at district hospitals, but field data collection for logistics uses paper forms entered digitally later; transition to Android app ongoing. No evidence of digital patient records (registers, prescriptions) at rural primary clinics; paper-based recording predominant per low HIS capacity and typical sub-Saharan patterns without pilots found.
Source URLs: [WHO SCORE Equatorial Guinea](https://cdn.who.int/media/docs/default-source/documents/ddi/score/country-profiles/who_score_gnq_en.pdf), [Mayo Clinic Digital Health Africa](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10512197/), [DHIS2 Bioko eLMIS](https://dhis2.org/equatorial-guinea-malaria-lmis/)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Equatorial Guinea rely entirely on paper-based recording for administrative data, attendance, and learning outcomes. Schools submit physical or basic info sheets annually for the Anuario (statistical yearbook), compiled centrally by the Ministry of Education with PRODEGE support, indicating manual processes at point of service. No evidence found of computers, tablets, or digital tools for daily recording in rural facilities; rural areas suffer electricity shortages (57% no access per 2011 DHS), poor infrastructure, and low resources (textbooks >3:1 ratio). EMIS exists at national level for aggregation (e.g., Rodriguez 2012 evaluation), but data collection remains paper-driven with follow-ups challenging in dispersed rural zones. Recent diagnostics note need for ICT policies and digital skills but confirm limitations in producing digitally skilled population, with no rural point-of-service digitalisation. Barriers: infrastructure deficits, economic crisis, capacity gaps in MEC, incomplete reporting. National ambitions (ADIGE digital agenda) focus on infrastructure/public platforms, not reaching rural school operations; gap evident as central analytics exist but rural reality is manual.
Source URLs: [World Bank Education Sector Diagnostic (2017)](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/356311499340208347/pdf/Equatorial-Guinea-education-sector-diagnostic.pdf); [World Bank Digital Economy Diagnostic (2024)](https://www.worldbank.org/en/country/equatorialguinea/publication/equatorial-guinea-digital-economy-country-diagnostic-bridging-the-gaps-to-develop-a-safe-and-inclusive-digital-transform); [FHI 360 EMIS report](https://www.fhi360.org/wp-content/uploads/drupal/documents/EQUIP2%20LL%20EMIS%20AAR.pdf)

Comments: Extensive searches across targeted queries ("Equatorial Guinea police case management system", "police records paper digital", etc.), US State Dept 2023 Human Rights Report, UK gov.uk guidance, UNODC/World Bank justice reports, and official govt sites (guineaecuatorialpress.com) yield no evidence of digital case management, incident reporting, or records systems for police or gendarmerie (rural force). Criminal record certificates issued manually via Ministry of Justice; processes described as traditional (complaints filed in person). Digital pilots limited to civil registry (World Bank). In francophone-influenced context, rural stations likely use handwritten procès-verbaux. No pilots/donor projects found for police digitalisation; systemic corruption/limited resources noted. Conforms to paper-based norm for rural African police absent contrary evidence.
Source URLs: [US State Dept 2023 HR Report](https://www.state.gov/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/528267_EQUATORIAL-GUINEA-2023-HUMAN-RIGHTS-REPORT.pdf), [Organized Crime Index](https://africa.ocindex.net/country/equatorial_guinea), [World Bank Digital Economy Diagnostic](https://thedocs.worldbank.org/en/doc/61714f214ed04bcd6e9623ad0e215897-0400012021/related/P1793391f4a4c800219f0014c68aefed3c8.pdf)

Comments: UNICEF CRVS profile (2017 data) states that birth, marriage, and death registration in Equatorial Guinea is processed manually on paper at civil registrar’s offices, mayor’s offices, and local authorities. Registration books are sent to central office and filed on paper, with no vital statistics publications or digital systems mentioned. WHO SCORE assessment (2013-2018 data) shows low CRVS capacity (birth registration completeness 53%), no electronic data exchange from local to central levels, and many core attributes missing. A digital civil registry pilot launched in 2018 and commitments to digitization (e.g., Africa CRVS Day 2023), but no evidence of implementation at rural facilities. Rural registry offices likely remain entirely paper-based, consistent with low national digital infrastructure and centralized structure.
Source URLs: [UNICEF CRVS Profile](https://data.unicef.org/crvs/equatorial-guinea/), [WHO SCORE Equatorial Guinea](https://cdn.who.int/media/docs/default-source/documents/ddi/score/country-profiles/who_score_gnq_en.pdf), [World Bank Digital Economy Diagnostic](https://www.worldbank.org/en/country/equatorialguinea/publication/equatorial-guinea-digital-economy-country-diagnostic-bridging-the-gaps-to-develop-a-safe-and-inclusive-digital-transform)"

Comments: Rural clinics in Eritrea primarily use paper-based recording for patient data at point of care, with aggregate data later entered into DHIS2 for reporting. DHIS2 connects 342 facilities (2018), including rural ones, via offline decentralized entry, but pre-DHIS2 relied on pen-and-paper, and no evidence confirms direct digital patient registration in most rural clinics. Recent tracker program aims at patient-level data, but implementation unclear; computers procured for districts (2020), digital platforms planned for hospitals, not clinics. Barriers: limited internet/power, suggesting reliance on aggregation not PoS digital. National HIS ambition exceeds rural PoS reality, typical of low-resource settings. Score 2 as digital limited to aggregate reporting/pilots.
Source URLs: [WHO Eritrea Biennium Report 2020-2021](https://www.afro.who.int/sites/default/files/2022-07/Who%20Eritrea%20Bienniun%20Report%20WHO%202020%20-%202021_0.pdf); [DHIS2 Community Eritrea](https://community.dhis2.org/t/overcoming-connectivity-barriers-the-role-of-dhis2-platform-in-enhancing-health-data-management-in-eritrea/57621); [Shabait DHIS2 Article](https://shabait.com/2018/11/07/dhis2-improving-data-management-and-health-in-eritrea/)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Eritrea rely on paper-based questionnaires and annual school censuses for recording administrative data, attendance, and learning outcomes, which are collected by Zoba education offices and sent to the Ministry of Education. Questionnaire 7 and 8 cover enrollment data from November each year, with 100% collection rate reported, but no mention of digital tools at school level; process implies manual filling and physical/digital transmission higher up. EMIS exists at national level since World Bank project (2003-2012), used for policy alongside GIS (not integrated), but school-level input remains paper-default per annual stats process. No evidence of routine digital recording (computers/tablets) at rural point-of-service; rural areas lack electricity/infrastructure for ICT, widening digital divide. Barriers: poor electricity/telecom in remote areas, inadequate ICT resources/maintenance, limited teacher training. National EMIS ambition contrasts with rural paper reality; data aggregated upward post-collection, fitting score 2 (predominant paper, digital for aggregate reporting).
Source URLs: [Eritrea Basic Education Statistics 2023-24](https://www.ecss-online.com/2013/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/Basic-Education-Statistics-2023-24.pdf); [UNESCO IICBA Eritrea Brief](https://www.iicba.unesco.org/en/eritrea); [World Bank EMIS Lessons](https://documents.worldbank.org/curated/en/607441491551866327/pdf/114096-PUB-PUBLIC-PUBDATE-4-6-17.pdf)

Comments: Extensive searches across web, academic papers, US State Dept HR reports, UK guidance, UNODC, Shabait (gov news), OSAC security report yield no evidence of digital case management, incident reporting, or records systems in Eritrean police, including rural stations. Recent digital initiatives (customs ASYCUDA, library digitization, police computer training) exist but not for police records. Eritrea's centralized, militarized, low-resource police structure (district stations, manual investigations per Criminal Procedure Code) and lack of mentions in intl reports imply entirely paper-based recording at rural facilities, consistent with most African countries without specific digitalization evidence. No rural-specific contradiction found; score 1 per instructions favoring paper if no digital evidence.

Comments: Rural registry offices in Eritrea lack dedicated civil status officers, resulting in entirely paper-based or non-existent birth registration processes. Births are declared via manual forms from local administrative areas (e.g., district committees), requiring witnesses and later court approval if delayed, then processed at urban municipal offices like Asmara. No evidence of electronic CRVS, DHIS2, or digital point-of-service recording in rural areas; urban areas have some network systems for population registers, but rural facilities do not. Key barriers include resource shortages, lack of policy enforcement, optional registration, low awareness, and no infrastructure for offices in rural zones (80%+ population). National policy under Transitional Civil Code exists but unimplemented rurally; 2025 UN metadata confirms no functional CRVS system countrywide, relying on old DHS surveys. Gap: Urban selective registration vs. rural absence; pilots absent.

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Eswatini predominantly use digital systems for patient data recording via the Client Management Information System (CMIS), an electronic health record (EHR) system. As of the 2024 National Health Sector Strategic Plan, 71% of health facilities have CMIS, with targets to reach 95% by 2028, indicating majority adoption including at primary level. CMIS supports point-of-care data entry for clinical management, reducing duplicates and improving patient flow. A 2020 World Bank project planned rollout to 50 'last-mile' (rural) PHC clinics for real-time RMNCAH/NCD data. Digital job aids on tablets link to CMIS for decision support at PHC. Community-level RHMs recently piloted CBHIS (2026, one region) to replace paper, integrating with CMIS, but clinics separate. No evidence of entirely paper-based rural clinics; partial paper may persist in 29% without CMIS. Barriers: infrastructure, training, connectivity (addressed via APNs). National ambition (full digital) aligns closely with rural reality, unlike many SSA countries.
Source URLs: [Eswatini NHSSP 2024-28](https://www.afro.who.int/sites/default/files/2024-10/Eswatini%20NHSSP%202024%20.pdf); [World Bank Health Project](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/667311591296915504/pdf/Eswatini-Health-System-Strengthening-for-Human-Capital-Development-in-Eswatini-Project.pdf); [World Bank CBHIS Article](https://www.worldbank.org/en/news/feature/2026/02/23/from-paper-to-pixels-driving-the-digital-transformation-of-community-health-services-in-eswatini)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Eswatini traditionally record administrative data (enrollment, attendance via 16th School Day Survey, termly tools), learner profiles, and support needs (OVC, disabilities) using paper forms and registers like Annual Education Census (AEC), which are sent to regional/central levels for manual entry into legacy systems.DHIS2 EMIS Report A DHIS2-based EMIS was piloted in 2021 in Manzini region (100 schools, including rural primaries e.g., Ekuthuleni, Mhlabubovu), enabling school-level digital entry of aggregate and individual PIN-tracked data via web/Android for real-time dashboards. Some rural schools entered data despite challenges, using it for planning (e.g., OVC support, meals).DHIS2 EMIS Report By 2024, MoET's national report draws from DHIS2 data collected from schools, indicating scaling.MoET Report 2024 However, no evidence confirms majority rural facilities routinely use digital at point-of-service; pilot highlighted persistent barriers: unreliable internet (weak signals, high costs), lack of school devices (reliance on personal/borrowed laptops, no tablets for offline), teacher workload/skills gaps, demotivation in rural areas.DHIS2 EMIS Report National policy aims for PIN-driven digital EMIS accessible nationwide, but rural reality remains predominantly paper-based with digital limited to pilots/early donor-supported rollout, not standard point-of-care recording.
Source URLs: [DHIS2 EMIS Assessment Report 2022](https://education.dhis2.org/wp-content/uploads/UiO-DHIS2-Eswatini-EMIS-Report-2022.pdf); [MoET National General Education Report 2024](https://www.gov.sz/images/Educationdocuments/National%20General%20Education%20Report%202024%202_compressed.pdf); [UNESCO Eswatini Education Brief](https://www.iicba.unesco.org/en/eswatini)

Comments: Rural police stations (posts) in Eswatini use paper-based systems like Occurrence Books (OB) for logging incidents, officers on duty, and events; these are operational documents submitted to courts and reviewed in inquests. Recent 2021-2022 leaks confirm handwritten entries at various stations. No evidence of digital recording at facility level; case files ("dockets") are paper-based. Digital initiatives exist only at central level: Taiwan-funded EDRMS project for police (ICDF), planned Criminal Intelligence/AFIS systems (Gov Digital Roadmap), and IECMS integrating police with justice (UNDP). These central systems are comparable to those in other score-1 countries (e.g., Kenya's Digital OB urban pilot, Nigeria's WAPIS) and do not affect rural point-of-service recording. Rural police stations remain entirely paper-based.

Comments: Rural registry offices in Eswatini (e.g., Hlatikhulu, Hluti, Nhlangano) use a hybrid process: informants provide paper letters from chiefs/indvunas confirming births, officers manually fill BMD-1 paper forms, then typists enter data electronically into the National Population Register (NPR) via networked terminals. All district-level civil registration offices countrywide, including rural, have digitized databases with electronic entry of vital events across all CRVS processes (notification to statistics), advanced features like fraud detection and encryption.UNECA CRVS Digitalization ReportEswatini Vital Statistics Report 2022.pdf) No evidence of fully paperless point-of-service (manual forms persist), but digital is standard/default with paper input only. Digital backend integrated with national systems; health facilities linked via CMIS but registration at CRD offices. Low completeness (36% births) due to access barriers, not tech. Plans for further digital roadmap (2024 tender).Eswatini Vital Statistics Report 2022.pdf) Barriers: rural connectivity, training, late registrations (61%). National ambition (CRVS plan 2022-27) pushes integration/mobile units, but rural PoS remains hybrid.UNECA CRVS Digitalization Report

Comments: Rural primary healthcare in Ethiopia operates through ~17,900 health posts staffed by Health Extension Workers (HEWs). The electronic Community Health Information System (eCHIS), introduced in 2018, has been scaled to approximately 8,011 health posts (~45% of total) as of 2024, with over 25 million individuals and 6 million households registered. eCHIS is a tablet-based platform covering modules for family folders, RMNCHN, disease prevention, and NCD management. However, actual utilisation remains low: only ~50% of HEWs at equipped posts actively use it, and just 24.5% offer ANC services through the platform. A 2023 maturity assessment rated ICT infrastructure at 1.67/5, citing inadequate power supply, limited connectivity, and insufficient hardware at health posts. DHIS2 covers all 30,000+ public health facilities for aggregate reporting (95%+ completeness), but data at health posts is still primarily collected on paper then entered at district level. EMR systems exist at only 58 public facilities nationally. Key barriers include unreliable electricity and internet at rural health posts, low digital literacy, heavy workloads creating dual paper-digital reporting burdens, and staff turnover. The scale of eCHIS deployment (~45% of health posts) represents meaningful digital presence at point of service, moving beyond entirely paper-based, though paper remains the predominant method.
Source URLs: [Frontiers eCHIS Implementation 2025](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/digital-health/articles/10.3389/fdgth.2025.1554995/pdf); [DHIS2 Ethiopia](https://dhis2.org/enhancing-healthcare-in-ethiopia-using-dhis2/); [PMC eCHIS Maturity Assessment](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12403253/)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Gabon predominantly use paper-based registers for patient data recording. Multiple studies confirm that health facilities, including clinics, collect data manually in registers, with data managers later aggregating and entering it into national systems like DHIS2 (launched nationally in 2024) or SNIS at district or regional levels. A 2022 JMIR pilot study explicitly states that facilities are compelled to use paper records due to inadequate digital infrastructure, making point-of-care electronic recording challenging. Recent evaluations (2022) show only 45% of structures have digital tools for data collection, with many lacking consultation registers entirely. National e-health initiatives (eGabon/SNIS since 2016, DHIS2 2024) focus on centralized aggregation, not routine point-of-service digital capture in rural areas. Pilots like Routine Info exist but are limited to select facilities and not scaled to most rural clinics. Barriers include poor internet outside cities, fragmented systems, low digital capacity, and medical desertification in rural zones. While national policy pushes digitalization, rural reality remains paper-dominant with digital limited to upward reporting, fitting score 1.

Comments: Rural registry offices in Gabon primarily use paper-based processes for birth registration. Declarations are made to civil registrars within extended timelines (1 month in rural areas per recent law changes), involving manual supplementary judgments from tribunals and transcription into certificates via "One-Stop Centres" in provinces. No evidence found of routine digital recording at point-of-service in rural facilities; processes described as complex administrative/legal without mention of e-CRVS or electronic entry at registries. Digital pilots exist for data collection (e.g., Go-Data app for mapping hard-to-reach populations) and national health info systems are planned, but CRVS remains non-centralized with poor interoperability to HMIS. Barriers include remoteness, low awareness, cultural practices among indigenous groups, coordination gaps, and Covid-19 delays. National policy aims at integration (e.g., registration at health facilities), but rural reality lags with low pickup rates (34%) and focus on late registrations via projects rather than real-time digital point-of-care. High national completeness (~90% in 2012 DHS) masks rural challenges; no specific rural digital evidence post-2012.

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Gambia predominantly use paper registers and structured forms for recording patient data such as antenatal visits, immunizations, and child health. Data is manually aggregated and transcribed into DHIS2 at district or regional levels for national reporting. The Smart Paper Technology (SPT) hybrid system—where paper forms are scanned and digitized post-visit, then synced to DHIS2—has been scaled nationally for the Expanded Programme on Immunization (EPI) and piloted in urban facilities like Bundung Maternal and Child Hospital, enabling mother-child record linkage and improved data quality. However, point-of-care recording remains paper-based even in SPT areas. Limited pilots like TB e-Tracker exist in some rural sites. Key barriers include unreliable electricity, internet connectivity, scanning equipment shortages, and staff training needs, especially in rural areas. While national policy emphasizes HMIS/DHIS2, rural facilities lag with paper as default; digital efforts are donor-driven pilots or aggregation tools, not routine point-of-service use. No evidence majority of rural clinics digitally record data onsite.World Bank PHC Assessment;Nigeria Health Watch SPT

Comments: Rural primary schools in The Gambia predominantly use paper-based recording for administrative data, attendance, and learning outcomes. The 2025 Education Statistics Summary Report indicates only 13.2% of schools (10.5% lower basic/primary) have internet for teaching and 15.6% have pedagogical computers nationally, with urban-rural divides exacerbating low access in rural areas (e.g., Regions 5N/6). EMIS data collection relies on school heads' contributions, likely paper forms aggregated centrally.
