Comments: Rural primary schools in Ghana predominantly use paper-based recording for administrative data, attendance, and learning outcomes, such as manual registers and annual census questionnaires completed by head teachers. These paper forms are forwarded to district and regional offices for data entry and aggregation into the national EMIS, with no routine digital entry at the point of service in most rural facilities.World Bank EMIS Ghana Digital systems like the GES EMIS mobile app for annual census data entry exist and are promoted for teachers, but daily attendance remains paper-based. Pilots such as mSRC app (2014, 20 districts) and GALOP project laptops for weekly attendance uploads represent isolated donor/Gov initiatives, not standard across rural Ghana. Recent studies (2025) show rural headteachers use basic digital tools (spreadsheets 50%, email 50%) inconsistently due to infrastructure gaps (internet, electricity, hardware).GSCARR ICT Study Barriers include poor connectivity, low IT capacity, teacher training deficits, and resource scarcity in rural areas vs. urban. National EMIS ambition contrasts with rural paper reality, where data is transcribed higher up.UEW Record Keeping Thesis
Source URLs: [World Bank EMIS Case Study](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/133081468031776923/pdf/466190NWP0Box310Case0Study0Ghana111.pdf); [GSCARR ICT Adoption Rural Ghana](https://gsconlinepress.com/journals/gscarr/sites/default/files/GSCARR-2025-0350.pdf); [UEW Sagnarigu Record Keeping](https://ir.uew.edu.gh/bitstream/handle/123456789/4484/Record%20keeping%20practices%20of%20primary%20school%20teachers%20in%20Sagnarigu%20district%20of%20northern%20region%20of%20Ghana.pdf?sequence=1&isAllowed=y)

Comments: Rural Ghana police stations predominantly use paper-based records for case files, complaints, and incidents. Key evidence: Local stations record on paper files (easily lost), lack modern technology especially in smaller towns/hinterlands; records physically transported to Accra CID HQ for central database (no remote sync). CDSB receives crime details on paper CID Forms 6/8 from stations. Ongoing central digitization of old records partial (AFIS 2011, but only 3700 fingerprints captured). Recent central services digitized (e.g., clearance 2023), but no evidence of station-level digital case recording. New IPDMS (2025) promoted but appears promotional/pilot, not rolled out to rural. Academic study (2023) confirms manual processes; hinterland data rarely included nationally. Score 1: entirely paper-based at rural facilities, digitized later centrally.
Source URLs: [Criminal records study](https://researchonline.jcu.edu.au/79959/1/79959.pdf), [GPS CDSB](https://police.gov.gh/en/index.php/criminal-data-service-bureau-cdsb/), [JCU study HTML](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/17488958231161429)

Comments: Rural registry offices in Ghana predominantly use paper-based recording for births, with forms filled manually at local points and later aggregated or scanned for electronic transmission to district/regional/central levels, as per traditional structure under Act 301/1027 and UNECA assessment listing Ghana for electronic transmission but not full district-wide digitalization at point-of-service [Journal of Public Health in Africa study]. Digital pilots like m-Birth (UNICEF-supported since 2017) enable some electronic capture via mobile in rural/remote areas, but coverage is limited by infrastructure/connectivity gaps, benefiting urban more than rural, with persistent urban-rural disparities (64-70% rural registration vs 80% urban) [Int J Pediatrics 2025]. Barriers include poor electricity/internet in rural areas, low staffing/resources, geographical access issues, and incomplete rollout beyond pilots/outreach. National ambitions (Act 1027 decentralization/digitalization, CRVS plans) outpace rural reality, where paper remains default with digital for aggregate reporting/pilots [UNECA CRVS Digitalization report].
Source URLs: [Journal of Public Health in Africa (2011 structure review)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5345480/); [Int J Pediatrics (2025 achievements/challenges)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12614733/); [UNECA CRVS Digitalization Africa (2023)](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/Digitalization%20of%20Civil%20Registration%20and%20Vital%20Statistics%20in%20Africa.pdf)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Guinea predominantly use paper-based systems for patient data recording, including registers and forms for routine care and initial surveillance reporting. Aggregate weekly disease data and individual case notifications are compiled on paper at clinics/health centers and sent to District Health Offices for digital entry into DHIS2, which is used nationally for surveillance and routine monthly reporting since 2016.Implementation of DHIS2 for Disease Surveillance in Guinea. No evidence of widespread point-of-care digital systems like EMRs in government rural clinics; Swiss Re Foundation notes pre-project reliance on paper.Swiss Re Foundation on Clinic+O. Digital pilots exist, e.g., Clinic+O NGO deploys offline EMRs and dashboards in select rural CHCs/community outreach, integrating with national systems via manual uploads; successful but limited scale.Oxford Open Digital Health study. Barriers: poor connectivity/electricity, staff shortages/training gaps, infrastructure lacks. National policy pushes DHIS2 post-Ebola, but rural point-of-service remains paper; digital for district aggregation only. Score 2 reflects paper default with isolated donor pilots, not majority routine use.
Source URLs: [DHIS2 Guinea Surveillance (Frontiers in Public Health)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8811041/); [Clinic+O Rural Guinea Digital Health (Swiss Re Foundation)](https://www.swissrefoundation.org/what-we-do/projects/access-to-health/Clinic-O--Strengthening-community-based-primary-healthcare-in-rural-Guinea.html); [Digital Tool Feasibility Study (Oxford Open Digital Health)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11932417/)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Guinea use paper-based registers for recording administrative data, attendance, and learning outcomes. National EMIS exists with data aggregated by the National Institute of Statistics into yearbooks, but collection at rural facilities remains entirely manual, later transcribed at higher levels. A World Bank project (BOSIB, closed 2024) piloted tablets for ~25,000 primary teachers and mobile data entry, but failed to achieve its targets: 0% of schools achieved digital data integration or dashboard usage. The GIGA/UNICEF connectivity project (2025-2026) aims to connect 2,200 schools but is prospective. Only 35% of primary schools have electricity. No evidence of any functioning digital recording system at rural school level; the failed pilot with 0% adoption does not constitute digital presence at point of service.
Source URLs: [UNESCO IICBA](https://www.iicba.unesco.org/en/guinea); [World Bank BOSIB](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099021025115512289/pdf/BOSIB-e98530da-4262-4114-a3b8-9e4daa06717a.pdf); [Ecofin GIGA](https://www.ecofinagency.com/news-digital/0711-50249-guinea-kicks-off-plan-to-bring-internet-to-2-200-schools)

Comments: Rural police stations in Guinea (Police Nationale/Gendarmerie) record case files, complaints, and incidents using handwritten paper-based procès-verbaux (PVs), as per standard francophone African practice and Guinea's Code de Procédure Pénale. The 2018 MSPC Police Statistical Yearbook reports 8,843 PVs drawn up nationwide (including rural CCPs/DRPs like N'Zérékoré, Faranah), based on manual registers/fiches; rural challenges include poor archiving, no computers or vehicles. The Innovatrics ABIS in Conakry digitises paper criminal records/fingerprints centrally, with regions sending paper for central processing — this does not constitute digital recording at rural facility level. US State Dept HR reports (2023/2024) describe manual detentions with no digital recording at stations. No rural-specific digital evidence found; entirely paper-based at point of service.
Source URLs: [MSPC Annuaire Statistique 2018](https://www.stat-guinee.org/images/Documents/Publications/SSN/mspc/MSPC_ANNUAIRE_2018.pdf); [Innovatrics Guinea Police Digitisation](https://www.innovatrics.com/resources/digitalization-of-police-investigation-in-west-africa/); [US State Dept 2024 HR Report](https://www.state.gov/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/624521_GUINEA-2024-HUMAN-RIGHTS-REPORT.pdf)

Comments: Rural registry offices in Guinea primarily use paper-based systems for birth registration. Vital events like births occurring at home are first recorded in paper "village notebooks" by local sector chiefs and then forwarded to the head of the rural commune (mayor or delegate), who serves as the official registrar and issues certificates manually (UNICEF Profile; UNECA Snapshot). Processing is explicitly "manually (on paper)." Limited digital elements exist in isolated pilots and some urban/commune centers: a 2017 modernization pilot in rural N’zerekore region introduced computers (standalone, not networked); an EU-backed project (2021-2024) in 10 municipalities digitizes existing paper records into a national database post-recording; ~40 centers acquiring computers with local backups planned. No evidence of digital tools at point-of-service in most rural facilities or networked systems for routine use (UNECA Snapshot; Enabel Project). Key barriers: poor accessibility (centers often >5km from villages), untrained volunteer staff, lack of electricity/connectivity, large backlog (>25% unregistered births). National CRVS Reform Strategy (2018-2022) aims at modernization/DHIS2 linkage, but rural point-of-service remains paper-dominant, with digital limited to aggregation/pilots—not shifting majority to digital recording.
Source URLs: [UNICEF Birth Registration Profile](https://fln.dk/media/0n3fpx2w/guin205.pdf); [UNECA CRVS Snapshot Guinea](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/CRVS_GuineeSnapshot_e.pdf); [Enabel Modernisation Pilot](https://citizenshiprightsafrica.org/the-modernisation-of-guineas-civil-registry-inspired-by-belgiums-experience/)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Guinea-Bissau predominantly use paper-based recording for patient data. The 2016 Health Sector Diagnostic states: "The majority of health centers don’t have automated process. All data is registered on forms and books, with a logical sequence and order; however, there is not a preservation mechanisms or control of the quality of registration." The 2019 World Bank Service Delivery Indicators (SDI) Health report notes "non-operational health-information systems" and relies on manual outpatient records for caseloads, with no mentions of digital tools at point-of-care in surveyed facilities (62% rural). No evidence found of routine electronic health records (EHR) or DHIS2 use at rural clinics for patient registration; DHIS2 is national-level for aggregate reporting/surveillance since ~2018, with data likely paper-collected then transcribed at district level. Isolated NGO pilots (e.g., DREAM HIV software, Clinic+O community digital tools in Guinea—not Bissau) exist but do not represent standard practice. Key barriers: poor infrastructure (45% facilities have basic electricity/water/sanitation), lack of computers/internet in rural areas, insufficient trained staff, political instability. National ambitions include DHIS2 expansion and recent platforms (e.g., NIA 2026), but rural point-of-service remains paper-based, with large gap between policy and reality.
Source URLs: [World Bank SDI Health Report 2019](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/294721561652896187/pdf/Guinea-Bissau-Service-Delivery-Indicators-Report-Health.pdf); [Guinea-Bissau Health Sector Diagnostic 2016](https://p4h.world/app/uploads/2023/02/GNB20-20Health20Sector20Diagnostic2020162020FINAL.x73677.pdf); [DHIS2.org Guinea-Bissau](https://dhis2.org/guinea-bissau-covid-surveillance/)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Guinea-Bissau operate under severe constraints: pupil-teacher ratio of 51.9:1, only 25% of primary schools offer full 6 grades (mostly urban), frequent teacher strikes, low infrastructure in rural areas (60% population rural). No evidence of digital recording of attendance, enrollment, grades at school level. Recent World Bank/GPE projects introduced electronic surveys to principals (GEMS-based) and Kobo Toolbox for inspectors in 5 targeted regions—pilots for aggregate stats/monitoring, not routine school data. No operational EMIS; historical absence of regular assessments. Data collection likely paper-based at rural facilities, aggregated centrally if at all. Score 1: entirely paper-based, consistent with low-income African rural norms absent specific digital evidence. (148 words)
Source URLs: [UNESCO IICBA Education Brief](https://www.iicba.unesco.org/en/guinea-bissau), [World Bank Quality Education ISR](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099021524143518031/pdf/P16067811d42bd0401beaf17918a568ea80.pdf), [EPDC National Education Profile](https://www.epdc.org/sites/default/files/documents/EPDC_NEP_2018_Guinea-Bissau.pdf)

Comments: No direct evidence on rural police stations, but justice sector shows early digital pilots (e.g., SIGDAJ for MoJ data aggregation, Judiciary Police judicial forum website, online complaint submission on pj.gw with case tracking Polícia Judiciária). UNDP E-Justice (2023) notes shift from paper-based methods, targeting regional decentralization, implying predominantly paper at rural/outlying stations with central digital entry/pilots. UNODC goCASE (2014) limited to urban TCU. Model station in rural Gabú (2024 UNODC/UNDP) focuses on community policing infrastructure, no records tech mentioned. Lacks evidence of predominant digital at station level; fits score 2 per rules (pilots/donor projects).
Source URLs: [UNDP E-Justice](https://www.undp.org/guinea-bissau/blog/e-justice-guinea-bissau-going-digital-can-improve-access-justice), [Polícia Judiciária GW](https://pj.gw), [UN Gabu Model Station](https://www.un.org/peacebuilding/content/Improving-community-trust-and-security-guinea-bissau%E2%80%99s-gabu-model-police-station), [UNODC goCASE](https://gocase.unodc.org/gocase/events/news141211.html)

Comments: Rural registry offices in Guinea-Bissau rely entirely on paper-based recording for births and vital events. UNICEF data confirms civil registration processing is manual (on paper), with new services at health facilities but no digital tools at point of service [UNICEF CRVS]. Low rural birth registration completeness (17.5% in 2014 MICS per World Bank, likely similar recently) reflects access barriers, not digitalization [World Bank rural data]. A national SIREC digital system began in 2025 for digitizing records and some birth registrations, supported by UNDP/UNICEF, with efforts to extend to remote areas and health facilities [Biometric Update]. However, as a recent pilot/early rollout (started ~3 months prior to June 2025), it represents isolated digital efforts, not standard at rural registries. Key barriers: poor infrastructure, low literacy, remoteness, funding shortages. National ambition for digital CRVS exists, but rural point-of-service remains paper-based with data later aggregated digitally if at all. No evidence majority rural facilities use digital routinely.
Source URLs: [UNICEF CRVS Guinea-Bissau](https://data.unicef.org/crvs/guinea-bissau/); [World Bank birth registration rural](https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/SP.REG.BRTH.RU.ZS?locations=GW); [Biometric Update SIREC](https://www.biometricupdate.com/202506/guinea-bissau-deploys-efforts-to-digitize-birth-registration-national-id-systems)"

Comments: Rural primary clinics in Kenya predominantly use paper registers for patient data recording, with dual documentation alongside digital systems where available. EMRs like KenyaEMR (KeEMR) cover ~91-93% of facilities including rural level 2/3 dispensaries and health centers (e.g., Homa Bay County survey of 112 facilities, 79% rural), but are mainly for HIV management, donor-driven, and fragmented (9-10 systems co-existing). General care relies on paper; data aggregated monthly into DHIS2 by HRIOs, not entered digitally at POC routinely. Pilots like ScanForm (AI paper digitization, 24% facilities) and AfyaEHMS exist but isolated. Barriers: poor internet/power, interoperability lack, training gaps, staff turnover. National policies (Digital Health Act 2023, eHealth Strategy) aim for integrated systems, but rural reality lags due to infra disparities, uneven county implementation—some manual, policy ambition unmet at POC (Homa Bay study; Policy Brief; DHIS2 perceptions).
Source URLs: [Homa Bay digital health study](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12165431/); [KNCHR Policy Brief on digitalisation](https://www.humanrights.dk/files/media/document/Policy-Brief---Digitalisation-of-Health-Services-in-Kenya-final-26-03-2025-a.pdf); [DHIS2 data use study](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12352824/)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Kenya predominantly use paper registers for recording administrative data, attendance, and learning outcomes at the point of service. National systems like NEMIS/KEMIS exist for aggregate reporting, but rural schools lack the infrastructure (electricity, internet, computers) and trained staff for routine digital entry—data is likely collected manually and submitted to district offices for digitization. Pilots and donor projects provide isolated digital tools (e.g., solar labs), but these do not represent the majority. Key barriers include poor connectivity, unreliable power, low teacher digital literacy, and high student-teacher ratios. National policies push EMIS adoption, but rural reality lags far behind, with studies showing low ICT readiness even in 2020s. Score 2 reflects predominant paper with some digital aggregation/pilots.
Source URLs: [World Bank ICT in Education Kenya](https://openknowledge.worldbank.org/bitstreams/ac6eed42-e1c7-5106-83be-69700a10dbc9/download); [ERIC ICT rural secondary Kenya](https://files.eric.ed.gov/fulltext/EJ1071353.pdf); [EdTechHub Computerizing Rural Primary](https://docs.edtechhub.org/lib/NMZU3HHJ); [Nyanza EMIS study](https://rsisinternational.org/journals/ijriss/articles/exploring-the-impact-of-technology-on-educational-management-information-systems-in-nyanza-county-kenya-a-comprehensive-analysis/)

Comments: Rural police stations in Kenya predominantly use paper-based systems for recording criminal cases and complaints, including manual Occurrence Books (OB), P3 medical forms, and paper dockets. These are later aggregated or digitized at higher levels, as evidenced by World Bank digitization of 17,000 paper crash reports from Nairobi stations. Digital initiatives like Digital OB (piloted/launched in urban stations such as Kasarani 2019 and Eldoret 2026) and NPSIMS (unified platform presented 2025) exist, but rollout depends on connectivity assessments, explicitly noting challenges in rural areas. A 2021 SRIC pilot digitized arms registers (not cases) in rural ASAL counties (Baringo, Samburu, etc.), confirming manual records pre-pilot. Recent plans (2025 digital services by April, online reporting) remain aspirational with infrastructure gaps (power, internet, training). No evidence majority rural facilities use digital at point-of-service; isolated pilots indicate score 1 (entirely paper), conservatively, due to thin rural-specific evidence amid urban-focused rollouts.
Source URLs: [National Police Service](http://nationalpolice.go.ke/ig-kanja-receives-comprehensive-presentation-national-police-service-information-management-system); [World Bank eCrash Report](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099812409142212391/pdf/IDU0fa12afc501d270442e0a7b7039186f51bfab.pdf); [SRIC Arms Digitisation Pilot](https://www.reinvent-kenya.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/01/SRIC-DIGITISATION-OF-POLICE-ARMS.pdf)"

Comments: In rural Kenya, birth registration primarily occurs at sub-locations manned by Assistant Chiefs (10,666 total) or rural health facilities via paper Form B1, completed manually in duplicate. The informant receives the original notification slip, while duplicates are batched and sent to Civil Registration Offices (CROs) for verification and entry into registers. Rural residents often travel 5-10 km (1-4 hours on foot) to access these points. Post-collection, sub-county registrars create Excel summaries from paper registers and transmit electronically to national CRS, but point-of-service remains paper-based. Digital efforts include: CRVSS piloted in some counties (not scaled due to resources); historical digitization of 46M records; IPRS for national linkage; hospital digital pilots (e.g., Estonia-supported e-birth registration, 2025—not rural-focused); small-scale mobile pilots (e.g., WHO MoVE-IT, Finnish/Nokia 2008-2012). Low rural completeness (e.g., ASAL counties <20%) reflects access barriers, insecurity, and lack of digital tools at RAs. Barriers: poor connectivity/electricity, low capacity among rural agents, backlogs, distance. National ambitions (e.g., 2018-2023 strategy, eCitizen integration) outpace rural rollout, where paper dominates point-of-service with digital limited to pilots/aggregation. Score 2: Predominantly paper; digital in isolated pilots/central reporting.UNECA CRVS Kenya Snapshot; Kenya Vital Statistics Report 2022; UNECA Digitalization CRVS Africa
Source URLs: [UNECA CRVS Kenya Snapshot](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/CRVS_KenyaSnapshot_e.pdf); [Kenya Vital Statistics Report 2022](https://www.knbs.or.ke/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/2022-Kenya-Vital-Statistics-Report.pdf); [UNECA Digitalization CRVS Africa](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/Digitalization%20of%20Civil%20Registration%20and%20Vital%20Statistics%20in%20Africa.pdf)

Comments: In rural primary healthcare clinics (health centers), patient data recording is predominantly paper-based for general care. Non-HIV/TB patients use paper health booklets, with data manually aggregated into national DHIS2 at higher levels MCC Blog 2023. For HIV/TB/PMTCT/cervical screening/pharmacy, eRegister EMR is deployed nationwide at all 170 government health centers (many rural) using decentralized local servers to handle poor connectivity/power; data auto-syncs to DHIS2 Oxford Digital Health 2024. Some duplicate paper processes persist. Digital systems are donor-driven (PEPFAR/ICAP) for specific diseases, not standard point-of-care for majority routine care. Pilots like village health worker tablets (BKM) exist but community-level. Barriers: unreliable power (outages, failing solar), low connectivity, technical capacity reliant on partners. MCC compact (2024+) plans expansion to all diseases, but currently not predominant at most rural clinics for all recording. Gap: National ambitions (Digital Health Strategy 2025-30 validated 2024) and HIV digital success contrast with paper-dominant routine rural PoC.
Source URLs: [MCC Blog 2023](https://www.mcc.gov/blog/entry/blog-121123-digital-health-lesotho/); [Oxford Open Digital Health 2024](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11932418/); [DHIS2 Community 2022](https://community.dhis2.org/t/improving-dhis2-integration-for-lesothos-health-information-ecosystem/47463)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Lesotho record administrative data, enrollment, attendance, and other metrics using paper-based Annual Statistical Census (ASC) forms filled by principals and submitted via district officers to the central Education Planning Unit.2023 Education Statistics Report This process remains manual at the point of service, with data later captured into central OpenEMIS for aggregated annual reporting, not real-time individual tracking.KIX EMIS Peer Review A 2017-2018 OpenEMIS pilot aimed at learner unit records was scaled back due to resource constraints, including lack of district statisticians, equipment, and internet.World Bank ICT Survey Only ~10/1477 primary schools have rudimentary ICT access (~20/1700 have electricity), severely limiting rural facilities (70% of population).KIX EMIS Peer Review Plans to shift to DHIS2 for Education emphasize district-level roles but no evidence of school-level digital rollout by 2023-2026. Key barriers: poor rural infrastructure, no electricity/internet, unqualified teachers (51% in mountains), funding gaps. National EMIS exists, but rural reality is entirely paper-based recording with central digitization—gap reflects ambition vs resource reality. (198 words)
Source URLs: [Lesotho Bureau of Statistics 2023 Education Report](http://www.bos.gov.ls/New%20Folder/Copy%20of%20Demography/2023_Education_Report.pdf); [GPE KIX EMIS Peer Review](https://www.gpekix.org/sites/default/files/webform/submit_to_the_library/460/Policy%20Brief_KIX%20EMIS%20Peer%20Review_cdv1.pdf); [World Bank ICT in Education Lesotho](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/865541468266696519/pdf/463840BRI0Box31ho010ICTed0Survey111.pdf)

Comments: Lesotho Mounted Police Service (LMPS) uses paper-based records like Occurrence Books (OB) for disciplinary cases, complaints registers, custody logs, and dockets at stations, including rural ones lacking electricity/telephones.APCOF Report Evidence of digital efforts: Integrated Case Management System (ICMS) login portal (v0.1.1), LMPS_RIMS project (2019+), GoLehokela digital policing platform. Courts use Synergy CMS integrating police data, but operational at limited urban sites (Maseru). No rural-specific digital rollout; rural stations rely on paper with later district entry possible. Predominantly paper with some digital pilots/urban systems = score 2.
Source URLs: [APCOF LMPS Assessment](https://apcof.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/05/The-Police-As-Friend-and-Helper-to-the-People-Assesing-the-Lesotho-Mounted-Police-Service-Perfomance-in-terms-of-the-SARPCCO-Code-of-Conduct-1.pdf), [LMPS ICMS](https://keshop.dedicated.co.za/ICMS/), [Synergy CMS Lesotho](https://www.capterra.co.za/software/43128/synergy-case-management-system), [LMPS RIMS LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/sekake-lenka-a7a8b0190_lesotho-mounted-police-service-record-information-activity-7178391503185076224-uSJu), [GoLehokela](https://golehokela.gov.ls)"

Comments: Rural registry offices and civil registration points in Lesotho predominantly use paper-based forms for birth registration, with records later digitized and transferred electronically to regional/national databases. UNECA APAI-CRVS Report (2023) confirms electronic transfer from local offices but Lesotho not among countries with universal district-level electronic entry. Automated digital systems for point-of-service electronic recording and immediate certification piloted in ~4 hospitals (since 2021) and select rural community councils (e.g., Qanya CC in Qacha’s Nek, 2022), supported by UNICEF. Lesotho Ministry of Home Affairs; Gov Lesotho. National CRVS Strategic Plan (2020-2024) promotes decentralization to chiefs/councils and digitalisation, with 2023 Ntlafatsa Bana system and 2024-2026 DPI commitments, but 2025 UNICEF consultancy reviews incomplete scale-up. Barriers: Rural infrastructure gaps (electricity/connectivity in 70% mountainous terrain), low digital literacy, staffing shortages. Gap: National electronic aggregation ambition vs. paper-default at most ~10,000 rural points; pilots cover minority of facilities, fitting score 2.
Source URLs: [UNECA APAI-CRVS Report](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/Digitalization%20of%20Civil%20Registration%20and%20Vital%20Statistics%20in%20Africa.pdf); [Lesotho Home Affairs Qanya CRVS](https://www.homeaffairs.gov.ls/commemoration-of-africa-civil-registration-and-vital-statistics-crvs-day-at-qanya-community-council-in-qachas-nek/); [Gov Lesotho Automated Launch](https://www.gov.ls/uncategorized/his-majesty-launches-automsted-birth-registration-system/)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Liberia predominantly use paper-based registers and ledgers for recording patient data, such as OPD, ANC, immunization, and inpatient registers (average 21-24 per facility). Data is aggregated monthly onto paper HMIS forms, verified, and submitted to county health teams, where it is entered into DHIS2 for analysis—no digital entry or EMR at the point of service in rural clinics.MEASURE Evaluation PRISM assessment 2014 Isolated digital pilots exist, e.g., OpenMRS EMR rollout by Partners In Health at J.J. Dossen Hospital (rural, Maryland County) in 2020, replacing paper records there, with plans for expansion, but limited to partner-supported sites.Partners In Health Recent sources (2026 FB post) note most facilities still rely on paper records. Key barriers: poor infrastructure (internet/power/connectivity), low staff skills for digital tools, funding shortages, post-Ebola fragility. National ambitions include DHIS2 at county level and digital transformation plans (e.g., 2022-2027 agenda), but rural point-of-care remains paper-based with digital only for aggregate reporting—wide gap between policy and rural reality.
Source URLs: [MEASURE Evaluation PRISM assessment (2014)](https://www.measureevaluation.org/our-work/routine-health-information-systems/rhis-curriculum-modules/handout-10-3.1.pdf); [Partners In Health OpenMRS rollout (2020)](https://www.pih.org/article/pih-rolls-out-electronic-medical-records-jj-dossen-hospital-liberia); [ISO Liberia LBR](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_3166-1_alpha-3)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Liberia predominantly use paper-based registers for daily administrative data, attendance, and learning records. National EMIS transitioned from paper-based 1.0 to planned digital 2.0, but data collection stalled post-2022 census due to funding shortages; no new school census data since.Liberia EMIS Peer Review Report Weak ICT infrastructure (limited hardware, high internet costs, unreliable electricity) severely limits point-of-service digital use in rural areas.Liberia EMIS Peer Review Report Recent initiatives include Mar 2026 Geo-Mapping M&E system for real-time monitoring (attendance, instruction) with geolocated inputs from schools (pilot incl. some rural counties like Rivercess, Bong), and planned mobile/SMS/ODK tools for low-connectivity.FrontPageAfrica Rural network expansion (128 sites covering 100+ schools by 2024) improves access but insufficient for routine digital recording.ZTE Report Barriers: funding dependency, staff capacity, utilities/transport. National ambition for EMIS 2.0 contrasts with rural paper reality; pilots indicate score 2, no evidence of majority digital at rural POSC.

Comments: Rural police stations in Liberia record criminal cases and complaints using paper files and dockets. A January 2026 report on Nyehn Town Police Station in rural Todee District describes no computers available; officers prioritize protecting paper documents from rain over duties, indicating entirely manual processes amid dilapidated infrastructure lacking basic logistics.FrontPageAfrica An EGAP study on rural community policing (2010s) notes historically low formal reporting (2% of crimes to police), relying on administrative data without digital mention.EGAP Digital systems exist at national/urban levels: UNDP-supported CSIS (2021, Montserrado urban pilot) uses tablets for biodata/GPS input and online aggregation, replacing paper stats reporting.UNDP CSIS CMIS/DHIS2 (2022 rollout to police) offers offline mobile app for case stats with GPS, improving data integrity, but no evidence of routine rural point-of-service use; training focused on county-level staff.UNDP CMIS Barriers: poor infrastructure/electricity, low connectivity, training gaps, resource scarcity in rural areas. Score 1: entirely paper at rural facilities; digital limited to urban pilots.

Comments: Rural registry offices and civil registration points in Liberia predominantly use paper-based forms for birth registrations. The 15 county-level registration centers are located in urban districts, serving rural households located 5-10 km away (often requiring 5+ hours travel by foot). Completeness remains low at <25% as of 2018.UNECA CRVS Snapshot No computers in local registration or health facilities; paper records are standard, with data potentially aggregated digitally at county centers. Digital initiatives are limited to pilots and donor projects: The Mobile Birth Registration (MBR) pilot (2009-2012) used Nokia phones for data collection by district health officers in one rural county (Bomi), supplementing paper processes to enable certificate printing; it demonstrated feasibility but faced scale-up barriers and remained isolated.VTT ReportWHO eCRVS Review Recent UNICEF efforts (2024) integrate birth registration in 98% health districts using tablets/online systems linked to DHIS2 for digital certificates, but challenges like unreliable electricity, internet, resources, and decentralized accountability persist, indicating non-standardized use.FrontPageAfrica/UNICEF Key barriers: poor infrastructure/power, low capacity, post-conflict fragility. National plans (2016-2020) envision expansion and online rollout, but rural point-of-service remains paper-default with digital only in isolated health-linked pilots—not majority routine use. Score 2 fits: predominantly paper with some digital pilots.
Source URLs: [UNECA CRVS Snapshot](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/CRVS_LiberiaSnapshot_e.pdf); [VTT MBR Review](https://sarjaweb.vtt.fi/pdf/workingpapers/2011/W159.pdf); [WHO eCRVS Review](https://cdn.who.int/media/docs/default-source/documents/ddi/systematic-review-of-ecrvs-and-mcrvs-interventions-in-low-and-middle-income-countries.pdf?sfvrsn=987de19d_2)

Comments: Rural primary clinics in Libya record patient data using paper-based systems, with aggregated data collated manually and reported upwards to district/national levels. No evidence of electronic medical records (EMR) or digital patient registration at point-of-service in rural facilities. A 2016 readiness study of five rural clinics found no e-health technologies in use and IT equipment not utilized for clinical purposes, rating maturity at level zero.University of Brighton Thesis. Recent assessments confirm national medical records remain paper-based, with no interoperable electronic health records.BMC Primary Care Assessment. DHIS2 adopted nationally in 2018 for monitoring/evaluation, piloted in limited PHC centers (e.g., 6 sites) and rolled out for aggregate reporting/surveillance, but not integrated into clinical care or point-of-care patient records; interviewees noted it is "too far removed from clinical care."WHO EMRO DHIS2 Training; WHO HIS 2017. Isolated donor projects like Speetar "Health Dots" e-clinics target rural south but remain pilots. Key barriers: protracted conflict damaging infrastructure (20% PHC facilities inaccessible/closed), electricity/internet shortages, lack of ICT/training, fragmented governance. National policy (2030 Vision, 2025-30 digital roadmap) aspires to digital HIS/electronic records, but rural reality lags with paper default and digital limited to upward aggregation/pilots—not PoS standard.
Source URLs: [WHO HIS Assessment 2017](https://applications.emro.who.int/docs/9789290224075-eng.pdf); [BMC Primary Care 2024](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11167861/); [Brighton e-Health Thesis 2016](https://research.brighton.ac.uk/en/studentTheses/the-development-and-implementation-of-e-health-services-for-the-l/)

Comments: Libya's Ministry of Education launched EMIS in July 2024 with UNICEF support, piloting in 205 institutions nationwide using online surveys for data on students, staff, etc. This indicates aggregate digital collection, likely from paper records at rural primary schools (attendance, enrollment, grades) entered centrally after pilot training. No evidence of direct digital recording at rural facilities; pre-2024 reports note lack of functional EMIS and reliance on annual stats reports implying paper-based. Recent nationwide pilot suggests some digital aggregate reporting but predominantly paper at source, fitting score 2. Rural inclusion via "all over the country" but no rural-specific digital tools found. Conflict/disruption hinders full rollout.
Source URLs: [Libya Observer EMIS launch](https://libyaobserver.ly/education/education-ministry-launches-emis-cooperation-unicef), [OpenEMIS Libya portal](https://ly-moe.openemis.org/core/), [Education Profiles Libya technology](https://education-profiles.org/northern-africa-and-western-asia/libya/~technology)

Comments: Libya's rural police stations record case files, complaints, and incident reports using paper-based procedures as per the 1953 Code of Criminal Procedure (still applicable). Judicial officers (police) draw up "reports" on incidents, seizures in sealed pouches, notifications, and monthly statistics submitted physically to prosecution; no mention of digital tools at station level. In remote areas, police delegated for felony investigations but refer to prosecution—implies paper transmission. No evidence found of digital case management systems deployed to rural stations; limited digital training for prosecutors/cybercrime urban-focused. Post-2011 instability fragmented policing; rural areas rely on local/customary resolution bypassing formal digital systems. General paper-based practice nationwide, score 1 per rules.
Source URLs: [DCAF Libya Criminal Justice System](https://security-legislation.ly/libyan-criminal-justice-system-lcjs/evidence-gathering/), [DCAF LCJS Overview](https://security-legislation.ly/libyan-criminal-justice-system-lcjs/), [USIP Policing Libya](https://www.usip.org/sites/default/files/PW117-Policing-Libya.pdf)

Comments: Libya's Civil Registration Authority (CRA) operates 12 branches, 133 issuing offices, and 400 service offices covering all Libya, including rural areas, all fully automated and electronically linked via a single database since at least 2020, enabling high-quality timely data (100% birth registration, >90% deaths) Seha.ly 2020 report. UNFPA assessment shows computers used in compilation (79%), storage (68%), validation (63%), transmission (58%), with adequate peripheral infrastructure UNFPA Arab CRVS. Older UN paper (~1980s) describes paper triplicate forms at local level with central computerization UNStats, but recent sources confirm shift to digital. Recent mentions of electronic civil registry phases. Predominantly digital at rural facilities, some legacy paper possible.
Source URLs: [Seha Vital Statistics 2020](https://seha.ly/media/statistics_reports/vital-statistics-report-for-the-year-2020mD5xx9G.pdf), [UNFPA CRVS Arab Countries](https://arabstates.unfpa.org/sites/default/files/pub-pdf/crvs_13_web_version.pdf), [UNStats IIVRS Libya](https://unstats.un.org/unsd/demographic-social/crvs/documents/IIVRS_papers/IIVRS_paper47.pdf)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics (Centres de Santé de Base, CSB) in Madagascar predominantly use paper-based registers for patient data recording, including consultations, vaccinations, prenatal care, and dispensing. Monthly activity reports (RMA) are compiled manually at CSB and aggregated/entered into DHIS2 at district level (SDSP), not at point-of-service.Plan Stratégique de Renforcement du Système d'Information Sanitaire 2023-2027 Electronic RMA piloted in 10 districts (e.g., Manjakandriana, Manakara) since 2021, allowing direct DHIS2 entry at some CSB, but limited to aggregate data, not individual patient records; scaling planned but not majority coverage.Plan Stratégique de Renforcement du Système d'Information Sanitaire 2023-2027 CommCare app enables digital community health data entry by volunteers (4,300+ users), integrated to national DHIS2 for reporting to CSB heads, replacing paper treks, but clinic-level patient recording remains paper-based.Management Sciences for Health Key barriers: poor network (58% CSB with 4G), energy shortages, low functionality (CSB1: 37% functional), equipment shortages. National ambitions include DHIS2 scaling, electronic registers, patient management systems by 2027, but rural reality lags—digital for surveillance/aggregates/pilots, paper default at clinics.Plan Stratégique de Renforcement du Système d'Information Sanitaire 2023-2027
Source URLs: [Plan Stratégique MSANP](https://www.msanp.gov.mg/upload/documents/PSRSIS_2023-2027.pdf); [MSH CommCare](https://msh.org/story/mobile-app-technology-improves-health-delivery-in-rural-madagascar/); [DHIS2 Madagascar](https://dhis2.org/madagascar-hisp-uio-one-health-initiative/)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Madagascar predominantly rely on paper-based registers for recording attendance, administrative data, and learning outcomes. The national Education Management Information System (EMIS) uses annual school censuses with questionnaires (levels 1-3 for public schools) likely completed manually at facilities and aggregated centrally via a data warehouse and SDMX standards, as per recent PAPESI project enhancements focusing on ministry-level capacity and infrastructure—not point-of-service digital tools in rural areas UNESCO IIEP; UNESCO EMIS. Isolated donor-funded pilots provide solar-powered tablets for teaching content in ~15-20 rural schools (e.g., Solar United Madagascar, SEED projects 2024-2026), but these target learning delivery, not administrative data recording, and represent a tiny fraction of facilities SEED Madagascar. No evidence of majority rural digital adoption. Key barriers: electricity/internet deficits (only 8% rural communes connected pre-2010) World Bank ICT, untrained teachers (many FRAM parent-hired), poor infrastructure. National EMIS ambitions contrast sharply with rural paper-dominant reality.
Source URLs: [UNESCO IIEP](https://www.iiep.unesco.org/en/articles/madagascar-educational-planners-embrace-future); [UNESCO EMIS](https://emis.uis.unesco.org/madagascar/); [World Bank ICT Survey](https://documents.worldbank.org/curated/en/293081468271510875/pdf/463800BRI0Box31ar010ICTed0Survey111.pdf)

Comments: Rural police stations in Madagascar, primarily managed by the National Gendarmerie, rely on entirely paper-based recording for criminal cases and complaints. APT guidelines mandate paper custody registers (registres de garde à vue) at all stations, including rural ones, as a core procedural guarantee.APT Guide No evidence found of routine digital case management at point-of-service in rural facilities. Digital efforts are limited to central-level initiatives like a planned UNDP-supported software for judicial file tracking in police technique/scientific police (2025 tender),UNGM UNDP and laptop donations to gendarmerie for basic admin (2025).MFT Justice sector has LOGIPENAL etc. for courts since 2018, but not extended to rural police recording.E-Governance Report Key barriers: low rural electricity (15%), internet access (~2% rural women), digital literacy gaps, ad hoc training, poor infrastructure, paper archives.Cybersecurity Review; Daily Maverick. National digital ambitions (PRODIGY ID system, e-gov) focus on civil registry/services, not police case dockets; rural point-of-service remains manual with later aggregation if any.
Source URLs: [APT Guide](https://www.apt.ch/sites/default/files/publications/Garde%20a%20vue%20a%20Madagascar%20-Web%20version.pdf); [UNDP Tender](https://www.ungm.org/Public/Notice/269304); [Cybersecurity Capacity Review](https://gcscc.ox.ac.uk/files/cmmrapportfinalcybersecuritemadagascarpdf)"

Comments: Rural registry offices (municipalities/fokontany) in Madagascar primarily record births using paper forms and maintain manual registers locally, with no centralized national digital repository.SSA POMS The CRVS system is inoperative nationally and functional only in urban areas like Antananarivo, where registers exist but are digitized post-collection; rural facilities lack comparable systems and resources.PMC Study Digital initiatives like the World Bank PRODIGY project plan hybrid analog-digital processes with offline apps for CR offices, as two-thirds lack stable internet, but remain in design/pilot phase (e.g., 2023 Toamasina trial, urban focus).World Bank OIF supports registration drives (65k targeted 2024-25), but no evidence of routine digital use at rural point-of-service. Barriers include connectivity, understaffing, remoteness. National policy aims e-CRVS and biometric ID, but rural reality is predominantly paper-based with low completeness.

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Malawi predominantly use paper-based systems for patient data recording at point-of-care. Health Passports (HPs), patient-held paper booklets, are updated manually during consultations with symptoms, diagnoses, treatments, and plans; they serve as the primary medical record since most rural facilities do not maintain outpatient files on-site. Post-consultation, data clerks transcribe from HPs into paper facility registers (e.g., OPD), which are aggregated monthly and entered into DHIS2 at district level—not at the facility POC. Digital initiatives like openSRP e-register (tablet-based EHR) are limited to pilots (e.g., Bilira Health Centre since ~2021, covering thousands of patients but one site) and community tools like iCHIS (3 districts). EHRs are mainly in urban/central hospitals. Key barriers: poor handwriting/abbreviations in HPs, low literacy, resource shortages (power/internet), high workload, no standardization/training. National policy (e.g., Digital Health Strategy) aims for digital HIS, DHIS2 mobile allows some facility entry (41% facilities use digital tech), but rural POC remains paper-default with digital for aggregate reporting only—gap between ambition and reality.
Source URLs: [BMC Health Services Research 2025](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12135469/); [Malawi MoHP Annual Report 2023-24](https://health.gov.mw/storage/resources/Annual%20Report%202023-24%20FY_FINAL_06122024docx%201.pdf); [GIZ Bilira Pilot 2021](https://health.bmz.de/stories/linking-rural-health-centres-up-with-malawis-digital-health-architecture/)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Malawi predominantly use paper-based registers for recording administrative data, attendance, and learning outcomes. Key documents include class registers (daily attendance marked manually, 92.6% update frequency), school registers for progress, lists for grades passed/failed, test scores, and absenteeism—all paper. Complementary records (e.g., behavior, personal data) are poorly maintained. EMIS data is collected via annual school census and monthly stats on paper forms, aggregated and sent physically to districts for digital entry, not captured digitally at school level.UMass Study Rural-urban gap: rural schools update class registers less frequently than urban due to poorer supervision and facilities; no electricity/telephones in most rural schools.UMass Study Digital pilots exist nationally (e.g., BEFIT tablets for learning in 5,770 primary schools, oneICT lessons in 1.9%, internet in 2.5%), but not for admin/attendance recording; rural electrification ~4%, internet access ~6%.EdTech Hub Plans for rural school connectivity (Connect a School, 2025-2026) focus on labs/internet, not point-of-service digital records.WB Digital Malawi Barriers: lack of electricity/devices/skills, poor infrastructure. National EMIS/HRMIS strengthened for aggregation, but rural point-of-service remains paper-based, with no evidence of majority digital adoption.WB MESIP
Source URLs: [UMass 2005 Study](https://scholarworks.umass.edu/server/api/core/bitstreams/f283600b-b66d-4f2b-af77-03eb96d8e064/content); [EdTech Hub 2024](https://docs.edtechhub.org/lib/NMJMENAG/download/DQBIS3ET); [World Bank MESIP](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/134201651499886937/pdf/Malawi-Education-Sector-Improvement-Project.pdf)"

Comments: Rural police stations in Malawi primarily use paper-based systems for recording criminal cases and complaints. Cases are entered manually in Occurrence Books (OB), custody registers (MP33), cell books, rapid response registers, and case files (MP71), which are physically moved between officers for investigation and prosecution. A 2019 National Audit Office performance audit of sampled stations (including rural Mchinji and Karonga) found no operational digital case recording, with issues like poor categorization and backlogs. A 2024 peer-reviewed study in rural Muloza (Mangochi District) states police have "no ICT resources deployed for the prevention, detection, and investigation of crime," relying on personal mobile phones informally; no digital case management. National efforts like AFIS fingerprints (centralized, failed rollout), e-Court pilots (4 urban stations), and ICT donations target headquarters or pilots, not rural point-of-service. Barriers include inadequate rural ICT infrastructure (fiber urban-concentrated per 2013 National ICT Policy), maintenance challenges, low resources, and poor connectivity. National digital ambitions (e.g., drone training, cyber units) contrast sharply with rural paper reality, where mobiles aid reporting but core docket recording remains manual.
Source URLs: [NAO Performance Audit Report](https://www.nao.gov.mw/index.php/en/documents/audit-reports/category/30-performance-audit-reports?download=54:performance-audit-report-on-handling-and-followup-of-crime-cases-in-malawi); [SciencePG Rural ICT Study](https://www.sciencepg.com/article/10.11648/j.ajasr.20241002.11); [ITU Malawi ICT Policy](https://www.itu.int/en/ITU-D/Cybersecurity/Documents/National_Strategies_Repository/Malawi_2013_Malawi_ICT_Policy.pdf)"

Comments: Rural registry offices and civil registration points in Malawi predominantly use paper-based processes for birth registration. The standard procedure involves informants completing the NR8 paper Birth Report Form at village heads or health facilities, verified by village head signature, with paper acknowledgement slips issued and certificates printed later at district offices or facilities with printers NRB Birth Registration. Isolated digital pilots exist, such as small-scale electronic village registers (EVR) in select rural villages without electricity (2013-2018) and recent Traditional Authority offices pilots reviewed in 2026, but these are not scaled nationwide PMC EVR. Nationally, the NRIS digital platform supports CRVS with mobile apps like Nzika for field registration (biometrics, offline), backed by UNDP IDT4M and World Bank Digital Malawi projects, enabling some digital point-of-service in equipped areas UNDP IDT4M. However, official procedures remain paper-default, with digital limited to donor-supported pilots and upward data entry. Barriers include poor rural electricity/connectivity (addressed partially by offline tools), infrastructure gaps, and enforcement issues. National ambitions for digital CRVS (e.g., 2025-2030 NRB Strategic Plan) outpace rural reality, where paper persists as default at most point-of-service facilities. (198 words)
Source URLs: [NRB](https://www.nrb.gov.mw/birth/); [UNDP IDT4M Portfolio](https://www.undp.org/sites/g/files/zskgke326/files/2026-02/undp_idt4m_supported_digital_systems_portfolio_v7_2701026.pdf); [PMC CRVS Assessment](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4576763/)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics (CSComs) in Mali predominantly use paper-based registers and forms for patient data recording at the point of service. Community health workers (ASCs) compile paper monthly reports from service registers and deliver them in person to CSComs, where staff review and enter aggregated data into the national DHIS2 system.Health Data Collaborative Mopti case study DHIS2 has been national since 2016, covering most districts, but relies on manual paper inputs from facilities, with data entry at facility/district level, not point-of-care.Gavi ZDLH Digital pilots include DISC-Mali smartphone app for CHWs (launched 2023, scale to 25k by 2025, replacing their paper reports)Muso and electronic vaccination registries in 2 districts. Logistics eLMIS piloted in some regions.DHIS2 Key barriers: rural electricity access (16% in 2020), poor internet, insecurity, paper shortages. National digital ambitions (DHIS2, DISC) exist, but rural CSCom patient recording remains paper-default, with digital limited to pilots and aggregation. No evidence of majority digital POS use.
Source URLs: [Health Data Collaborative](https://www.healthdatacollaborative.org/fileadmin/uploads/hdc/Documents/Working_Groups/RHIS/B._Country-specific_documentation__case_studies__best_practices_/Community_HIS_country_case_studies/Case_studies/CS_Health_Data_Reporting_Challenges_in_Mali.pdf); [Gavi Zero-Dose Landscape](https://zdlh.gavi.org/country-profiles/mali); [Muso DISC-Mali](https://www.musohealth.org/post/steering-mali-s-community-health-plan-digitalizing-and-strengthening-primary-health-care)"

Comments: Rural primary schools in Mali predominantly use paper-based recording for attendance, enrollment, grades, and admin data. A 2024 study on Malian schools (basic education level) found 90% of administrative tasks, including student enrollment in paper registers or Excel, student dossiers as physical files, teacher records in registers, are manual. No evidence of digital recording at school level; EMIS exists at national level with paper collection from schools then centralized entry (ADEA peer review 2017 notes resource shortages, delays). Recent digital platforms (e.g., web-based for registration/attendance) appear urban/pilot-focused; rural schools lack infrastructure (no networks, electricity issues). World Bank/GPE projects emphasize access/kits, not school digital records. Score 1: entirely paper-based at rural facility level.

Comments: Mali's 2024 Code de Procédure Pénale requires Officers of Judicial Police (OPJs) at stations to draft procès-verbaux (PVs) immediately on the spot, signed on each page by OPJ and witnesses, with originals transmitted directly to the Prosecutor. Physical registers (coté et paraphé) are mandated for custody logs. No mention of digital drafting or electronic signatures for initial recording. Monthly reports on case files ("dossiers") are sent, but initial capture is paper-based. Recent digital initiatives (e.g., SIG-DJ 2025 for judicial dossier management, WAPIS 2015 for data centralization, online casier judiciaire 2026) focus on higher-level justice systems, converting existing paper data or central storage, not station-level recording. US State Dept 2023 report notes poor record-keeping but no digital at police level; rural areas face same issues plus access challenges. In francophone Africa, rural police rely on handwritten PVs; no rural-specific digital pilots found. Thus, rural police stations record case files, complaints, incidents on paper.

Comments: Rural registry offices and village declaration points in Mali primarily use paper-based forms for birth registration, with parents submitting notifications from health facilities or home births to local council centers. High national coverage (90%) achieved through extensive network (8000 points), community agents, free services. Digital CRVS software exists nationally (commercial off-the-shelf), but no evidence of routine electronic entry at most rural points-of-service; likely paper collected and later digitized/aggregated. Plans to expand digital processes, digitize records, leverage digital tools for remote areas indicate emerging digital elements/pilots/donor efforts. Key barriers: poor rural connectivity/electricity, insecurity, vast territory. National CRVS strategy ambitious (2018-2022+), but rural reality lags with paper predominant at point-of-service. Disparities noted in remote/conflict areas.
