Health posts submit paper summaries to centers, where aggregate data is entered into DHIS2 for national reporting—no electronic medical records (EMR) or point-of-care digital systems at rural facilities.World Bank PHC Assessment (2019-2022) Digital systems like DHIS2 are used for upward aggregate reporting from district level, but not patient-level recording at clinics.BMC Health Services Research (2021) Recent national Digital Health Strategy (2023+) aims for shared records and facility digitization, but remains aspirational with no evidence of majority rural adoption by 2026; pilots if any are isolated.Digital Watch Observatory Key barriers: poor rural infrastructure (electricity, internet), limited training/staff, vast desert geography, nomadic populations. Gap: National policy emphasizes HMIS aggregation; rural point-of-service lags, relying on manual processes despite ambitions.
Source URLs: [World Bank PHC Assessment](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099121523194016438/pdf/P1598650398a4706d09a340d1a66200239a.pdf); [BMC RDHA Study](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12004592/); [Digital Health Strategy Summary](https://dig.watch/resource/digital-health-strategy-for-mauritania)

Comments: Mauritania operates a national EMIS called SIRAGE, supported by World Bank projects, operational across 72 decentralized units and used as the single tool for sector data collection including enrollment and transition rates.World Bank BESSP Phase 2 Annual Statistical Yearbook produced from EMIS data.UNESCO IICBA Country Brief No evidence of digital recording at rural primary school level for attendance, enrollment, grades; rural areas lack infrastructure. Standard EMIS practice involves paper collection at schools, digital aggregation at district level. Recent digital teaching pilots (2024-25) do not indicate facility-level data digitalisation. Score 2: Predominantly paper with digital aggregate reporting.
Source URLs: [World Bank BESSP](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099102225151016957/pdf/P163143-1376a5b5-8550-428b-b0c6-cfdfbfdc24d0.pdf), [UNESCO IICBA](https://www.iicba.unesco.org/en/mauritania), [Gov Digital School](https://mtnima.gov.mr/fr/transformation-numerique-sectorielle-lecole-numerique-au-service-de-la-reforme-du-systeme-educatif/)

Comments: Rural police stations in Mauritania, primarily served by the Gendarmerie Nationale, record case files, complaints, and incident reports on paper. UK FCDO prisoner pack describes arrest statements as handwritten "procès-verbal" in local language at basic stations lacking electricity/office equipment and with low literacy UK GOV Prisoner Pack. US State Dept 2022 HR report notes UN HRC finding of poorly maintained police detainee records, indicating manual systems State Dept HR Report. No evidence of digital case management at station level; justice sector digitisation efforts (e.g., Morocco coop 2025, govt reform) are central/court-focused, not rural police. Official gendarmerie site shows no digital tools for records Gendarmerie MR. Francophone context supports paper-based "procès-verbaux" standard.
Source URLs: [UK FCDO Prisoner Pack](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/mauritania-prisoner-pack--2/mauritania-prisoner-pack), [US State Dept 2022 HR Report](https://www.state.gov/wp-content/uploads/2023/02/415610_MAURITANIA-2022-HUMAN-RIGHTS-REPORT.pdf), [Gendarmerie Nationale Website](https://www.gendarmerie.mr/fr)

Comments: Mauritania's civil registration is managed by ANRPTS through Citizens’ Reception Centres (CAC) across the country, including rural areas. UNICEF CRVS profile (2017) states birth registration processing is "Electronically," but requires paper extracts from health facilities for registration at local CACs within 60 days. No evidence of direct digital recording at rural points; likely paper declarations entered digitally centrally, consistent with many African CRVS where local sites use paper and aggregate later. Recent digital ID pilots (e-ID, Houwiyeti app for verification) indicate national digitization push but not facility-level recording. Low completeness rates (World Bank data) and rural connectivity challenges (UNDP) support predominantly paper at rural registries with central digital processing. Score 2: predominantly paper locally, some digital aggregation/pilots.
Source URLs: [UNICEF CRVS Mauritania](https://data.unicef.org/crvs/mauritania/), [FamilySearch Mauritania Civil Registration](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Mauritania_Civil_Registration), [UNDP Mauritania digital ID](https://www.undp.org/blog/mauritania-pilots-digital-id-app), [ANRPTS Houwiyeti](https://anrpts.gov.mr/en/verify)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics (Area Health Centres, Medi-Clinics, Community Health Centres) in Mauritius predominantly use paper-based recording for patient data. Government audits confirm failed implementations of digital systems like IPCS at these facilities, with hardware unused and records kept manually as of 2016.Mauritius Audit Report WHO's 2015 report notes no EMR/EHR at primary level, reliant on paper forms.WHO He@lth 2015 As of Feb 2025, the system remains mostly paper-based per UNDP roadmap, with the 'One Patient, One Record' PAS/EHR project in early rollout: piloting at one regional hospital, planned extension to health centers/mediclinics/community centers (Phase 1), but not yet standard at point of service.UNDP Roadmap No evidence of majority rural adoption; represents donor/govt project akin to score 2. Barriers: IT infrastructure gaps, staff training, business process re-engineering, past system failures. National ambition (eHealth strategy) outpaces rural reality, where paper persists for daily recording, with possible aggregate reporting digital.
Source URLs: [Mauritius Government Audit 2016](http://download.govmu.org/files/2016/COMPUTERISATION%20OF%20PATIENT%20HEALTH%20RECORDS.pdf); [WHO He@lth 2015](https://cdn.who.int/media/docs/default-source/digital-health-documents/global-observatory-on-digital-health/mauritius_health2015.pdf?sfvrsn=24a9d1f6_3); [UNDP Digital Health Roadmap Feb 2025](https://digitalhealthfordevelopment.undp.org/upload/documents/Roadmap_for_Digital_Health_Transformation_in_Mauritius.pdf)

Comments: Mauritius has deployed OpenEMIS for education data management at national, zonal, and school levels, with UNESCO capacity building in 2022 following 2019 needs assessment and SIMIS design. All primary schools have high-speed internet (pre-2020). Government publishes detailed digital enrollment data by grade/gender. Recent AI tools like mytGPT piloted in primary schools for real-time monitoring. Digital Mauritius 2030 and reforms aim for full sector digitalisation, including student tracking. No evidence of paper-based recording at rural primaries; EMIS implies direct digital entry at schools. Rodrigues (rural) included in pilots/data. As upper-middle income, predominantly digital with possible legacy paper in remote areas.
Source URLs: [UNESCO IITE OpenEMIS Mauritius](https://iite.unesco.org/news/openemis-capacity-building-in-mauritius/), [UNESCO EMIS Support Mauritius](https://iite.unesco.org/news/mauritius-emis-support-and-monitoring-of-education-2030-agenda/), [GovMU Primary Enrollment Data](https://data.govmu.org/dataset/enrolment-in-primary-schools-by-year-grade-and-gender-for-the-republic-of-mauritius_2019-2020), [Education Profiles Mauritius Tech](https://education-profiles.org/sub-saharan-africa/mauritius/~technology)

Comments: Mauritius Police Force (MPF) maintains a centralized digital Criminal Attribute Database (CADB) at Crime Records Office (CRO) for convictions and fingerprints, with 12,457 electronic fingerprint submissions from 7 remote workstations including Rodrigues (rural island) in 2023-24 MPF Annual Report 2023-2024. Police IT Unit handles digital forensics (2,034 exams), CCTV integration via Safe City/PMCCC, and network support across force MPF Annual Report 2023-2024. Certificate of Character (criminal record check) available online via portal, processed at DHQs including rural areas police.govmu.org. However, case dockets (PF forms) and outstanding cases (91k crimes) suggest initial paper recording at 72 stations (many rural/peripheral), with data uploaded centrally. No evidence of full digital case/incident logging at rural stations; hybrid model predominant: paper-based locally, digital aggregates/pilots centrally. GIS/CCTV limited to urban (Port Louis/Grand Bay). Score 2 fits predominantly paper with some digital (central reporting, pilots).
Source URLs: [MPF Annual Report 2023-2024](https://police.govmu.org/police/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/Annual-Report-2023-2024.pdf), [Police IT Unit](https://police.govmu.org/police/?page_id=5779), [Certificate of Character Portal](https://police.govmu.org/police/?page_id=7617)

Comments: Rural registry offices in Mauritius likely rely predominantly on paper-based processes for birth registration, with parents appearing at district Civil Status Offices to submit documents (hospital attestation, IDs, etc.) and sign a physical register, as per the official government process description with no mention of on-site digital entry.Civil Status Division A 2012 UNECA report notes 47 total offices, 32 fully computerized for instant digital entry, and 15 non-computerized using manual entry forms transferred to computerized sites for data entry—non-computerized offices are plausibly the more remote/rural ones given Mauritius' small size and centralized structure.UNECA CRVS Report Data reaches central electronic Central Population Database and Statistics Mauritius monthly in digital format, indicating aggregation rather than point-of-service digitalisation. No recent rural-specific evidence found of predominant digital shift; online access exists for certificates via Mokloud, but not registration. Mauritius has strong overall CRVS (complete per older sources), but rural facilities appear to use paper as default with digital support centrally. Key barriers: legacy non-computerized remote offices, potential infrastructure limits. National policy emphasizes digital gov (e.g., Digital Mauritius 2030), but rural point-of-service lags, fitting score 2 (pilots/digital in some, paper predominant).
Source URLs: [Civil Status Division](https://csd.govmu.org/Pages/Registration/Birth.aspx); [UNECA Mauritius CRVS Report (2012)](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/Country%20Report%20-%20Mauritius_Eng_0.pdf); [WHO SCORE Mauritius](https://cdn.who.int/media/docs/default-source/documents/ddi/score/country-profiles/who_score_mus_en.pdf)

Comments: Morocco's national HMIS development (WB ~2017) notes fragmented systems with paper-based data collection at service delivery level, including rural primary facilities like clinics and dispensaries, with aggregate entry higher up. WHO EMRO eHealth profile indicates no national EHR, N/A EHR usage in primary care facilities (clinics/health centers). Recent EHR in urban hospitals (e.g., HOSIX since 2009, BMC 2025 study), slow expansion to primary/secondary (120M USD plan), pilots like digital prescriptions (2026). No evidence of facility-level digital patient records/registers in rural clinics; HMIS likely aggregate reporting. Digitalisation predominantly paper at rural clinics with emerging pilots/digital aggregate systems.
Source URLs: [WHO EMRO eHealth Profile](https://www.emro.who.int/images/stories/ehealth/documents/morocco-ehealth-country-profile.pdf), [World Bank HMIS Report](https://openknowledge.worldbank.org/bitstreams/a43405ca-e6f6-5e6d-bcc2-c6d90b8fcc53/download), [BMC EHR Study](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11869421/)

Comments: Morocco has a national EMIS called "Pathway," as noted in UNESCO UIS EMIS Typology report. MCC projects provide technical assistance to improve EMIS capabilities nationwide, including student assessments (Mathematica MCC project). Recent initiatives like Orange Foundation's "Écoles numériques" equip 55 rural primary schools with digital tools and content, training teachers, but this is a pilot (Medias24). No direct evidence of routine digital recording at rural schools for attendance/enrollment/grades; typical for EMIS in developing countries to involve paper collection at schools with later digital aggregation at higher levels. Recent digital academy and AI programs indicate growing digital infrastructure, but rural primary facilities likely predominantly paper-based with some pilots/projects. Score 2.
Source URLs: [UNESCO UIS EMIS Typology](https://emis.uis.unesco.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/5/2024/02/IP65-EMIS-Typology-FINAL_Upload.pdf), [Mathematica MCC Morocco Education](https://www.mathematica.org/projects/morocco-secondary-education-activity-for-employability-project), [Medias24 Écoles Numériques](https://medias24.com/2024/09/06/55-nouvelles-ecoles-rurales-beneficieront-du-programme-ecoles-numeriques-de-la-fondation-orange-maroc/)"

Comments: Morocco's police (DGSN) and gendarmerie are digitizing case management. DGSN's GESTARR system with integrated crime/delinquency management and procedure digitization is deployed in urban prefectures, regional sûretés (e.g., rural Ouarzazate, Errachidia, Taza), and provincial sûretés, with national rollout planned Mafrique, AllAfrica. Royal Gendarmerie uses HID Livescan for digital biometric booking, replacing outdated manual processes, usable at remote outposts YouTube. Justice sector advancing with online criminal records Justice.gov.ma. No evidence of entirely digital initial recording at all rural stations; ongoing pilots/rollout in some rural areas indicates predominant paper with growing digital systems (score 2). No mentions of paper-only in recent sources; HR reports lack specifics on recording methods.
Source URLs: [DGSN Bilan via Mafrique](https://mafrique.ma/2024/12/27/maroc-plateforme-numerique-de-services-les-solutions-technologiques-au-service-du-citoyen-dgsn/), [AllAfrica DGSN Report](https://fr.allafrica.com/stories/202412250141.html), [HID Gendarmerie System](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7Piuv1z3lpM), [Casier Judiciaire Portal](https://casierjudiciaire.justice.gov.ma/Accueil.aspx?culture=fr-FR)

Comments: Rural registry offices in Morocco (1282 principal rural offices out of ~2200 total) primarily use paper-based handwritten ledgers for birth registration, with no connected computer systems in the majority as of 2018 World Bank ID4D Diagnostic. Citizens obtain certified paper copies from local offices. Aggregate data is reported upward manually. In 2025, a national project (194M MAD) launched to digitize 38M historical records across all 1821 offices, including rural via regional lots (e.g., southern rural regions), involving manual entry of paper records and connecting offices to a unified system Morocco World News. Law 36.21 (2023) enables e-pre-declarations, but point-of-service recording remains paper-default. No evidence majority rural facilities use digital at point of care; project indicates ongoing transition from paper. Barriers: poor rural IT infrastructure, decentralization. National ambition (Digital Morocco 2030) outpaces rural reality, with digitization as upward reporting/pilot-like effort.
Source URLs: [World Bank ID4D Diagnostic](https://id4d.worldbank.org/sites/default/files/2018-04/Morocco_ID4D_Diagnostic_Web404018.pdf); [Morocco World News](https://www.moroccoworldnews.com/2025/04/192844/morocco-launches-19-4-million-digital-transformation-of-civil-registry/); [Atalayar](https://www.atalayar.com/en/articulo/new-technologies-innovation/morocco-digitises-its-civil-registry-with-historic-investment-of-194-million-dollars/20250423140207213825.html)

Comments: Rural primary clinics in Mozambique predominantly use paper-based systems for patient data recording, including handwritten registers, patient-held medical cards, and manual logs later aggregated for national DHIS2 at district levels. No evidence of routine electronic entry at point-of-service in most rural facilities; digital remains limited to pilots and national aggregation. Digital systems: National DHIS2 HMIS for aggregates; pilots like DHIS2 Tracker app for immunization in 5/11 provinces (2024, 19k children); ECR platform developing for facilities; 2024 hospital pilots to reduce paper. Barriers: Rural electricity/connectivity shortages, low digital skills, infrastructure gaps (2025 Digital Health Strategy plans rural pilots with renewables/satellites). Policy ambition (PESS, Digital Strategy) targets EMR/RES by 2030, but rural PoS lags behind—paper default, digital for reporting only. Conservative score 1; pilots hint at 2 potential. (198 words)
Source URLs: [MoH Digital Health Strategy](https://sisma.misau.gov.mz/docs/Estrat%C3%A9gia%20de%20Sa%C3%BAde%20Digital.pdf); [DHIS2 Immunization Pilot](https://dhis2.org/zero-dose-mozambique/); [Hospital Digital Pilot](https://clubofmozambique.com/news/mozambique-launches-cost-saving-hospital-digitalization-project/); [Patient Records Study](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8197044/); [Facility Readiness](https://www.joghr.org/article/89000-structural-readiness-of-health-facilities-in-mozambique-how-is-mozambique-positioned-to-deliver-nutrition-specific-interventions-to-women-and-childre)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Mozambique primarily use paper-based methods for recording administrative data, attendance, and learning outcomes. Core EMIS data is collected via hand-filled standardized paper questionnaires during semi-annual school censuses (enrolment in March, results in December), covering student numbers, repeaters, teachers, infrastructure, and resources. These forms are forwarded through districts to provinces for collation and centralized digitization in Maputo—no digital entry at school level. Attendance and marks are tracked in handwritten 'livro de turma' class registers, forming the basis for semester/annual paper school reports on staffing, finances, and pedagogy, which have limited integration with EMIS. No evidence found of routine electronic recording at point-of-service in rural schools. Digital pilots exist, e.g., Carta Escolar (GIS for school mapping/planning, piloted in some provinces but not fully functional or used for attendance/learning data); limited ICT initiatives focus on secondary/urban schools or learning (e.g., SchoolNet, NEPAD e-Schools demos), not admin recording. Key barriers: lack of electricity, computers, internet in rural areas; untrained staff; unreliable infrastructure; focus on basic operations. National EMIS provides aggregate planning data, but rural reality remains paper-dominant, with digitization only at higher levels—wide gap between policy ambitions and rural implementation. (198 words)
Source URLs: [World Bank EMIS Case Study](https://www.scribd.com/document/62143392/Working-Paper-03-EMIS-Case-Study-Mozambique); [UNESCO IICBA Mozambique Brief](https://www.iicba.unesco.org/en/mozambique); [World Bank ICT in Education Mozambique](https://openknowledge.worldbank.org/bitstreams/b9e8c797-12ef-5e67-a98c-8829c5362ced/download)"

Comments: Rural police stations in Mozambique (PRM) use entirely paper-based systems for case files, complaints, and incident reports (e.g., Auto de Notícia forms). A 2014 study of 40 investigators in rural Nampula province found manual information systems in use, with no IT despite staff capability.UFMG Thesis A 2024 example from Rapale district (rural Nampula) shows typed paper-style incident report digitized as PDF.Scribd PRM Form Justice sector digitalization efforts (UNODC seminar 2025) face rural tech access barriers; no police-wide digital case management. Central records manual (fingerprints). Under-resourcing noted.Dullah Omar Score 1: paper-based at station level.
Source URLs: [UFMG Thesis](https://bdtd.ibict.br/vufind/Record/UFMG_6a5efb7c2abc54c82cd13340c46ca514), [UNODC Digitalization](https://www.unodc.org/rosaf/stories/2025/February/digitalizing-mozambiques-legal-and-judicial-sector.html), [PRM Auto de Notícia](https://www.scribd.com/document/845072165/Auto-de-Noticia-01)"

Comments: Rural civil registration posts (345 administrative posts) predominantly use paper forms for recording births, which are then transmitted (via mobile notifications or electronic media) to district conservatories for digitization in the eCRVS system.UNECA CRVS Snapshot Local agents fill forms at point-of-service; districts enter electronically, with data synced centrally.CRVS Webinar Mozambique Digital systems rolled out to all 68 conservatories and ~120 posts (2018, no rural split), plus 156 hospital posts (2024) and experimental offline modules for conflict zones.Child Identity Presentation Under-5 birth registration at 32% (2023 DHS), indicating gaps. Barriers: poor rural internet/electricity, conflict/cyclones damaging infrastructure, low capacity/training.World Bank Blog National SIRCEV plan (2019-29) targets 80%, with donor pilots (UNICEF/WB), but rural point-of-care remains paper-default with digital aggregation.
Source URLs: [UNECA CRVS Snapshot](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/CRVS_MozambiqueSnapshot_e.pdf); [Child Identity Presentation](https://www.child-identity.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/Arnaldo-ENglish-Reuniao-de-Lanc%C2%B8amento-Regional-do-Quadro-de-Programa-Estrate%C2%B4gico-para-o-Fortalecimento-do-RCEV-ENGL-1-002-en-GB.pdf); [CRVS Webinar](https://crvssystems.ca/sites/default/files/assets/images/CRVS_Webinar_Mozambique_e_WEB.pdf)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Namibia predominantly use paper-based patient medical cards and health passports for recording patient data at the point of service. Nurses manually record data, which is later aggregated and entered into national DHIS2 systems at district or regional levels, not directly at clinics. No evidence of widespread EMR/EHR use in rural clinics; systems remain fragmented and vertical (e.g., HIV/TB-specific). Recent 2025 studies confirm manual data collection due to connectivity issues, equipment shortages, and poor internet in rural areas. National eHealth Strategy (2021-2025) highlights paper dominance and rural connectivity gaps (many facilities without reliable internet despite targets). New National Digital Health Policy (2026-2036, launched Feb 2026) aims to digitize records and expand telehealth to rural areas, but implementation is nascent. Barriers: low rural smartphone ownership (11.8%), intermittent power/network, vast geography/low density, workforce shortages. Gap: National ambitions for UHC via digital tools contrast with rural paper reality; pilots absent or isolated, no majority adoption.
Source URLs: [Namibia National eHealth Strategy 2021-2025](https://extranet.who.int/countryplanningcycles/sites/default/files/public_file_rep/NAM_Namibia_National-eHealth-strategy_2021-2025.pdf); [Health system constraints in cervical cancer prevention rural Namibia](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12421780/); [Health information systems in Namibia](https://www.kab.ac.ug/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/10-1108_ILS-03-2018-0015_Health-Information-systems-in-Namibia.pdf)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Namibia predominantly use paper-based registers for recording administrative data, attendance, and learning outcomes, which are collected twice yearly by education regions and processed centrally by the EMIS division for national reports like the Annual Education Census and School Day Reports. While a national EMIS database exists for aggregation and planning, evidence indicates paper remains the default at point-of-service in rural areas, with data likely submitted via physical forms to regional offices before digital entry. Digital pilots exist, such as solar electrification of ~15 rural schools enabling computers and internet, and proposals for automated attendance systems, but these are isolated donor projects (e.g., GPE, Grüne Bürgerenergie). Key barriers include lack of electricity (180+ unelectrified rural schools), poor connectivity (16% rural mobile internet access), low device ownership, teacher training gaps, and affordability. Recent 2025 reports show ongoing manual census processes with strict form submission deadlines to circuits/regions. National ambitions for digital transformation (e.g., N$145M rural connectivity fund, Youth Coding) lag in rural reality, where infrastructure deficits keep most facilities paper-reliant with digital only for upward reporting. Score 2 reflects predominant paper with some digital pilots, no evidence of majority routine POS digital use.
Source URLs: [Ministry of Education EMIS](https://www.meiysac.gov.na/index.php/dep/dep-fa/dir-pad/emis); [15th School Day Report 2025](https://www.meiysac.gov.na/index.php/downloads/24-resources/29-emis-reports?download=122%3A15th-school-day-report-2025); [APC Digital Rights Report](https://cadeproject.org/updates/apc-report-highlights-digital-rights-challenges-in-namibia-ahead-of-upr-review/); [Electrification Rural Schools](https://energypedia.info/wiki/Electrification_of_Rural_Primary_Schools_in_Namibia); [UNESCO EMIS Best Practices](https://media.unesco.org/sites/default/files/webform/ed3002/AT1GP29_Building_a_Functional_Education_Management_Information_System.pdf)"

Comments: Namibia Police Force (NAMPOL) maintains paper-based initial recording at stations, including manual station visits, affidavits, Occurrence Books (OB), A1 statement forms, books/registers/Pol forms per Customer Service Charter (register cases "on e-policing system and manually"). Info first manually recorded with physical records permanent, then entered electronically (2012 NAMPOL). E-Policing (since 2014) stores case dockets digitally (central), biometric N-ABIS (2016), but station-level procedures indicate predominant paper with digital aggregation. Rural stations follow same standards but slower response; no evidence of full digital at rural outposts, likely paper-dominant given manuals and forms. Score 2: predominantly paper with digital backend/pilots.
Source URLs: [NAMPOL Customer Service Charter](https://www.nampol.gov.na/documents/3978963/6322183/STATIONS+CSC.pdf/0bee6ea9-477f-1239-c69c-908d6a38f679?t=1750952854889), [Canada IRB Namibia Police Procedures](https://www.ecoi.net/de/dokument/2011346.html), [UK FCDO Rape Guide Namibia](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/namibia-information-for-survivors-of-rape-and-sexual-assault/information-for-survivors-of-rape-and-sexual-assault-in-namibia), [NAMPOL About Us](https://nampol.gov.na/about-us1), [Namibian Newspaper e-Policing](https://www.namibian.com.na/police-lauch-automated-biometric-identification-system/)"

Comments: Rural registry offices in Namibia (sub-regional Category II offices, serving remote thinly populated areas) are part of 62 decentralized registration centers connected to the National Population Registration System (NPRS), enabling real-time electronic recording of births and deaths at point of service. Certificates are issued on the spot from NPRS. E-birth/death notifications (since 2017/2018) integrate health facilities (153 total incl. private), feeding directly into NPRS. Birth registration completeness reached 87% nationally (2018-19), with 61% at health facilities, supported by mobile registrations in regions. Digital systems: NPRS (2011), M-files for historical scanned records (2010). No evidence of routine paper recording at facilities; processes digitized with forms supporting electronic entry. Barriers: Some sub-regional offices lack full NPRS connectivity or have slow networks, official reluctance to new systems; rural internet gaps persist (e.g., Zambezi region complaints 2025-26). National policy achieved high decentralization/digital coverage despite sparsity (2nd least dense after Mongolia), but occasional connectivity issues mean some paper fallback possible—hence predominantly digital (score 3), not entirely (4). Gap: Policy ambition met with strong implementation, though infrastructure challenges remain in most remote rural spots.
Source URLs: [UN CRVSID Namibia Case Study](https://unstats.un.org/legal-identity-agenda/documents/Paper/2023/CRVSID-Namibia.pdf); [Ministry of Home Affairs Civil Registration](https://mha.gov.na/civil-registration); [Namibia Vital Statistics Report 2018-2021](https://nsa.org.na/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/2018-2021_Namibia-Vital-Statistics-Report_April-2024.pdf)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics (health centres/health posts) in Niger predominantly use paper-based recording for patient data, with nurses handling basic services like immunization, maternal/child care, and curative care. No evidence of routine electronic health records or DHIS2 at point of care; DHIS2 functions as national HMIS for aggregate data management and monitoring, likely from facility paper tallies transcribed at district level. Digital systems piloted in isolated projects: OpenClinic HIS at select district hospitals, computerised maintenance in 3 rural districts (management-focused, not patient records). National E-Sante Strategy (2019-2023) prioritizes rural ICT via connectivity and governance pillars, but rollout remains limited. Barriers include poor electricity/internet, low skills, security/climate risks in 80% rural areas. Gap: National digital ambitions for UHC contrast sharply with rural paper-dominant reality; digital confined to aggregation/pilots, not majority POC use. (187 words)
Source URLs: [Frontiers in Digital Health review](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8967994/); [Digital Impact Exchange DHIS2](https://exchange.dial.global/projects/niger-country-hmis-dhis2); [JoGHEP PHC integration](https://joghep.scholasticahq.com/article/144373-integrating-specialised-services-into-primary-health-care-in-niger-from-fragmentation-to-coordination)

Comments: Niger's rural primary schools record attendance, enrollment, and grades using paper-based questionnaires filled by school heads during annual school censuses (recensement scolaire). Data is collected on paper at the school level, then controlled, entered digitally at central/regional levels, and compiled into statistical yearbooks by the Ministry of Education. EMIS exists for aggregate data production and yearbooks, with a transition plan (PTSEF 2020-2022) to strengthen it, but no evidence of digital recording or tools at rural school level. Recent projects provide computers (e.g., 38k devices via World Bank LIRE project), but these target learning environments, not data recording. Recent digital library pilots in rural schools are exceptional. Given poor infrastructure in rural areas (82% rural population), paper-based recording predominates. Score 1: entirely paper-based at facility level.
Source URLs: [Niger MEN Statistical Yearbook 2020-2021](https://www.stat-niger.org/wp-content/uploads/publication_sectorielle/annuaire/secondaire/Annuaire_MEN_2020_2021_VF.pdf), [UNESCO IICBA Niger Brief](https://www.iicba.unesco.org/en/niger), [UNOPS Niger Education Digital Equipment](https://niger.un.org/fr/308330-le-niger-progresse-vers-une-%C3%A9ducation-accessible-et-de-qualit%C3%A9), [Journal du Niger Rural Digital Library](https://www.journalduniger.com/niger-le-numerique-sinvite-a-lecole-rurale-de-karey-gorou/)

Comments: Rural police stations (brigades) in Niger rely on paper-based recording for case files, complaints, and incident reports, primarily through handwritten procès-verbaux (PVs). Ethnographic studies describe the process: initial data in notebooks/sheets, interviews on loose paper, pencil drafts, pen/red pen corrections, and final typing on typewriters or limited brigade computers (one per brigade, self-funded, reverts to typewriter on blackouts). Paper scarcity noted; only ~10% complaints become formal PVs. No evidence of digital recording at station level; trainings (e.g., UNODC cyber) target specialized digital forensics, not routine case management. Francophone tradition of handwritten PVs persists in rural areas without digitisation pilots/projects found. Matches broader low digitalisation in Niger's justice/police sectors.
Source URLs: [Bureaucratic aesthetics: Report writing in Nigérien gendarmerie](https://d-nb.info/1103138715/34), [Policing the Frontier: Ethnography Niger gendarmerie](https://www.jstor.org/stable/10.7591/j.ctvpwhg9t), [Gendarmerie Nationale Niger](http://www.gendarmerie-nationale.defense.gouv.ne/organisation)

Comments: Rural registry offices in Niger (11,056 out of 11,638 total) record births and vital events manually on paper, producing duplicate handwritten forms: one archived locally, one sent to higher levels.CRVS Snapshot Notification and registration remain paper-based nationwide, with plans for digitization stalled by lack of electricity, internet, poor infrastructure, and climate damaging paper records. Birth registration completeness is 66.5% (2019), but vital stats compilation lags (last report 2014). Digital pilots include NRC/UNICEF equipping 81 communes (~30% of 270 total) with computers/solar to digitize 240k existing handwritten records via mobile teams in villages (2024)—not electronic point-of-service recording.NRC WURI pilot (2025) in Filingué targets 21k under-5s, digitizing existing records and training on form-filling (paper).Biometric Update UNECA notes electronic DBs only urban.UNECA Key barriers: rural infrastructure deficits. National e-gov ambitions (High Commissioner ICT since 2016) vs rural reality: paper default, donor pilots don't scale to majority facilities. Score 2: predominantly paper, some digital pilots for aggregation/digitization.
Source URLs: [CRVS Snapshot Niger](https://crvssystems.ca/country-profile/niger); [NRC Digitisation](https://www.nrc.no/perspectives/2024/securing-the-future-of-nigers-children-digitising-birth-certificates); [UNECA CRVS Digitalization](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/Digitalization%20of%20Civil%20Registration%20and%20Vital%20Statistics%20in%20Africa.pdf)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Nigeria predominantly use paper-based registers for patient data recording, including visits, files, and NCD records. Recent studies confirm 100% reliance on paper files with 0% electronic records in surveyed PHCs, and national EHR adoption remains low at ~18%, with rural areas facing acute barriers like unreliable electricity (41% grid access), poor internet (40% penetration), high costs, low digital literacy (26.7% proficient workers), and lack of training. Digital systems like DHIS2 are used nationally for aggregate HMIS reporting, with some pilots for immunization logistics (e.g., ~9,000 facilities reporting stock data), but point-of-care patient records remain manual; data is collated on paper and entered at LGA/district levels. Pilots exist (e.g., offline hardware trials, state telemedicine), but are isolated/donor-driven, not scaling to majority of ~30,000 rural PHCs. Key barriers: infrastructure deficits, policy fragmentation, resistance to change from paper workflows. National ambitions (e.g., NHMIS digitalization) contrast sharply with rural reality of paper dominance, limiting point-of-service digitalisation.
Source URLs: [BMC Public Health 2025 rural digital tools study](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12706899/); [Research Square 2024 PHC records assessment](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10996784/); [Global J Med Students 2025 EHR barriers](https://thegjms.org/barriers-to-the-adoption-of-electronic-health-records-in-nigerian-healthcare-systems-analysing-infrastructure-training-and-policy-challenges/)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Nigeria predominantly use paper-based registers for recording attendance, administrative data, and learning outcomes at the point of service. For example, in Nassarawo Primary School (Adamawa State, rural northeast), teachers manually tick names in worn paper books daily, facing issues like rain damage and termite destruction; digital backups are personal copies, not systematic.DPI Africa (2026). National EMIS (NEMIS on DHIS2) exists for aggregation, with pilots like EdoEMIS (51 schools including rural, daily digital attendance via app) and training in 12 states, but these are isolated or state-specific donor projects not representing most rural facilities.DHIS2.org (2025). World Bank analysis confirms schools complete paper census forms forwarded for central digital entry, not digital at school level.World Bank EMIS Study. Barriers: poor electricity/internet (41% offline), low digital literacy (<20% rural youth computer-proficient), high costs, insecurity. National policy pushes EMIS/DPI (UBEC digital platform 2024, GenU9ja connectivity), but rural reality lags with manual processes and slow pilot scaling.
Source URLs: [DPI Africa article](https://dpi.africa/classroom-attendance-in-nigeria-still-relies-on-paper/); [DHIS2 EdoEMIS](https://dhis2.org/school-reporting-edo-state-nigeria/); [World Bank Nigeria EMIS Study](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/951371468333598459/pdf/466180NWP0Box310Study0of0Nigeria111.pdf)

Comments: Rural police stations in Nigeria record criminal cases and complaints using manual paper-based systems, including handwritten statements, petitions, police reports, and exhibits logs. A 2020 assessment confirms records are kept in hard copy at stations, with limited electronic storage only at state headquarters; computer networks and databases see very low use across formations, and many cases go unrecorded due to officer fatigue.US Department of Justice report Academic studies highlight inadequate police presence and infrastructure (power, roads) in rural areas, relying on community policing without mention of digital tools at point-of-service.Heliyon study National digital initiatives exist: WAPIS (Interpol/EU-funded national criminal database since 2019), pilots like CRIMS, and planned smart stations (2025), but these are centralized or urban-focused, with no evidence of routine rural deployment.Interpol WAPIS Barriers include poor infrastructure, limited resources/training, and low government commitment to nationwide rollout. National ambitions for digital policing contrast sharply with rural paper reality, where data is likely aggregated manually upward.
Source URLs: [US Department of Justice](https://www.justice.gov/eoir/page/file/1342136/dl); [Heliyon](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7522756/); [Interpol WAPIS](https://www.interpol.int/en/How-we-work/Capacity-building/Capacity-building-projects/WAPIS-Programme)

Comments: Rural registry offices in Nigeria (NPC local registration centers, ~4000 nationwide, serving groups of rural communities) predominantly use paper-based recording for births, with registrars entering data manually into paper forms.UNECA CRVS Nigeria Snapshot Data is later digitized at regional levels, creating backlogs, but point-of-service remains manual.UNECA CRVS Nigeria Snapshot Digital systems like RapidSMS (since 2011) support monitoring/reporting, not registration.UNECA CRVS Nigeria Snapshot Recent eCRVS pilots with UNICEF (e.g., Nasarawa, Kano states using OpenCRVS/mobile apps at health facilities/community drives) and national platforms (ecrvs.nationalpopulation.gov.ng, self-service portal) indicate donor-supported digital efforts, but limited to pilots/select states, not majority of rural facilities.OpenCRVS Nigeria;Biometric Update Electronic databases exist only in urban areas.UNECA Digitalization Africa Barriers: poor infrastructure (electricity/internet in rural areas), low digital literacy, funding gaps, uneven rollout. National ambitions (eCRVS launches 2023-2026, legal reforms) outpace rural reality, where paper dominates default processes (score 2).NPC News
Source URLs: [UNECA CRVS Nigeria Snapshot](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/CRVS_NigeriaSnapshot_e.pdf);[UNECA Digitalization of CRVS in Africa](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/Digitalization%20of%20Civil%20Registration%20and%20Vital%20Statistics%20in%20Africa.pdf);[NPC News on Digital Reforms](https://nationalpopulation.gov.ng/news/npc-calls-for-consolidation-of-civil-registration-laws-to-boost-digital-reforms)"

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Republic of the Congo (COG) predominantly use paper-based systems for patient records, registers, and prescriptions. DHIS2 implementation was announced in 2019 by the Ministry of Health, but as of 2020-2021 assessments, no evidence of completion or facility-level digital recording exists; national HMIS relies on aggregate data entry, likely from paper reports at district level GHS Index report. No electronic health records in use (score 0 in 2020 assessment). World Bank health projects support DHIS2 nationally with tablets for verification, but no mention of rural clinic-level digital patient data capture World Bank Health Sector Project. WHO notes limited eHealth; francophone African rural clinics typically rely on paper 'registres' and 'cartes de patient', with central digitization. No recent (2024-2026) evidence of shift to digital at rural level; score 1 as paper is standard without rural-specific digital pilots.
Source URLs: [GHS Index Congo-Brazzaville](https://ghsindex.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/12/Congo-Brazzaville.pdf), [World Bank Health Sector Project](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/997271619365476164/pdf/Congo-Republic-of-Health-Sector-Project.pdf), [WHO AFRO eHealth](https://www.afro.who.int/news/promise-ehealth-african-region)

Comments: Republic of the Congo is developing an integrated EMIS as part of the GPE-funded TRESOR project (2024), including national strategy, legal framework, platform operationalization, yearbooks, and dashboards. Republic participates in AU/GPE KIX EMIS workshops (2024). UNESCO notes efforts to digitize primary education after prior EMIS non-functionality. No evidence of digital recording at rural primary school level (attendance, enrollment, grades); data collection likely paper-based at schools with aggregation/digital entry at higher levels. Fits "predominantly paper with some digital (pilots, donor projects, or aggregate reporting)."
Source URLs: [GPE TRESOR Grant Agreement](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099072224105580047/pdf/TF0C5564b1bf690d71bcb8130cedc821c69.pdf), [UNESCO IICBA Congo Brief](https://www.iicba.unesco.org/fr/congo-republic), [GPE KIX EMIS Meeting](https://www.gpekix.org/news/education-management-information-systems-emis-heart-kix-objectives)

Comments: Extensive searches across web, academic papers, US State Department 2023 HR report, UNODC, and targeted queries for Congo-Brazzaville (Republic of the Congo) rural police stations yielded no specific evidence on how rural police record criminal cases or complaints. Reports highlight police abuses, corruption, and poor conditions in stations (mostly urban), but no mentions of digital case management, EMIS-like systems, or even paper vs. digital practices in rural areas. Congo's low development, poor rural infrastructure, and lack of documented reforms suggest paper-based if any, but genuinely no evidence found. Score 0: unknown.
Source URLs: [US State Dept 2023 HR Report Republic of the Congo](https://www.state.gov/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/528267_CONGO-REP-2023-HUMAN-RIGHTS-REPORT.pdf), [Afrobarometer Congo-Brazzaville Police](https://www.afrobarometer.org/publication/ad841-citizens-in-congo-brazzaville-complain-about-police-corruption-lack-of-professionalism/), [Gendarmerie Congo Info](https://www.force-publique.net/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/2017-Congo-gb.pdf)

Comments: Rural civil registration offices (centres d'état civil) in the Republic of the Congo predominantly use paper-based birth registers. No evidence found of operational electronic CRVS systems in rural areas. A national digital system, SIFEC (Système Intégré des Faits d'État Civil), was validated in February 2026 but remains in implementation phase, with no confirmed rural rollout. WHO reports functional civil registration (75-94% completeness) nationally but lacking vital statistics; rural completeness likely lower per World Bank/UNICEF surveys, though specific figures unavailable. Births declared at local offices within 30 days.
Source URLs: [World Bank Rural Birth Registration](https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/SP.REG.BRTH.RU.ZS?locations=CG), [WHO CRVS Analysis](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10680109/), [ACSI SIFEC](https://acsi.cg/actualites/presentation-de-la-plateforme-denommee-systeme-integre-des-faits-detat-civil-sifec/)"

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics (health centers) in Rwanda predominantly use digital systems for patient data recording. OpenMRS EMR modules are deployed in nearly 400 of ~500 health centers (2018 baseline), primarily for HIV/TB but expanding, functioning as point-of-care local systems.Rwanda Digital Health Strategy National rollout of integrated EMR (eBuzima) ongoing via MoH partnerships with NGOs like Health Builders in rural districts (e.g., Rwamagana, Rulindo), replacing paper-based records and improving accuracy in pilots.Health Builders Report DHIS2 HMIS collects aggregates nationwide since 2012, supported by revised registers.DHIS2 2022 survey at 54 rural health centers shows high EMR acceptability/use despite some infrastructure issues and possible dual paper entry.JMIR Study Barriers: connectivity/power outages (25% sites), low digital literacy, resistance to change; addressed via training, local servers. National policy targets 100% EMR coverage by 2022, with recent (2025) district rollouts indicating majority digital at POC, though some paper fallback persists in rollout gaps.
Source URLs: [Rwanda Digital Health Strategy 2018-2023](https://extranet.who.int/countryplanningcycles/sites/default/files/public_file_rep/RWA_Rwanda_Digital-Health-Strategy_2018-2023.Pdf); [DHIS2 Rwanda HMIS](https://docs.dhis2.org/en/topics/user-stories/rwanda-hmis-powered-by-dhis2.html); [JMIR EHR Survey Rwanda](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9115652/); [Health Builders Rural EMR](https://dukeghic.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/20/2025/09/Transforming-Rural-Healthcare-in-Rwanda-with-a-Centralized-Electronic-Medical-Records-System-2.pdf)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Rwanda use digital systems like SDMS for enrollment, monthly attendance aggregates, and admin data; CA-MIS for assessment grades entry by teachers World Bank Learning Resilience Report. A 2025 mobile app enables daily digital attendance recording, rolled out to 2,371 schools across 22 districts including rural Nyamagabe, where teachers log attendance directly, generating real-time data New Times. Digital systems are standard (e.g., 85% teachers on CAMIS nationally), but paper may remain for daily details before monthly entry or in low-connectivity areas (56.7% schools connected, teacher-computer 14:1 primary). Pilots like OLPC in ~25% schools; national rollout indicates majority use. Barriers: rural connectivity gaps, device shortages, training. National EMIS ambition advancing, with rural reality catching up via recent apps and projects (Starlink, fiber). (148 words)
Source URLs: [World Bank Learning Resilience Report](https://thedocs.worldbank.org/en/doc/e4ce55dbf9e381faa76ce4bad55d336d-0140022025/related/2Rwanda-TF0C2272-Final-Report-Learning-Resilience-in-Rwanda-Basic-Education-System.pdf); [New Times Rwanda](https://www.newtimes.co.rw/article/32152/news/technology/new-digital-attendance-system-aims-to-curb-school-dropouts); [REB ICT Policy](https://www.reb.gov.rw/fileadmin/user_upload/REB/Publications/POLICIES/ICT_In_Education_Policy.pdf)

Comments: Rwanda National Police (RNP) has undergone sustained digital transformation extending to rural areas. The Integrated Electronic Case Management System (IECMS), launched January 2016, covers the entire justice chain — police (Criminal Investigation Department), NPPA, judiciary, and correctional services. 100% of cases are filed electronically, reducing average processing time from 7 to 3.4 months. The IECMS was specifically designed to reach rural areas: a 2023 University of Rwanda dissertation studied its effectiveness in Nyaruguru District, a remote rural area, confirming deployment there. Government has subsidised internet connectivity at 45 rural and remote police sites. SMS notifications allow citizens to track case progress using basic mobile phones. In December 2025, RNP introduced four new online reporting systems. Criminal records are fully digitalised via the Irembo platform. However, ICT literacy gaps and infrastructure limitations remain significant obstacles for rural personnel. Internet penetration stands at ~31%. Some rural stations may still use paper for initial documentation before entering cases into IECMS. Rwanda is categorically different from most African countries — the default is digital, not paper, making it score 3.
Source URLs: https://www.synisys.com/case-studies/rwandas-justice-sector-integrated-electronic-case-management-system-iecms/; https://dr.ur.ac.rw/handle/123456789/2173; https://police.gov.rw/media/news-detail/news/inside-rwanda-polices-high-tech-shift-to-safer-smarter-policing/

Comments: Rural registry offices in Rwanda, primarily at the cell level (2148 cells covering rural areas), use the National Centralized and Integrated CRVS (NCI-CRVS) system for recording vital events like births and deaths at the point of service. For home births (common in rural areas), village chiefs complete paper community notification forms, which cell executive secretaries then enter electronically into NCI-CRVS within 30 days, generating a unique NIN and integrating with the National Population Register (NPR). Health facilities (rural included) have data managers who digitize maternity registers directly. Certification occurs via digital platforms like IremboGov. Law 32/2016 (revised 2020) mandates electronic registers, with paper used alongside for a transition period not exceeding 6 months, after which only electronic is used. Over 416 sectors and 2148 cells provide real-time digital services, contributing to high completeness rates (90%+ births in 2024). Digital systems are standard across rural facilities, but initial paper proofs persist, indicating some paper processes remain. No major barriers noted; strong government commitment and decentralization bridge national digital ambition with rural reality, though early transition (2020-2021) saw slight dips in completeness due to awareness and COVID.
Source URLs: [NIDA CRVS SoP User Guide](https://www.nida.gov.rw/fileadmin/user_upload/NIDA/Publications/CRVS_SoP_USER_GUIDE.pdf); [UN CRVSID Rwanda Case Study](https://unstats.un.org/legal-identity-agenda/documents/Paper/2023/CRVSID-Rwanda.pdf); [MINALOC CRVS Launch](https://www.minaloc.gov.rw/news-detail/rwanda-launches-new-integrated-civil-registration-system)"

Comments: São Tomé and Príncipe has implemented DHIS2 as its national health information system, with individual tracker modules for primary health care (PHC) patient registry and consultations since 2020, supported by WHO and UNDP (tablets provided). Training occurs at facility level. However, pre-DHIS2 systems were mostly paper-based with parallel subsystems. A 2023 WHO EMTCT assessment (for HIV/syphilis) confirms rural health posts and primary centers use manual registries, patient cards, and paper aggregation sent upward; DHIS2 used at district level for aggregates, not routine facility digital entry. No direct evidence of digital patient records (registers, prescriptions) at rural clinics; likely paper collection with later digital entry at higher levels, fitting score 2 (predominantly paper with some digital/aggregate reporting). Recent (2025) efforts assess longitudinal records, but point-of-care remains paper-dominant per latest facility-specific report.
Source URLs: [WHO DHIS2 Implementation](https://www.who.int/about/accountability/results/who-results-report-2020-mtr/country-story/2020/implementing-the-dhis2-to-accelerate-universal-health-coverage-in-s%C3%A3o-tom%C3%A9-and-principe), [WHO EMTCT Assessment (2023)](https://cdn.who.int/media/docs/default-source/hq-hiv-hepatitis-and-stis-library/final-emtct-assessment-report-sao-tome--principe-12-april-2023.pdf?sfvrsn=af2c9828_1), [DHIS2 HISP Highlights (2025)](https://dhis2.org/hisp-highlights-july-2025/)

Comments: Sao Tome and Principe has implemented an integrated Education EMIS system at the Department of Education level, supporting evidence-based policymaking and data management. However, no specific details found on rural primary schools using digital tools for attendance, enrollment, grades, or admin data. Given rural infrastructure challenges and lack of school-level digital mentions, processes are likely predominantly paper-based with some aggregation into central digital EMIS.
Source URLs: [UNESCO IICBA Country Brief](https://www.iicba.unesco.org/en/sao-tome-and-principe), [UNESCO Planipolis](http://planipolis.iiep.unesco.org/en/2022/education-sector-plan-202223–202627-7445), [Education Profiles](https://education-profiles.org/sub-saharan-africa/sao-tome-and-principe/~inclusion)"

Comments: No evidence found of digital case management in rural police stations of Sao Tome and Principe. A national Integrated Case Management System (ICMS) contract was awarded in Dec 2022, with phase 1 rollout planned for police, prosecution, judiciary, and prisons in the city of Sao Tome only (urban), with later expansion nationwide planned but no confirmed progress or rural implementation as of 2026. US State Dept reports and other sources describe police as ineffective/corrupt but silent on recording methods; likely paper-based dockets (procès-verbaux) in rural areas given small scale and lack of mentions.
Source URLs: [Synergy ICMS](https://www.synisys.com/news/transforming-the-justice-system-in-sao-tome-and-principe-with-synergy/), [US State Dept 2023 HR Report](https://www.state.gov/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/528267_SAO-TOME-AND-PRINCIPE-2023-HUMAN-RIGHTS-REPORT.pdf), [OSAC Country Report](https://www.osac.gov/Content/Report/15cfa519-f9ce-4169-969a-1cb10cb09f42)"

Comments: Rural registry offices (Postos de Registo Civil) in Sao Tome and Principe predominantly use paper forms for birth registration, with high coverage (98% rural births registered per 2023 UN/World Bank data via campaigns). Electronic databases exist only in urban areas. Local rural offices send paper copies to regional offices for electronic processing and transmission to central DGRN. Digitization efforts ongoing (pilots for MOSIP digital ID anchored on CRVS since 2024, World Bank project strategy), but rural facilities remain paper-based with some digital aggregation upstream. Score 2 reflects predominant paper at rural points with partial digital backend.
Source URLs: [UNECA CRVS Report](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/Digitalization%20of%20Civil%20Registration%20and%20Vital%20Statistics%20in%20Africa.pdf), [Biometric Update MOSIP Pilot](https://www.biometricupdate.com/202506/sao-tome-and-principe-pilots-mosip-based-national-digital-id-system), [DGRN Official Site](https://dgrn.gov.st/sobre.php)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Senegal, including health centers, posts, and huts, predominantly use paper-based registers for patient data recording at the point of service. Aggregate data from these facilities is compiled and entered into the national DHIS2 system at district or higher levels for reporting and analysis, but there is no evidence of routine electronic health records (EMR) or point-of-care digital entry in most rural facilities.Plan Stratégique Santé Digitale 2018-2023 Digital initiatives exist, such as pilots for electronic patient records in a few facilities (2 hospitals, 1 health center, 1 post as of 2025, covering 127k patients) and national programs like PDSS (Programme de Digitalisation du Système de Santé) supported by World Bank, targeting 20% EMR adoption in health centers by end-2025.WeAreTech Africa article;World Bank Digital Economy Project Key barriers include poor rural internet connectivity (only 51/1600 facilities connected baseline), unreliable power, lack of ICT-trained staff, high costs, and uneven health worker distribution.Plan Stratégique Santé Digitale 2018-2023 There is a significant gap between national ambitions (Digital Senegal 2025, PSSD for full interconnection) and rural reality, where paper remains default and digital is limited to pilots and aggregate reporting.
Source URLs: [Plan Stratégique Santé Digitale 2018-2023](https://www.sante.gouv.sn/sites/default/files/plsantedigitale.pdf); [World Bank Senegal Digital Economy Acceleration Project](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099050124114539720/pdf/P17252418e081d077193351132e1eeb0805.pdf); [Senegal Launches EMR Projects](https://www.wearetech.africa/en/fils-uk/brief/brief-simple/senegal-launches-six-major-projects-for-electronic-records-telemedicine)

Comments: Senegal has a functioning national Education Management Information System (EMIS) called SIMEN, which produces reliable annual statistical yearbooks on enrollment, attendance, completion, and teacher data. Data flows from rural primary schools through district (IEF) and regional (IA) levels to central SIMEN, with schools using performance-based contracts (PBCs) for reporting. No evidence of direct digital entry at rural school level; collection likely paper-based at facilities (e.g., logs, registers), aggregated digitally at higher levels, consistent with decentralized structure and rural infrastructure challenges (limited internet/electricity). Recent pilots: DHIS2-based EMIS aggregate data in two districts since 2024 (GPE KIX project), digital hubs in 70 rural primary schools (2023+), but not nationwide rollout. World Bank PAPSE project (2022) strengthens SIMEN capacity but focuses on central improvements. Score 2: predominantly paper with some digital pilots/aggregate reporting.
Source URLs:
[DHIS2 Education Senegal](https://education.dhis2.org/implementation/senegal/), [World Bank PAPSE Project](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/583961644420984807/pdf/Senegal-Improvement-of-Education-System-Performance-PAPSE-Project.pdf), [World Bank PAPSE Summary](https://www.worldbank.org/en/projects-operations/project-detail/P169916)"
