Comments: Senegal's rural police stations, primarily handled by Gendarmerie brigades, record case files, complaints, and incident reports using handwritten procès-verbaux (PVs) as mandated by the Criminal Procedure Code. PVs are drafted "sur le champ" (on the spot), signed on each page, with no mention of digital tools; originals sent physically to prosecutors. Francophone standard PVs are paper-based. Recent digital initiatives (e.g., cybercrime reporting portal 2026, e-Senegal police clearances, AFIS for Gendarmerie) focus on online services and central systems, not frontline rural recording. No evidence of digital case logging at rural stations; procedures imply paper-first, possible later entry at higher levels. Score 1: entirely paper-based at rural facilities.
Source URLs: [Senegal Criminal Procedure Code](https://justice.sec.gouv.sn/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/code-procedure-penal.pdf), [US State Dept 2020 HR Report](https://www.state.gov/wp-content/uploads/2021/03/SENEGAL-2020-HUMAN-RIGHTS-REPORT.pdf), [e-Senegal Portal](https://www.biometricupdate.com/202603/senegal-launches-digital-government-portal-to-ease-access-to-public-services), [Gendarmerie AFIS](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gxWQajmfQaE)"

Comments: Rural registry offices (in communautés rurales, ~70% of population) and village auxiliaries predominantly use paper-based recording, with village chiefs issued notebooks for vital events like births. Forms (e.g., Form No. 1) are provided at point of service, and low rural birth registration rates (~29%) reflect access barriers.GFF Data Portal Some digital exists: >100 centers computerized (out of 587 total, some rural), local servers for data entry/backup.UNECA CRVS Snapshot Pilots include RAPIDPRO mobile app for birth capture in Kolda region (southern rural).UNECA CRVS Snapshot Recent efforts: digitizing 20M+ historical records, new digital platform/mobile app launched (2025), secondary centers in remote rural areas, but legislation to enable full digital processes pending; rural-urban disparities persist.BiometricUpdate Barriers: infrastructure/electricity in remote areas (rainy season isolation), training, legacy paper systems (1972 Family Code). National ambition for e-CRVS outpaces rural point-of-service reality, where paper remains default except isolated pilots/donor projects; no evidence majority rural facilities routinely digital at capture.
Source URLs: [UNECA CRVS Snapshot](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/CRVS_SenegalSnapshot_e.pdf); [BiometricUpdate 2025](https://www.biometricupdate.com/202506/chad-mali-senegal-pursue-birth-registration-reforms-to-close-gaps); [UNICEF CHIP Report](https://www.child-identity.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/11/UNICEF-CHIP-Report-Senegal-EN.pdf)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Seychelles (termed community health centres or PHCs on outer islands/districts) continue to rely predominantly on paper-based patient data recording, despite national eHIS rollout. The Ministry of Health's 2022-2026 Strategic Plan states data capture is "primarily paper-based" with duplicative tools, and eHIS implementation (started 2020) has stalled, creating an urgent need for transition Seychelles MoH Strategic Plan. The 2023 Annual Performance Report shows progress with 88% eHIS patient registration coverage in PHCs (up from 29% in 2022), but actual consultation recording lags, remaining below expectations even after EMR training for 140 doctors MoH Performance Report 2023. No specific rural/outer island distinctions, but given small scale (17 CHCs nationwide), rollout likely covers them. Digital systems like eHIS integrate surveillance/labs centrally, but point-of-care is hybrid. Barriers: stalled implementation, staff training/satisfaction, data quality. National ambition for full eHIS contrasts with persistent paper dominance at clinics Commonwealth WHO Assessment. Score 3 reflects majority digital registration standardizing, with paper fallback for consultations.
Source URLs: [Seychelles MoH Strategic Plan](https://www.health.gov.sc/national-health-strategic-plan-2022-2026-2/strategic-direction-six-improve-data-for-impact/), [MoH Performance Report 2023](https://www.health.gov.sc/wp-content/uploads/Annual-Health-Sector-Performance-Report-2023.pdf), [Commonwealth WHO Digital Health Assessment](https://thecommonwealth.org/news/seychelles-health-system-bolstered-commonwealth-who-partnership), [GHS Index Seychelles](https://ghsindex.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/12/Seychelles.pdf)

Comments: Seychelles Ministry of Education operates a national EMIS for collecting school data on students, staff, infrastructure from all institutions, including primary schools on outer islands (rural equivalents). In 2019, AU's IPED piloted DataPlat digital platform at schools (e.g., Anse Royale secondary) to address prior issues of inconsistent, unreliable, slow paper-based collection methods, enabling direct digital entry to national repository. OpenEMIS advisors assisted MoE in 2025 ("jump-start" EMIS), indicating ongoing digital rollout. Annual Education Statistics booklets (2020-2024) published by EMIS section. No evidence of paper-only at schools post-pilots; small nation-wide system suggests predominant digital recording at rural primary schools for attendance/enrollment/grades/admin, score 3.
Source URLs: [Seychelles Nation 2019 DataPlat pilot](https://www.nation.sc/articles/1130/education-related-data-collection-gets-big-boost), [Community Systems Foundation OpenEMIS 2025](https://www.facebook.com/CommunitySystemsFoundation/photos/today-our-openemis-advisors-are-wrapping-up-an-intensive-two-weeks-in-seychelles/868470265505776/), [MoE Statistics page](https://edu.gov.sc/education-statistics/)"

Comments: Rural police stations in Seychelles (e.g., Praslin, La Digue, Mont Fleuri) record criminal cases and complaints using a national digital case management system built on FileMaker platform, funded by UNODC and developed by Transmedia. Deployed across all stations, which are connected nationwide, allowing point-of-service entry of incident reports, occurrence books, and case details on desktops/iPads. It replaced parallel manual records, digitizes intake forms, enables searches across districts, cross-references (vehicles, phones), and supports court use. Detective from Mont Fleuri station (Mahe rural-ish) confirms: "all police stations are connected... transformed the life of the Seychelles police force." Evolutions include 2023 Cautions module, digitized Occurrence Book. Some paper may linger for minor/admin tasks, but digital is standard at rural point-of-service. No evidence of majority paper or pilots only. Barriers: small population (98k), remote islands may limit infra/maintenance, but connectivity confirmed. National e-gov push aligns with rural reality.Winsoft International; Seychelles Police; Forensic Science Int.
Source URLs: [Winsoft International](https://winsoft-international.com/seychelles-police-department-chooses-the-filemaker-platform-to-aid-fight-against-crime/); [Seychelles Police Directory](https://www.police.gov.sc/contact/directory); [PMC Forensic Seychelles](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7930355/)

Comments: Seychelles Civil Status has regional offices on islands like Praslin serving rural populations. WHO SCORE assessment (2013-2018 data) states: "All data are exchanged electronically from local to regional offices and then to central offices: Paper-based locally, electronic processing". Birth registration requires in-person submission of paper maternity certificate at regional offices. Recent 2024 launches (SeyID virtual IDs, CertExpress online certificates) enable digital access to records but data entry remains by central officers on paper inputs. No evidence of digital recording at rural points; fits predominantly paper with central digitization (score 2). Completeness 90%.
Source URLs: [WHO SCORE Seychelles](https://cdn.who.int/media/docs/default-source/documents/ddi/score/country-profiles/who_score_syc_en.pdf), [ICS Birth Registration](https://www.ics.gov.sc/civil-status/birth-registration), [ICS Digital Solutions Launch](https://www.ics.gov.sc/about/media-centre/publications/launch-of-new-digital-solutions-for-public-services)"

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics (PHUs) in Sierra Leone predominantly use paper-based recording for patient data, including forms HF1-HF9 for aggregate service reporting to NHMIS/DHIS2 and paper patient files at point-of-care. A 2022 survey of 55 rural facilities found majority use 1-2 digital tools, mainly DHIS2/eIDSR for aggregate/weekly disease surveillance reporting via tablets/mobile (not individual patient records), with no EMR or patient registries in use; individualized longitudinal data not exchanged.JMIR Formative Research. Digital surveillance rolled out nationwide to 1500+ facilities by 2019, with >95% timeliness/completeness via mobile apps, but limited to epidemiological data.WHO AFRO. Isolated EMR pilots exist (e.g., GOPA-GIZ in 2 rural PHUs in Kambia/Port Loko; planned QEHSSSP hub-spokes in select rural districts), but no nationwide scaling; paper remains default due to infrastructure gaps (power, connectivity).Sierra Leone Digital Health Roadmap 2024-2026. Key barriers: poor electricity/internet in rural areas (50% districts off-grid), hardware shortages, low digital literacy. National ambitions (e.g., DHIS2, strategies) focus on aggregate/surveillance, not point-of-care patient records, creating gap with rural paper reality.
Source URLs: [JMIR Formative Research](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9233249/); [WHO AFRO eIDSR](https://www.afro.who.int/countries/sierra-leone/news/sierra-leone-sustains-digital-health-gains-through-nationwide-health-facility-device-troubleshooting); [SL Digital Health Roadmap](https://mohs.gov.sl/download/68/digital-health/18150/sierral-leone-digital-health-roadmap-2024-2026-2.pdf)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Sierra Leone record administrative data such as attendance, enrolment, and learning outcomes predominantly using paper registers and forms. For the Annual School Census (ASC), school heads pre-fill paper questionnaires, which enumerators then digitize using SurveyCTO tablets during visits, verifying on-site headcounts. No evidence found of routine digital tools (e.g., tablets, apps) at rural schools for point-of-service recording; EMIS serves as a national repository for aggregated ASC data, not real-time school-level use.Annual School Census Report 2022Annual School Censuses 2023-2024 In rural areas, QAOs often skip full verification due to remoteness, and connectivity limits uploads; headteachers lack IT skills for digital entry.Sightsavers EMIS Mapping Pilots like SLEAMS (2020, 43 schools) and recent tablet rollouts (e.g., Wi De Ya for attendance, One Tablet per School) exist but are isolated/district-level, not majority rural adoption. Barriers include poor electricity (85% primary schools lack), low connectivity (91% no internet), untrained staff, and infrastructure gaps, especially rural. National ambitions (e.g., NDLS 2025-2030, EMIS strengthening) focus on aggregation/policy, not point-of-service digitalisation in rural facilities, creating a gap where paper remains default.
Source URLs: [MBSSE Annual School Census 2022](https://mbsse.gov.sl/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/Annual-School-Census-Report_2022.pdf); [MBSSE Annual School Censuses 2023-2024](https://mbsseknowledgeplatform.gov.sl/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/Annual-School-Censuses-2023-and-2024-Draft-Report.pdf); [Sightsavers EMIS Report 2022](https://research.sightsavers.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/2211_Sightsavers-mapping-the-existing-emis-system-in-sierra-leone.pdf)

Comments: Rural police stations in Sierra Leone record criminal cases and complaints using entirely paper-based methods, such as occurrence books, dockets, and manual files stored in locked cabinets. No evidence of digital case management systems deployed at point-of-service in rural stations; the SLP's 2025-2029 Strategic Development Plan plans an e-Case Management System (baseline survey 2025, implementation 2026+), confirming current paper reliance and need to migrate from paper to digital for criminal records. Key barriers include inadequate electricity especially in rural areas (reliance on thermal generators, poor supply), insufficient internet connectivity in most facilities, lack of computers/printers, and low ICT capacity. Recent digitizations (HR system 2025) and donations (media computers) are urban/regional or non-case related. National ambitions via SLDTP contrast with rural infrastructure gaps, keeping point-of-service recording manual.
Source URLs: [SLP Strategic Development Plan 2025-2029 (UNDP)](https://www.undp.org/sites/g/files/zskgke326/files/2025-04/police_strategy_booklet_29th_jan-2025.pdf); [AdvocAid Police Complaints Manual (2019)](http://advocaidsl.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/04/AdvocAid-Police-Complaints-Manual_final.pdf); [JMIR: Electricity and ICT in SL Health Facilities (proxy for rural)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8855303/)

Comments: Rural birth registration in Sierra Leone occurs primarily at 1,300 health facilities by 2,600 health workers via MoH memorandum, at chiefdom/community level.UNECA CRVS Snapshot Registration is mostly paper-based; computers only in urban centers, not rural health facilities or chiefdom registries.UNECA CRVS Snapshot NCRA plans expansion but assessments show rural not ready. Digital efforts: national eCRVS upgraded, NIN enrollment ~90%, biometric IDs piloted.ID4Africa PDF 2021 pilot training on mobile app for birth notification in Western Rural health units (outsourced app stores data directly to NCRA).NCRA Training Similar trainings elsewhere, but no evidence of routine point-of-service digital recording in most rural facilities—paper default, digital for aggregate/national.World Bank Blog Barriers: electricity, connectivity, no computers, costs (digital ID 7x paper).The Conversation National ambition (NCRA Act 2016 enables e-systems, 90% coverage) vs rural reality: pilots/donor apps in isolated districts, majority paper at point-of-service (score 2).
Source URLs: [UNECA CRVS Sierra Leone Snapshot (2019)](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/CRVS_SierraLeoneSnapshot_e.pdf); [NCRA Mobile App Training Western Rural (2021)](https://ncra.gov.sl/ncra-trains-health-workers-in-the-western-rural-district-on-births-and-deaths-registration-procedures-using-simple-mobile-app/); [ID4Africa Sierra Leone Digital ID (2024)](https://id4africa.com/2024/ps2/PS2-3-Sierra-Leone-Final.pdf)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Somalia predominantly record patient data using standardized paper registers (12 types covering OPD, IPD, immunization, MCH, etc.), with monthly aggregated summary forms sent to district HMIS officers for entry into the national DHIS2 system.WHO HIS Assessment Although DHIS2 enables direct entry via computer/tablet, in practice most facilities—including rural primary health units and health centres—use paper at point-of-service, as confirmed by 2022 field visits to rural sites like Dayah HC (Galmudug).WHO HIS Assessment No nationwide EMR exists; WHO eHealth profile indicates N/A EHR adoption at primary facilities.WHO eHealth Profile Digital pilots include OGOW app (Puntland vaccinations via tablets), IDSR tracker (2023), electronic immunization registry, and donor projects like CaafimaadPlus C+ DDS (Ogow Health, live in 43 south-central facilities incl. rural since 2024 for patient records/dashboard), RAAD EMR (scaling to 40 clinics).ReliefWeb CaafimaadPlus These are isolated, partner-funded, not scaled as standard across most rural clinics. Barriers: Limited rural electricity/internet/devices, HMIS officer shortages (58-70% districts lack), staff multi-tasking (95%), conflict/displacement, donor fragmentation.WHO HIS Assessment National policy (HIS strategy, EPHS) aims for EMR/digital HIS (2023-26), but rural PoS remains paper-dominant with digital limited to aggregation/pilots—clear gap between ambition and reality.
Source URLs: [WHO HIS Assessment 2022](https://applications.emro.who.int/docs/9789292742188-eng.pdf); [WHO eHealth Profile Somalia](https://www.emro.who.int/images/stories/ehealth/documents/somalia-ehealth-country-profile.pdf); [CaafimaadPlus Digital Health Rollout](https://reliefweb.int/report/somalia/digital-health-somalia-game-changer-humanitarian-crisis-response)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Somalia predominantly use paper-based recording for administrative data, attendance, and learning outcomes. According to the 2018 World Bank EMIS report, data collection relies on enumerators visiting ~1,300 schools (75% coverage, including rural) with printed 13-page census questionnaires filled out on paper; schools provide info verbally or on paper, with no federal digital tools at point-of-service. Forms are aggregated manually at regional/state levels before central digital entry into PINEAPPLES software. State variations exist (e.g., some Galmudug rural head teachers upload online), but federal/national picture shows paper default. The MoECHE 2020 Yearbook claims head teachers enter data via online EMIS, but lacks methodological details distinguishing rural paper vs digital and aligns with prior paper-upward model; remote rural data hard to verify due to access/security. Digital pilots like UNICEF/World Vision Digital Attendance App (DAA) limited to 3 schools in Puntland (urban Garowe?), needing $100k to scale; not representative of rural majority. Barriers: insecurity limiting rural access, low incentives for non-state schools (majority), poor infrastructure/power/mobile data in rural/nomadic areas, staff shortages, no universal devices/training. National ambition for decentralized EMIS (ESSP 2018-20, recent policy) contrasts with rural reality of annual paper censuses and sporadic records; no evidence majority rural schools routinely use digital at point-of-service (score 1). World Bank EMIS Report; MoECHE 2020 Yearbook; DAA Pilot
Source URLs: [World Bank EMIS Status Report](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/317341555399356772/pdf/Status-of-Education-Management-Information-System.pdf); [MoECHE Education Statistics Yearbook 2020](https://moe.gov.so/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/Education-Statistics-2020.pdf); [Response Innovation Lab DAA Pilot](https://www.responseinnovationlab.com/updates/behind-the-digital-attendance-app-pilot-in-somalia)

Comments: Rural police stations in Somalia predominantly use paper-based records such as occurrence books (OB)/station diaries, crime registers, lost property registers, lock-up registers, and prisoners property registers, as per Somali Police Basic Recruit Curriculum from UNSOM, which trains on 'police books and forms' without mentioning digital tools. Official Somali Police site notes UNODC-supported digital crime statistics system populated with 'reported data' from police across Somalia (including rural posts), implying aggregation from paper at higher levels rather than direct digital entry at stations. Recent IECMS (Oct 2025) launch replaces historical paper-based processes but targets CID, prosecutors, courts, prisons—not routine station case files; rural rollout unlikely given fragmented infrastructure and calls for centralized systems. Prisons still paper-based per reports; similar for rural police. No rural-specific digital pilots found; score 1 as broad evidence points to paper at facility level.
Source URLs: [Somali Police Force Data Statistics](https://police.gov.so/data-statistics/), [SONNA IECMS Launch](https://sonna.so/en/somalia-launches-groundbreaking-electronic-case-management-system-to-revolutionize-justice-sector/), [UNSOM Police Curriculum](https://unsom.unmissions.org/sites/default/files/basic_recruit_curriculum_brc_v31.pdf), [UNODC CIP Presentation](https://www.unodc.org/documents/commissions/CCPCJ/CCPCJ_Sessions/CCPCJ_31/TD_presentations/UNODC_Somalia_Elena_JULVE_session_1_PM.pdf)

Comments: Rural registry offices (Degmada/local municipalities) rely on paper forms for births; extremely low completeness (3.5% children <5 registered, 0.3% certificates; 2020 SHDS, no rural-urban gap Immigration and Refugee Board of Canada). Home/nomadic births undocumented. UNFPA 2021: No national CRVS office, decentralized, policy for digital repository but unimplemented; fragility/infrastructure gaps block rollout UNFPA CRVS Profile. Digital pilots: OpenCRVS in 30 districts (many rural), 160 hospitals linked; 28k+ births registered digitally at point-of-service to central DB (2026) OpenCRVS. Launched 19 districts incl. rural (e.g., Abudwaq, Hudur, Kismayo) Aug 2025 SONNA. Barriers: Insecurity, nomadic lifestyle, poor connectivity, low awareness. Gap: CRVS policy/law (2021+), 2024-29 plan ambitious but pilots cover minority facilities; majority rural remains paper-based.
Source URLs: [UNFPA CRVS Profile](https://somalia.unfpa.org/sites/default/files/pub-pdf/crvs_report.pdf); [IRB Canada](https://www.ecoi.net/en/document/2090364.html); [OpenCRVS Somalia](https://www.opencrvs.org/about-us/country-stories/somalia)"

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in South Africa predominantly use paper-based systems for recording patient data at the point of service, including patient records and tick registers (manual logbooks for services like vaccinations, antenatal care). Clinicians record data manually, consolidate monthly into paper forms, and dedicated data capturers then enter aggregates into DHIS2 for national reporting—not used for individual patient records at point-of-care.Quality of routine health data in DHIS2 (SciELO SA) No national EHR exists in public primary clinics; existing digital tools (e.g., Tier.net for HIV/TB, HPRS for demographics) are stand-alone inventories or back-end digitization from paper, with clinicians lacking real-time digital access during consultations.Exploring barriers to EHRs (Frontiers) Digital pilots like E-tick (electronic tick register) stalled after district-level trials in urban Gauteng; Western Cape's PHCIS offers partial digital entry but retains paper barcodes.E-tick case study (JMIR) Key barriers: poor rural connectivity (40% facilities lack reliable internet), hardware theft, funding shortages, skills gaps, governance issues, urban-rural divide. National ambitions (NHI, Digital Health Strategy) focus on future EHRs, but rural point-of-service remains paper-default with digital only for upward aggregation—gap evident in poor DHIS2 data quality from transcription errors.
Source URLs: [Quality of routine health data in DHIS2 (SciELO SA)](https://scielo.org.za/pdf/sajim/v26n1/55.pdf); [Exploring barriers and facilitators to EHRs (Frontiers in Digital Health)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10437058/); [E-tick Qualitative Insights (JMIR Med Inform)](https://medinform.jmir.org/2026/1/e73831)

Comments: Rural primary schools in South Africa use the South African School Administration and Management System (SA-SAMS), a digital tool for recording administrative data, attendance, and learning outcomes, which feeds into the national EMIS. DBE reports 99.64% school adoption (excl. Western Cape), implying widespread including rural deployment with 81% connectivity. However, recent peer-reviewed studies on rural districts (iLembe, Eastern Cape) reveal predominant paper-based practices with some digital: discrepancies between manual records and digital entries persist, SA-SAMS underutilized due to unreliable internet/power, outdated hardware, insufficient training/support, and low digital literacy. Data often collected via hybrid methods, with batch uploads to provincial systems rather than real-time point-of-service entry. No evidence of majority rural facilities routinely using digital as default; challenges indicate paper default with digital for aggregate reporting. Key barriers: infrastructure deficits, training gaps, digital divide. National policy pushes SA-SAMS universally, but rural reality lags due to resource constraints, creating a gap where digital ambition exceeds implementation (198 words).
Source URLs: [DBE Annual Report 2024/25](https://www.gov.za/sites/default/files/gcis_document/202511/dbeannualreport20242025.pdf); [ERIC SA-SAMS Rural Review](https://files.eric.ed.gov/fulltext/EJ1467203.pdf); [SCIRP iLembe SA-SAMS Study](https://www.scirp.org/journal/paperinformation?paperid=137516)

Comments: Rural police stations in South Africa predominantly use manual paper-based docket systems for recording criminal cases and complaints, strained by rising crime rates.Electronic Docket Adoption in Rural Police Stations The national ICDMS (e-docket) system, rolled out to ~80% of stations by 2017 (including many rural), aims for electronic case management but faces poor implementation, low training (e.g., 72.7% detectives untrained in 2019), lost dockets, and no external linkages.MEC Albert Fritz on e-docket Recent 2024-2025 studies highlight ongoing barriers in rural areas like Amatole district (Eastern Cape): low computer literacy, poor infrastructure access, socio-economic challenges.Electronic Docket Adoption in Rural Police Stations Security issues persist even where e-dockets used (e.g., password-only access, manual backups).Keeping e-dockets secure in Limpopo No evidence majority of rural stations routinely use digital at point-of-service; adoption limited to pilots/challenges, with paper default. Key barriers: infrastructure, skills, connectivity in rural areas. National ambition (ICDMS) outpaces rural reality, where paper remains primary.SAPS Annual Report 2022/23
Source URLs: [Electronic Docket Adoption in Rural Police Stations](https://www.igi-global.com/article/electronic-docket-adoption-in-rural-police-stations/357268); [MEC Fritz e-docket assessment](https://www.gov.za/news/media-statements/mec-albert-fritz-e-docket-software-not-being-effectively-used-saps-and-courts); [e-dockets Limpopo security](https://journals.co.za/doi/10.17159/sacq.n74.19281)

Comments: Rural registry offices (Department of Home Affairs points) in South Africa primarily use paper-based forms (e.g., DHA-24) for birth registration at point of service, requiring in-person visits.South African Government Parents in remote areas face barriers like long travel distances, poverty, and inadequate infrastructure, leading to late registrations.Legalwise/CSO submission to OHCHR Digital initiatives include online birth registration at 391 health facilities (mostly urban/hospital-based), but only 8/91 targeted priority (rural/low-resource) facilities equipped due to connectivity and IT limitations.Centre for Child Law 2025 Mobile Registration Units with digital tools serve remote areas as special outreach, not standard.Legalwise/CSO DHA's 2025-2030 plan emphasizes national digital transformation (e.g., e-Birth, Digital ID), but current rural PoS remains predominantly paper with isolated digital pilots/outreach.DHA Strategic Plan Key barriers: poor rural connectivity, underfunding, reliance on physical offices. National policy aspires to full digital, but rural reality lags, with paper default.
Source URLs: [South African Government](https://www.gov.za/services/services-residents/birth/register-birth); [Centre for Child Law submissions](https://www.centreforchildlaw.co.za/dev/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/Centre-for-Child-Law-submissions-ACERWC-10.7.2025-final.pdf); [DHA Strategic Plan 2025-2030](https://www.dha.gov.za/images/AnnualReports/DHA-STRATEGIC-PLAN-25-30.pdf)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics (PHCUs/PHCCs) in South Sudan record patient data using paper registers and handwritten monthly summary reports, which are physically delivered to County Health Departments (CHDs). CHDs then transcribe these into DHIS2 for aggregate reporting. No evidence of electronic health records or digital entry at point-of-care in rural clinics; facilities lack infrastructure (electricity, internet, hardware). A 2020 pilot provided tablets for DHIS2 entry in 57 HIV facilities, but expansion stalled due to overburdened staff and infrastructure gaps. SMS used for weekly disease surveillance aggregates (not patient records) in some pilots, replacing paper delivery but sourced from paper forms. Recent 2023 study in Maridi County rural PHCCs confirms all use manual methods. Key barriers: conflict, poor roads, staff shortages, no connectivity, low capacity. National DHIS2 ambition exists at CHD/state levels, but rural point-of-service remains entirely paper-based (score 1), with digital limited to aggregation.
Source URLs: [World Bank Routine HMIS Brief](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/399871623734227498/pdf/Routine-Health-Information-Systems.pdf); [SAJIM Maridi Data Quality](https://sajim.co.za/index.php/sajim/article/view/1856/2925); [mHealth SMS Surveillance](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5344115/)

Comments: Rural primary schools in South Sudan predominantly use handwritten classroom registers and paper record books for attendance, enrollment, and administrative data, which are aggregated manually at state level for national EMIS reporting. Recent 2026 AU IPED assessment traces data flow from paper at school level, with field visits confirming reliance on paper tools even in Juba schools; rural areas face worse ICT deficits (no computers, internet, power). The 2023 National Education Census involved enumerators visiting schools for headcounts and data capture, indicating no routine digital POS systems. Pilots like SSSAMS (SMS-based attendance via phones, donor-funded GESS project 2013-2020, still partially used) represent isolated digital elements, but not standard at most rural facilities. National EMIS exists for aggregation, but rural point-of-service remains paper-based. Key barriers: conflict, inadequate infrastructure/connectivity esp rural, low capacity, donor dependency. Gap: Policy aims for EMIS strengthening/digital census tools, but rural reality is manual due to logistics/power shortages.
Source URLs: [AU IPED EMIS Report March 2026](https://au.int/en/pressreleases/20260324/au-iped-supports-south-sudan-strengthen-education-data-systems-through-emis); [MoGEI National Education Census Summary 2023](https://mogei.gov.ss/super/assets/documents/resources/2023%20Nat-Education%20Census%20Basic%20Summary%20Report_Final.pdf); [UNESCO EMIS Case Study South Sudan](https://unesdoc.unesco.org/ark:/48223/pf0000375815)

Comments: Rural police stations in South Sudan record criminal cases and complaints using entirely manual, paper-based systems such as handwritten dockets and pen-and-paper documentation, as evidenced by studies on stations like Malakia central police station (not rural but indicative) describing the system as "purely manual" and proposals for digitization planned initially for cities/towns. No evidence found of digital case management systems, computers, or IT infrastructure deployed at rural point-of-service facilities; searches across general web, academic papers, World Bank, UN/UNDP/USAID reports yielded only urban initiatives (e.g., Juba surveillance cameras, judiciary portals, personnel digitization from 2012). Key barriers include chronic conflict, limited resources, lack of electricity/infrastructure, illiteracy among officers, and weak national capacity, as noted in OSAC and Small Arms Survey reports on SSNPS challenges outside capitals. National policy focuses on reform and community policing but shows vast gap with rural reality, where customary justice dominates due to ineffective statutory policing; no pilots or donor projects indicate routine digital use at rural stations.
Source URLs: [Academia study on criminal records](https://www.academia.edu/103107583/CRIMINAL_RECORDS_INFORMATION_SYSTEM); [Small Arms Survey Policing in South Sudan](https://www.smallarmssurvey.org/sites/default/files/resources/HSBA-IB26-Policing-in-South-Sudan.pdf); [US State Dept 2020 Human Rights Report](https://www.state.gov/wp-content/uploads/2021/09/SOUTH-SUDAN-2020-HUMAN-RIGHTS-REPORT.pdf)

Comments: Rural registry offices in South Sudan rely entirely on paper-based recording for births and other vital events, using manual forms and registers like traditional birth certificates with handwritten serial numbers, dates, and names. No evidence found of digital systems at point-of-service in rural facilities; birth registration coverage remains very low (~1-10% estimated from anecdotes), with services inaccessible due to conflict, poor infrastructure, and understaffing. Recent developments include the 2018 Civil Registry Act and 2024 Regulations establishing legal framework, plus pilots for birth registration in Juba (urban) supported by Vital Strategies/UNHCR, but these are isolated and not scaled to rural areas. A costed CRVS plan exists, but implementation lags due to institutional weaknesses, electricity shortages (only ~5% national access), no internet in rural areas, ongoing violence, and funding gaps. National ambitions for CRVS strengthening contrast sharply with rural reality, where paper processes prevail and even notification is minimal; digitization scores <35% per 2017 UNECA assessment, with 0% death registration per 2025 WHO report. Score 1 conservative given lack of rural-specific digital evidence.
Source URLs: [UNECA CRVS Africa Report (2017)](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/report_on_the_status_of_civil_registration_and_vital_statistics_in_africa.pdf); [WHO South Sudan HIS Maturity (2025)](https://www.afro.who.int/sites/default/files/2025-03/Knowledge%20Management%20Series%20for%20Health%20Maturity%20of%20the%20Health%20Information%20System%20of%20South%20Sudan%20_%20progress,%20gaps%20and%20priorities%20for%20investment.pdf); [Statelessness Alliance Project (2025)](https://statelessnessalliance.org/online-marketplace/strengthening-civil-registration-and-ending-statelessness-in-south-sudan/)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Sudan predominantly use paper-based registers and forms for patient data recording at point of service, with 11-13 monthly reporting forms per facility burdening health workers where no statisticians are present. Aggregate data is manually compiled on paper/Excel at facilities, submitted to locality offices, and entered into national DHIS2 at state/locality levels (76% locality coverage, 64% facility reporting rate as of 2020, lower in rural/fragile states). No evidence of widespread EMR or DHIS2 use at rural clinics for individual patient records; digital systems limited to aggregate HMIS reporting upward.WHO EMRO HIS Assessment 2020 Recent pilots include mobile telemedicine (2025), IConnect telehealth with EMR support rolled out to 400 facilities (from pilot in 6), and a survey claiming 65% EMR adoption in unspecified rural facilities (potentially donor-linked).UNICEF digital innovations; Zenodo survey Key barriers: lack of electricity/internet/IT equipment in rural areas, staffing shortages, conflict/insecurity, parallel vertical paper systems.WHO EMRO HIS Assessment 2020 National ambition for integrated DHIS2 contrasts with rural paper reality, where pilots do not yet represent majority practice amid ongoing war disrupting services (70-80% facilities non-operational in some areas).
Source URLs: [WHO EMRO HIS Assessment 2020](https://applications.emro.who.int/docs/9789290229681-eng.pdf); [UNICEF SHARE digital health](https://sudan.un.org/en/301575-rebuilding-sudan%E2%80%99s-health-system-through-digital-innovations); [Zenodo EMR survey Sudan/Uganda](https://zenodo.org/records/18917536)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Sudan predominantly use paper-based systems for recording attendance, enrollment, and grades. EMIS operates at an 'emerging' stage per SABER, centrally coordinated with minimal school-level digital exchange; data lags 12-14 months, implying manual collection at facilities and later aggregation. Only ~50% primary schools have electricity, rare internet/computers in rural areas (66% population rural). UNESCO review notes outdated yearly books (paper-implied) and rapid paper surveys; no evidence of digital tools at rural school level. World Bank highlights EMIS strengthening needs, low ICT readiness. Pilots/digital aggregate reporting do not reach facility recording.
Source URLs: [UNESCO Sudan Education Policy Review](https://sudan.un.org/sites/default/files/2019-10/Sudan%20Education%20Policy%20Review%20UNESCO.pdf), [World Bank ICT in Sudan Education](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099306006222242031/pdf/P1752420c94e4c060080100d5e6fa9f82c8.pdf), [SABER ETI Sudan EMIS Analysis](https://sabapub.com/index.php/tts/article/download/642/329)"

Comments: No evidence found of digital case management, incident reporting, or record-keeping systems in Sudanese police stations, including rural ones. Extensive searches across US State Dept human rights reports, UK CPIN, UNODC, police websites (moi.gov.sd, sudanpolice.net), and queries on police IT systems yielded no mentions of digital tools for core police functions like case files or complaints. Police operations described involve manual detention procedures (e.g., 24-hour holds) and physical abuse for confessions, consistent with paper-based processes typical in low-resource African contexts. Ongoing conflict has disrupted infrastructure, but pre-conflict reports (e.g., Afrobarometer) highlight police unprofessionalism without digital references. Official sites show some e-services (vehicle lookup), but not operational records. Rural facilities likely rely entirely on paper, as no digitization pilots or systems noted.
Source URLs: [US State Dept 2023 Sudan HR Report](https://www.state.gov/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/528267_SUDAN-2023-HUMAN-RIGHTS-REPORT-1.pdf), [UK CPIN Sudan Security](https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/media/679b68026bb4c44f0805e7c4/SDN+CPIN+-+Security+situation.pdf), [Sudan Ministry of Interior](https://moi.gov.sd), [Sudan Police](https://www.sudanpolice.net), [Afrobarometer Sudan Police](https://www.afrobarometer.org/publication/ad984-amid-growing-insecurity-sudanese-see-lack-of-police-professionalism/)"

Comments: Rural registry offices in Sudan predominantly use paper-based processes for birth registration at point-of-service. Parents or health workers submit paper notices of birth (from hospitals or midwives for home births, common in rural areas) to local civil registry centers, where events are recorded manually before electronic transmission to regional and central databases. The 2011 Civil Registration Act and electronic civil registry enable national digital aggregation with biometrics and national ID numbers, but rural point-of-service remains paper-default, as evidenced by 2012 CRC4D report calling for ICT replacement of paper systems and 2023 Landinfo report noting lower coverage in rural/peripheral areas (<50% in Darfur vs. urban 89%). Mobile units aided mass registration including remote areas, but no evidence of routine digital tools (e.g., tablets, real-time entry) at most rural facilities; ECA report confirms electronic transmission from local but not full district-wide digital business processes. Recent plans like SudaPass (2025) indicate ambition but not rural implementation. Barriers: low density requiring 23k offices, conflict/insecurity (Darfur, Kordofan), poverty, distance, poor infrastructure, home births. National policy aims for digital population register, but rural reality lags with paper at facilities and aggregation higher up.
Source URLs: [CRC4D Promoting Birth Registration in Sudan (2012)](https://www.crc4d.com/downloads/2012-02-promoting-birth-registration-in-sudan.pdf); [Landinfo Sudan Civil Registration Report (2023)](https://landinfo.no/wp-content/uploads/2023/03/Report-Sudan-Civil-Registration-ID-documents-and-passports-03032023.pdf); [UN ECA Digitalization of CRVS in Africa](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/Digitalization%20of%20Civil%20Registration%20and%20Vital%20Statistics%20in%20Africa.pdf)

Comments: Rural clinics (dispensaries/health centres) in Tanzania predominantly use digital systems like GoTHoMIS for patient records at point of service, deployed in >3,000 PHC facilities including ~71% of dispensaries (5,545 total facilities, 75% coverage as of 2025).Kapsule Tech citing official stats; Ifakara Report Pilots have scaled nationally via HSSP V and Digital Health Strategy. However, parallel paper use persists due to infrastructure gaps (power/internet), training deficits, and workarounds in rural areas; studies show partial implementation and lower rural EMR data use.Emerald study on parallel use; Frontiers Dodoma rural low Barriers: poor connectivity, HR shortages, low digital literacy among rural staff.DHL study National policy aims full digital, but rural lags in full adoption/reliable use vs urban/higher levels, with DHIS2 mainly for aggregate reporting.DHIS2 site
Source URLs: [Ifakara Health Inst. Report](https://ihi.or.tz/media/List_and_report/MTR_Data_Management_-_REPORT_iGUVS7W.pdf); [Kapsule Tanzania Health System](https://kapsuletech.com/blog/tanzania-healthcare-system/); [PMC Digital Health Literacy](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12605890/); [Frontiers EMR Use](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/digital-health/articles/10.3389/fdgth.2023.1259268/full); [Emerald Parallel EMR](https://www.emerald.com/rmj/article/36/1/69/1307296/Transitioning-to-digital-systems-in-Tanzanian)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Tanzania predominantly use paper-based registers for recording administrative data, attendance, and learning outcomes, which are then aggregated manually into national EMIS systems at district or regional levels. Digital pilots exist, such as InfoTaaluma SMS/app in 253 schools (~4% of secondary schools, unclear primary coverage) and School Information System (SIS) with optional offline/mobile entry proposed in ESDP 2025/30, but these are isolated donor/NGO projects or emerging, not standard at most rural facilities. National EMIS/BEST relies on annual paper forms (TSA) from schools, with rural data gaps due to poor infrastructure, electricity/internet shortages, low digital literacy, and uneven ICT distribution.Tanzania ESDP 2025/30;World Bank SABER. Key barriers: lack of reliable power/internet in rural areas (high cost, sporadic access), inadequate devices/training, teacher workload. National ambitions (ESMIS prototype 2025, digital strategy) outpace rural reality, where paper remains default for point-of-service recording. Score 2 reflects predominant paper with limited digital pilots/aggregation.
Source URLs: [Tanzania ESDP 2025/30](https://www.moe.go.tz/sites/default/files/ESDP%202025-26%20hadi%202029-30%20Final-%2026%20February%202025.pdf); [World Bank Tanzania Schools Report](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/700811625119767503/pdf/Making-Great-Strides-Yet-a-Learning-Crisis-Remains-in-Tanzania-Results-of-the-SDI-and-SABER-service-Delivery-Surveys.pdf); [JICA EMIS Report](https://openjicareport.jica.go.jp/pdf/11798204_04.pdf)

Comments: Tanzania Police Force collects crime data at all stations (including rural), which is sent to a central Statistics Unit under the Director of Criminal Investigation for compilation and analysis into reports, indicating paper-based or aggregate reporting from rural stations rather than direct digital entry.Crime and Traffic Incidents Statistics Report Jan-Jun 2024 Online services exist for police clearance certificates (pcc.tpf.go.tz) and prosecutions case management (cmis.nps.go.tz), suggesting some digital aggregation at higher levels. Justice sector shows digital disparities in rural areas, with infrastructure gaps limiting e-systems at police/prison levels.Digitalization And Access To Adjudication In Tanzania No evidence of routine digital case/incident recording at rural police stations; central forensics handles records via fingerprints. Predominantly paper with some digital (central/pilots).
Source URLs: [Crime Statistics NBS](https://www.nbs.go.tz/uploads/statistics/documents/en-1755675635-Crime%20and%20Traffic%20Incidents%20Statistics%20January%20-%20June,%202024.pdf), [Digitalization Justice Assessment](https://rjpn.org/ijcspub/papers/IJCSP25C1124.pdf), [Tanzania Police Official Site](https://polisi.go.tz)

Comments: In rural Tanzania, birth registration at registry points (Ward Executive Offices and rural health facilities) is predominantly paper-based: registrars complete handwritten forms and issue hand-written certificates on-site.APAI-CRVS Snapshot;Decentralization Study. Digital elements include mobile phone uploads of basic data to RITA's central database post-registration and scanning forms for validation, but initial recording remains manual.APAI-CRVS Snapshot. National digital CRVS launched in 2022 has digitized over 3M records, with eRITA for online applications/certificates, but rural point-of-service unchanged per recent reports.Daily News. Barriers: poor infrastructure, low digital literacy, connectivity issues, reliance on partners for funding/ICT.UNECA Digitalization Report. Coverage ~28-38% (2017-18); decentralization improved access but not to majority digital POS. Gap: National ambitions (eRITA, digital infrastructure) vs rural paper default with aggregate digital reporting (score 2).
Source URLs: [APAI-CRVS Tanzania Snapshot](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/CRVS_TanzaniaMainlandSnapshot_e.pdf); [Decentralization of Birth Registration Study](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7595116/); [UNECA CRVS Digitalization in Africa](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/Digitalization%20of%20Civil%20Registration%20and%20Vital%20Statistics%20in%20Africa.pdf)"

Comments: Rural primary clinics in Togo predominantly use paper-based patient registers and records at point of care. Aggregated weekly surveillance data (e.g., cases/deaths for 15 diseases) is tallied from facility registries onto paper forms, then sent via SMS using the Argus app on dedicated phones for upward reporting—not patient-level digital entry Argus study. No evidence of routine EMR or DHIS2 at rural clinics; only 11.8% of surveyed doctors nationwide use EMR, reflecting low digitization Doctors EMR survey. Pilots exist: Integrate Health ICBHSS in 21 northern rural facilities focuses on clinical mentoring/supply chains without digital records ICBHSS protocol; DokitaEyes app (230 facilities) for digital patient files, but private/NGO-led, offline-capable, not standard public rural practice; recent World Bank $100M digital project connects facilities to broadband (2024), but focuses on connectivity/skills, not POC systems. National DHIS2 used for aggregate HMIS/IDSR, not facility-level recording. Barriers: poor internet/power in rural areas, low EMR adoption, reliance on paper transmission historically. Policy (e.g., National Digital Health Center) aims at digital transformation, but rural POC remains paper-based, with digital limited to pilots/aggregation. (198 words)
Source URLs: [Argus Togo study](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7707507/); [Togolese doctors EMR survey](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12817914/); [ICBHSS rural Togo](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6796416/); [World Bank Togo digital](https://www.worldbank.org/en/news/press-release/2024/12/19/togo-100-million-in-funding-to-accelerate-digital-transformation)

Comments: Togo's rural primary schools use a hybrid paper-digital system for data collection (attendance, enrollment, grades). Data from 16,000 schools, including rural primaries, is first collected on paper forms at schools, then transmitted to district education inspections for review and subsequent digitization into StatEduc. Digital processing/analysis occurs via DHIS2 EMIS (piloted nationally since 2021 with GPE KIX/Norad support), but initial recording remains paper-based at facility level. No evidence of on-site digital recording in rural schools; digitization is at district level. This aligns with francophone African norms and recent reports (2022-2025). Score 1 as rural facilities record on paper.
Source URLs: [DHIS2 Togo EMIS Report](https://education.dhis2.org/fr/emis-togo-2022/), [GPE KIX Togo Blog](https://www.gpekix.org/blog/togo-empowering-schools-and-districts-data), [DHIS2 Implementation Togo](https://education.dhis2.org/implementation/togo/)"

Comments: Togo's rural policing is handled by Gendarmerie Nationale posts. The Criminal Procedure Code mandates officers (incl. rural gendarmerie) to draft and sign procès-verbaux (PVs) on-site for incidents, with physical custody registers requiring signatures—standard paper-based francophone practice [Togo Code de Procédure Pénale]. UK FCDO arrest guidance describes signed station registers and "police records" for inquiries, implying manual processes [UK Prisoner Pack]. Central WAPIS (2022) enables national data sharing but no evidence of initial rural recording being digital; likely paper first, digitized centrally (scores 1 per rules) [INTERPOL WAPIS]. No rural digital tools on official sites. Score 1: entirely paper-based at rural facilities.
Source URLs: [Togo Code de Procédure Pénale](https://www.vertic.org/media/National%20Legislation/Togo/TG_Code_Procedure_Penale.pdf), [UK FCDO Togo Prisoner Pack](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/togo-prisoner-pack/information-pack-for-british-nationals-arrested-or-detained-in-togo), [INTERPOL WAPIS Programme Togo](https://www.interpol.int/en/How-we-work/Capacity-building/Capacity-building-projects/WAPIS-Programme)

Comments: Togo's rural registry offices (état civil at prefectures/town halls) predominantly use paper-based recording. Births are declared at local Etat Civil with hospital attestation, recorded in multi-leaflet paper registers; leaflet given as Acte de Naissance. No electronic CRVS database; paper used throughout for transfers to central levels per ECA-APAI report. Low completeness (e.g., 22-30% late registrations via courts). Recent pilots (Dec 2025) allow online requests for copies in 5 communes (some rural like Avé 2), implying scanned paper records for digital access, but core recording at rural facilities remains paper/manual. Pilots indicate emerging digital services, but not at recording stage. Score 2: predominantly paper with some digital pilots.
Source URLs: [US State Dept Togo Civil Docs](https://travel.state.gov/content/travel/en/us-visas/Visa-Reciprocity-and-Civil-Documents-by-Country/Togo.html), [ECA Digitalization CRVS Africa](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/Digitalization%20of%20Civil%20Registration%20and%20Vital%20Statistics%20in%20Africa.pdf), [Togo Govt Digitalisation Pilot](https://territoire.gouv.tg/digitalisation-de-letat-civil-le-togo-lance-officiellement-la-demande-en-ligne-des-copies-dactes/)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Tunisia predominantly use paper-based recording for patient data, including nursing documentation, which remains largely manual across public facilities despite national eHealth initiatives. No evidence found of routine EMR or DHIS2 use at point-of-care in rural clinics; WHO data confirms no EHR in primary care facilities (clinics/health centers). Recent digital efforts focus on urban/regional hospitals (e.g., full digitalization target by 2026), telemedicine pilots, connectivity expansion to primary facilities, and national systems like "One Health," but these represent emerging donor/government projects not yet standard at rural point-of-service. A 2024 study notes paper-based nursing records cause traceability issues, with EMR gaining momentum but not implemented routinely. Barriers include poor internet in rural areas, staff training gaps, fragmented HIS, and focus on aggregation over facility-level digitization. National ambitions (e.g., 2025 digital hospital projects, World Bank support for EMR training) outpace rural reality, where paper registers prevail for patient encounters, with data likely transcribed later at district levels.
Source URLs: [WHO Tunisia eHealth Profile](https://www.emro.who.int/images/stories/ehealth/documents/tunisia-ehealth-country-profile.pdf); [Libyan Journal of Medicine: ENR Impact Study](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11583327/); [World Bank Health System Strengthening](https://www.worldbank.org/en/news/press-release/2025/05/28/new-project-to-boost-tunisia-s-health-system-and-pandemic-response)"

Comments: Tunisia has implemented national digital platforms like Tarbia.tn (launched under World Bank-supported PREFAT project targeting lagging/rural regions), enabling parents to track real-time attendance, assignments, and progress reports, with teachers managing classes digitally [We Are Tech Africa, World Bank PREFAT]. Equipped 1,600+ computer labs in primary schools, including rural areas, for digital tools. However, no direct evidence of entirely digital recording at rural primary school level for attendance, enrollment, grades; likely paper-based at facilities with aggregation/digital entry at higher levels, given EMIS-like systems common in region but pilots/donor-focused here. Rural infrastructure challenges persist (e.g., poor conditions per reports). Predominantly paper with significant digital pilots/central reporting.
Source URLs: [We Are Tech Africa](https://www.wearetech.africa/en/fils-uk/news/public-management/tunisia-unveils-digital-platform-to-boost-education-transparency-equity), [World Bank PREFAT](https://www.worldbank.org/en/news/feature/2026/01/29/transforming-early-education-in-tunisia-prefat-s-impact-on-access-quality-and-innovation), [Tarbia.tn](https://www.tarbia.tn/ar)

Comments: No evidence found of digital case management systems at rural police stations or gendarmerie posts in Tunisia. Extensive searches reveal digital efforts in cybercrime investigation, forensics training (UNODC), and justice sector digitization (EU/World Bank for courts), but police procedures rely on traditional paper-based processes typical of francophone Africa: handwritten procès-verbaux (PVs) for incidents/complaints, occurrence books ("main courante"), and manual case files. Criminal records (B3) originate from police/gendarmerie stations and involve paper stamps/forms, even if online request portals exist for clean extracts. Rural areas (87% territory covered by National Guard/gendarmerie) lack specific digital pilots; aggregate/central systems absent. US State Dept reports note requirement for "complete record" of arrests/notifications, consistent with paper logs. Score 1: entirely paper-based at facility level.
Source URLs: [Ministry of Interior](https://www.interieur.gov.tn), [UNODC Tunisia Crime Scene](https://www.unodc.org/romena/en/Stories/2019/May/tunisia_-strengthening-crime-scene-investigation-and-forensic-chain-of-custody.html), [EU Justice Digitisation](https://www.eumedbridge.eu/news/tunisia-eu-supports-digitisation-justice-system-part-support-programme/)"

Comments: Rural registry offices in Tunisia (municipal civil status bureaus, including subsidiary offices, annexes, and mobile units covering rural areas) primarily record births and other civil events by hand in duplicate paper registers, which hold legal probative force. Events are then manually transcribed into the national Madania digital system by registrars for centralized database access and certificate issuance anywhere.Centre of Excellence for CRVS Systems Digitization began in major municipalities in the 1980s, with full national rollout by 2005, interconnecting all 350+ principal and subsidiary offices.Centre of Excellence for CRVS Systems The UNFPA notes the CRVS is only partially digitized, limited to births/deaths (excluding marriages/divorces).UNFPA Tunisia No evidence found of direct digital entry at point-of-service replacing paper as default in rural facilities; paper remains standard with digital for aggregation/reporting. Recent pilots (e.g., online certificate issuance 2022) enhance access but not recording. Barriers: Legal reliance on paper, infrastructure in remote areas. National ambition (Tunisie Digitale 2025) outpaces rural reality, where paper dominates despite equipment claims.
Source URLs: [Centre of Excellence for CRVS Systems report](https://crvssystems.ca/sites/default/files/assets/files/SocialProtection_5_Tunisia_e_WEB.pdf); [UNFPA Knowledge Brief on Birth Registration](https://tunisia.unfpa.org/sites/default/files/pub-pdf/knowledge_brief_on_birth_registration_in_tunisia.pdf); [UNStats Tunisian CRVS presentation](https://unstats.un.org/unsd/demographic-social/meetings/2017/new-york--egm-on-management-and-evaluation-of-crvs-systems/Session19-Tunisia.pdf)

Comments: Rural primary clinics (HC II/III) in Uganda predominantly use paper-based registers for patient data recording at point of care, including outpatient/inpatient registers, lab forms, and HMIS tools. Aggregate data from these paper forms is later transcribed into DHIS2 at district level for reporting, leading to errors and delays. Digital systems like EMRs (e.g., UgandaEMR) and DHIS2 are available in higher facilities (HCIV+) but mostly program-specific (HIV), with hybrid paper-digital dominant (94%+ HCIVs); pure digital rare (1-2%). Pilots like Stre@mline exist in select rural hospitals but not scaled nationally. Barriers include unreliable electricity/internet in rural areas (~12% stable connectivity), staff shortages, high turnover, poor ICT infrastructure, and donor-driven silos. National policies (2023-2025 Digital Health Strategy) aim for point-of-care digital by 2025/26, but rural reality lags, with paper default and digital for upward reporting only.
Source URLs: [PMC policy analysis](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12816509/); [BMC EMR study](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12512544/); [MIT info streams](https://direct.mit.edu/dint/article/4/4/882/112748/Information-Streams-in-Health-Facilities-The-Case)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Uganda predominantly use paper-based registers for recording administrative data, attendance, and learning outcomes at the point of service. Head teachers fill extensive paper forms for the Annual School Census (ASC), which are submitted to District Education Offices (DEOs) and manually entered into national EMIS systems.UNESCO EMIS Uganda Case Study%20and%20Data%20for%20Increased%20Resilience%20to%20Crisis_%20country%20case%20study_%20Uganda.pdf) EMIS data collection stalled post-2017 due to poor coverage and quality from paper processes. Digital pilots exist, such as DHIS2-DEMIS (termly paper tools digitized at district level in 10/175 sites since 2019, including rural Gulu/Entebbe) and SMS attendance (eduTrac in 37 districts, SESIL in rural West Nile).DHIS2 Uganda No evidence of majority rural schools using digital tools routinely at point-of-service; daily attendance remains manual, aggregated on paper. Key barriers: lack of electricity (13% rural households connected), computers/internet, trained staff, poor roads limiting verification; rural-urban digital divide stark.Afrobarometer National EMIS ambitions contrast with rural paper reality, reliant on donor pilots not scaled nationwide.
Source URLs: [UNESCO EMIS Case Study](https://www.educationcannotwait.org/sites/default/files/2022-03/Strengthening%20Education%20Management%20Information%20Systems%20(EMIS)%20and%20Data%20for%20Increased%20Resilience%20to%20Crisis_%20country%20case%20study_%20Uganda.pdf); [DHIS2 Routine Data Uganda](https://education.dhis2.org/routine-data-collection-uganda/); [Afrobarometer Barriers](https://www.afrobarometer.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/ad515-barriers_to_e-learning_in_uganda-afrobarometer_dispatch-10april22.pdf)

Comments: Rural police stations in Uganda predominantly use paper-based recording for case files, complaints, and incidents. Standard practice involves manual entry in hard-backed occurrence books as the foundation document for all reports, with no evidence of digital replacement at station level, especially rural ones lacking infrastructure (no reliable electricity, internet, or computers). A 2017 Crime Records Management System (CRMS) was developed for CID case tracking, piloted only in 10 Kampala divisions—not rural or station-level. Judiciary is digitizing (ECCMIS), but police stations remain manual per UK DFID 1998 evaluation (still relevant as no rural rollout found). US State Dept HR reports describe investigations without noting digital tools at stations. UNODC notes digital in prosecution/judiciary, not police. Annual crime reports imply aggregate data entry post-paper collection. Score 1: entirely paper-based at rural facilities.
Source URLs: [UPF CRMS](https://upf.go.ug/police-develops-crime-records-management-system/), [UK DFID Police Project Eval](https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/media/5a78d60ced915d07d35b2cde/ev591.pdf), [US State Dept 2023 HR Report](https://www.state.gov/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/528267_UGANDA-2023-HUMAN-RIGHTS-REPORT.pdf)"

Comments: Rural registry offices (86% of 152 total birth/death registration offices at district level) conduct birth registration using paper-based processes at the point of service. Data is later entered digitally at regional levels and transferred via CDs/flash drives to national database. All local offices have computers, but registration remains manual/paper. Mobile Vital Record System (piloted 2013 with UNICEF) enables birth notifications from rural areas via mobile to central database, but full registration still requires paper at district registries. Low completeness (~32% children under 5 registered, 2016). Barriers: distance (>10km to offices, up to 4hr travel), low awareness, costs for certificates. National digital ID push (2025-26 MOSIP migration 28M+ records) focuses on IDs, not CRVS point-of-service in rural registries. Pilots like mobile notification indicate some digital elements, but default is paper at rural facilities. Gap: National ambitions for integrated digital ID/CRVS exist, but rural point-of-registration lags due to infrastructure/connectivity issues. (178 words)
Source URLs: [UNECA CRVS Uganda Snapshot 2019](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/CRVS_UgandaSnapshot_e.pdf); [UBoS Vital Statistics Report 2024](https://www.ubos.org/wp-content/uploads/publications/Uganda-Vital-Statistics-Report-2024.pdf); [World Bank Rural Birth Reg Data](https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/SP.REG.BRTH.RU.ZS?locations=UG)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Zambia predominantly use paper-based registers and tally sheets for patient data recording at the point of service. Data is aggregated monthly on paper reports and submitted to district offices for entry into DHIS2, the national HMIS platform.Health Policy and Planning 2023 Patient details are often recorded twice: in paper health passports and facility registers.Zambia HIS Assessment 2007 Digital systems like SmartCare (EHR) and DHIS2 exist nationally, with SmartCare Pro rolled out to 877 facilities (of 3,540 total) as of 2025, but most rural clinics lack computers, reliable power, and internet, remaining paper-based.Global Fund Zambia Digital Health 2025 Pilots like CHIP for community health workers (2025) and limited SmartCare in select rural antenatal sites indicate some digital adoption, but not widespread at clinics.PATH article Key barriers include frequent power outages (hydro-dependent grid), poor rural connectivity, staff shortages, overwhelming registers (21+ per small facility), and flooding/transport issues delaying submissions. National ambitions (Digital Health Strategy 2022-2026, SmartCare expansion) outpace rural reality, where paper persists despite aggregation-level digitalization. Score 2: digital limited to pilots/district entry, not routine point-of-service.
Source URLs: [Health Policy and Planning 2023](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10566315/); [Zambia HIS Assessment 2007](https://static1.squarespace.com/static/58d002f017bffcf99fe21889/t/5a7cb066085229f6f85203eb/1518121063297/ZambiaHISAssessmentReport.pdf); [Global Fund Zambia Digital Health Case Study Apr 2025](https://www.theglobalfund.org/media/w4gdfjob/publication_zambia-digital-health_casestudy_en.pdf)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Zambia predominantly use paper-based registers for daily administrative data like attendance and learning outcomes, as evidenced by biometric pilots replacing inaccurate paper systems and descriptions of annual census data as outdated. The Ministry of Education's EMIS relies on annual school census data collected via CS Pro 7.7 tool deployed electronically to schools, indicating some digital entry at school level during census periods, but not routine point-of-service use. Pilots like Akros DHIS2 in Namwala district and Simprints biometrics in 8 rural eastern schools introduce digital tracking for attendance and aggregates, supported by UNICEF/World Bank projects aiming to strengthen EMIS, but these are isolated/district-level, not majority coverage. Barriers include poor rural infrastructure (electricity, internet, devices), low teacher digital capacity, and reliance on annual aggregates over real-time data. National policy pushes digital migration (e.g., 2024 ESB describes shift from paper), but rural reality lags with paper default and digital limited to pilots/periodic reporting.Ministry of Education Zambia 2024 ESB; Simprints rural Zambia schools case study; Akros Zambia EMIS pilot
Source URLs: [Zambia Ministry of Education 2024 Education Statistics Bulletin](https://www.edu.gov.zm/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/Education-Statistics-Bulletin-2024.pdf); [Simprints Zambia rural schools biometrics](https://www.simprints.com/simprints-drives-improvement-in-zambian-schools/); [Akros DHIS2 EMIS Namwala](https://docs.dhis2.org/en/topics/user-stories/zambia-education-management-system-emis.html)

Comments: Rural police stations in Zambia predominantly use entirely paper-based systems for recording criminal cases and complaints. Baseline studies (2020) show 85.2% of police stations map crimes using physical maps and pins, with all stations processing crime reports manually into occurrence books, registers, and dockets; no digital entry at point of service. Paper forms are filled for complaints, witness statements, case dockets, police bonds, etc. Recent efforts (2026) by SMART Zambia to equip 40 stations with networking gear for centralized digital systems indicate ongoing pilots or upgrades, but these are nascent and not yet standard. Challenges include poor rural ICT infrastructure, low officer computer skills (24% trained), electricity/power issues, transport shortages, and manual data aggregation at higher levels. National ambitions for e-policing (proposed systems like cloud crime mapping, ZPS platform) and digital transformation (GMIS, e-learning) exist, but rural point-of-care remains paper-reliant, with data likely transcribed later at district levels. No evidence majority of rural stations use digital routinely; isolated national-level tools (AFIS fingerprints) do not extend to case recording. Score 1 reflects entirely paper at rural facilities despite policy pushes.
Source URLs:
[Crime Mapping Model Zambia Police](https://thesai.org/Downloads/Volume11No1/Paper_32-Crime_Mapping_Model_based_on_Cloud_and_Spatial_Data.pdf); [Secure Automation Zambia Police](https://www.ijirset.com/upload/2015/december/141_Secure_F.pdf); [E-Policing Zambia](http://article.sapub.org/10.5923.j.scit.20160602.03.html)"

Comments: Rural registry offices in Zambia primarily use paper-based systems for birth registration, with manual records leading to low coverage (9% rural births registered per 2007 ZDHS; overall <15% as of 2024 per UNICEF).WHO Zambia CRVS Plan 2015, [BiometricUpdate 2025] Digital efforts include the national INRIS platform (launched ~2022, scaled to 40+ districts), decentralization of certification to district levels (SI 44/2016), mobile registration units with digital tools for remote areas (UNICEF/World Vision), and plans for full digitization/integration with health systems (CRVS Policy 2022).[OHCHR subm.], [UNECA digital CRVS] However, no evidence shows majority rural facilities routinely use digital at point-of-service; rural lags urban in performance, with infrastructure gaps (electricity, internet, staffing), low awareness, distance, poverty as barriers.[WHO Plan], [OHCHR] National ambitions (50% registration by 2026, digital ID 2027) outpace rural reality, where paper remains default and digital is limited to pilots/outreach, not standard POS.
Source URLs: [Zambia National CRVS Strategic Plan (WHO)](https://platform.who.int/docs/default-source/mca-documents/policy-documents/plan-strategy/ZMB-CC-55-01-PLAN-STRATEGY-2015-eng-National-Strategic-Plan-of-Action-CRVS.pdf); [OHCHR Zambia Birth Reg Submission](https://www.ohchr.org/sites/default/files/documents/issues/digitalage/cfis/birth-registration/subm-universal-birth-registration-sta-zambia.pdf); [UNECA Digitalization of CRVS in Africa](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/Digitalization%20of%20Civil%20Registration%20and%20Vital%20Statistics%20in%20Africa.pdf)

Comments: Rural primary healthcare clinics in Zimbabwe traditionally rely on paper registers for patient data recording, with aggregate data later entered into national DHIS2 at district levels. The Impilo EMR system, developed since 2015, is now deployed in 1,254 of ~1,900 public health facilities (66%), explicitly targeting rural 'last mile' sites like Sipepa Rural Hospital through solar power (1,044 facilities, 60% coverage) and internet upgrades to enable digital point-of-care recording. Provincial data (Matabeleland South, Q1 2025) shows 72% fully functional among 153 facilities, though rural districts lag. Digital systems are the emerging standard at most facilities, reducing paper burden and improving patient management, but some paper persists in the remaining ~34% uncovered sites and where infrastructure fails. Key barriers include frequent power outages (costing 6% GDP), poor rural internet/connectivity, training gaps, and staff shortages. National eHealth Strategy (2021-2025) bridges policy ambition with rollout, though rural implementation trails urban areas; majority now predominantly digital with residual paper processes.
Source URLs: [Global Fund Zimbabwe Digital Health Case Study Aug 2025](https://www.theglobalfund.org/media/qksld2tg/publication_zimbabwe-digital-health_casestudy_en.pdf); [Impilo EHR Evaluation Matabeleland South Q1 2025](https://rsisinternational.org/journals/ijrsi/uploads/vol12-iss15-pg2476-2481-202511_pdf.pdf); [WHO eHealth Strategy Zimbabwe 2012-17](https://cdn.who.int/media/docs/default-source/digital-health-documents/global-observatory-on-digital-health/zwe_ehealth.pdf)

Comments: Rural primary schools in Zimbabwe primarily use paper-based registers for recording administrative data, attendance, and learning outcomes. Data is collected via annual school census forms filled manually at schools and aggregated upward to the national EMIS, often leading to delays, gaps, and inaccuracies due to manual transcription at district levels. Most rural schools lack digital devices, internet connectivity, and power, preventing real-time digital entry into EMIS; teachers in areas like Mutoko report no access to digital tools for data contribution.Kuyenda Collective EMIS exists nationally, with high continental compliance scores and plans for decentralization, but rural point-of-service remains analog.UNESCO IICBA Pilots like school management software or e-learning platforms (e.g., Ruzivo) target urban/private schools or supplementary use, not routine admin recording in rural public primaries. Key barriers: poor infrastructure (no electricity/internet), teacher ICT skills gaps, funding shortages, high attrition. National policies (e.g., Education 5.0) aspire to digital inclusion, but rural reality lags far behind, with data digitized only post-collection at higher levels. (148 words)
Source URLs: [Kuyenda Collective](https://www.kuyendacollective.org/article/investing-in-education-management-information-systems-emis/); [UNESCO IICBA Brief](https://www.iicba.unesco.org/en/zimbabwe); [Taylor & Francis Satellite Schools](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/2331186X.2025.2521164)

Comments: Rural police stations in Zimbabwe primarily use manual paper-based systems for recording criminal cases and complaints, relying on physical dockets prone to loss, tampering, and bribery. Academic studies (e.g., 2019 GIS crime mapping in rural Chivi district) describe traditional crime registers and dockets, with data collected via manual processes and GPS for analysis, indicating no routine digital entry at point of service. ZRP has centralized digital databases (e.g., Criminal Records Office), but rural stations forward paper dockets for processing. Plans for an electronic docket system ("soon" transition announced Dec 2025) and internet provision to rural stations (pledged 2021, per Horizon 2025) remain unimplemented, with no evidence of rural rollout or pilots shifting the norm. Key barriers: poor internet/electricity in rural areas, low device access, resource constraints, as noted in World Bank digital economy report and POTRAZ analyses. National ambitions (e.g., smart policing) contrast sharply with rural paper reality, where digital tools like DMR radios exist for comms but not case recording. Score 1 reflects entirely paper-based point-of-service recording.
Source URLs: [ZRP Official Site](https://zrp.gov.zw/?p=7347); [Newsday on Rural Internet Pledge](https://www.newsday.co.zw/local-news/article/18294/govt-pledges-to-provide-internet-services-to-rural-police-officers); [GIS Crime Mapping Study](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/23311886.2019.1661606); [World Bank Digital Economy Report](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/982981621880260112/pdf/Digital-Economy-for-Zimbabwe-Country-Diagnostic-Report.pdf)

Comments: Rural registry offices in Zimbabwe rely entirely on paper-based processes for birth registration, requiring parents to present physical documents such as national ID cards, birth confirmation records (from hospitals/clinics), marriage certificates, baptismal certificates, witness IDs, or letters from village heads/farmers for home/farm births. No evidence found of electronic recording at the point of service in rural facilities; processes emphasize manual declarations and forms. Registration completeness is low in rural areas (~32% in 2015 vs higher urban), with rural residence a key predictor of non-registration alongside poverty, home births, and low maternal education Comparative Population Studies. Nationally, moderate digitization (50-64%) exists for aggregation, but rural point-of-service remains paper due to infrastructure gaps, limited staffing, and indirect costs like transport to district offices UNECA CRVS Report. Recent national digital initiatives (e.g., ZPR integration with health for automated notifications, planned 2023) focus on central systems, starting urban, with mobile apps piloted but no rural rollout confirmed; rural lags persist Biometric Update. Barriers: poor connectivity, economic crisis, under-resourced offices. Gap: National ambitions for e-CRVS contrast with rural paper reality, where data likely transcribed later at district level.
Source URLs: [Comparative Population Studies (2023)](https://comparativepopulationstudies.de/index.php/CPoS/article/view/473); [UNECA CRVS Report (2016)](https://apai-crvs.uneca.org/sites/default/files/resourcefiles/report_on_the_status_of_civil_registration_and_vital_statistics_in_africa.pdf); [Zimparis Gov Process](http://www.zimparis.gov.zw/index.php/consular/registration/birth-death-and-marriage-certificate), [Biometric Update (2023)](https://www.biometricupdate.com/202304/integrated-digital-system-in-zimbabwe-to-enhance-id-issuance-birth-registration)
